docs.text: Before we discuss itemized deductions, a critical clarification: not all deductions require you to itemize. Some are called above-the-line deductions, and they reduce your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) regardless of whether you take the standard deduction or itemize. These are the ones to know first.

docs.title: Above-the-Line Deductions: Available to Everyone
docs.text:
Above-the-line deductions (formally called "adjustments to income") appear on Schedule 1 of your Form 1040. Because they reduce your AGI, they have a compounding effect — a lower AGI unlocks eligibility for other credits and deductions that phase out based on income. The most commonly valuable above-the-line deductions for 2025 include:

  • Traditional IRA contributions (if eligible for the deduction based on income and workplace plan rules)
  • HSA contributions not made through payroll
  • Student loan interest — up to $2,500 per year, phases out at higher incomes
  • Self-employment tax deduction — self-employed individuals can deduct the employer-equivalent portion (half) of their self-employment tax
  • Self-employed health insurance premiums — 100% deductible for self-employed people not eligible for an employer plan
  • SEP-IRA, SIMPLE IRA, or Solo 401(k) contributions — for self-employed individuals, limits are substantially higher than traditional IRAs
  • Alimony paid under pre-2019 divorce agreements — still deductible for agreements finalized before January 1, 2019
  • Educator expenses — teachers can deduct up to $300 for out-of-pocket classroom supplies

The self-employment deductions deserve special emphasis. If you have freelance income, gig income, or run a side business, the ability to deduct half your self-employment tax, your health insurance premiums, and contributions to a Solo 401(k) or SEP-IRA can dramatically reduce your taxable income — often by more than any combination of itemized deductions.

docs.title: Mortgage Interest and SALT
docs.text:
If you own a home and pay a mortgage, the Mortgage Interest Deduction allows you to deduct the interest portion of your mortgage payments as an itemized deduction on Schedule A. For mortgages taken out after December 15, 2017, you can deduct interest on up to **\(750,000 of mortgage principal** (\)375,000 if married filing separately). For older mortgages originated before that date, the limit is $1,000,000. The deduction covers your primary home and one additional qualified residence (a vacation home, for example).

In practice, the value of the mortgage interest deduction depends entirely on whether your total itemized deductions exceed the standard deduction — which in 2025 is $15,000 for single filers and $30,000 for married filing jointly. If your only significant itemized deduction is mortgage interest, many homeowners will find they are better off taking the standard deduction, especially in the early-to-mid years of a mortgage when interest payments are highest but may still not exceed the standard deduction threshold.

The SALT Deduction Cap refers to the State and Local Tax deduction. You can deduct the state and local taxes you paid — including state income taxes (or sales taxes if you choose that option) and property taxes — up to a combined cap of $10,000 per year for both single filers and married filing jointly. This cap, established by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, is a significant limitation for taxpayers in high-tax states like California, New York, and New Jersey, where property taxes alone can exceed $10,000. The $10,000 ceiling is the same for both single and married filers, which creates a "marriage penalty" for high-tax-state couples. As of this writing, the SALT cap remains in place for 2025, though it has been an ongoing topic of legislative debate.

The $10,000 SALT Ceiling Is a Real Constraint

If you live in a state with high income or property taxes, the SALT cap may already consume your full $10,000 deduction allowance, leaving your mortgage interest as the only remaining itemized deduction to add. Run the numbers: if mortgage interest + $10,000 SALT does not exceed the standard deduction, you are usually better off taking the standard deduction.

docs.title: Medical Expenses: The High Bar
docs.text:
Medical expenses are deductible on Schedule A, but the Medical Expense Threshold sets a high bar: you can only deduct the portion of your unreimbursed medical expenses that exceeds 7.5% of your AGI. If your AGI is $60,000, the threshold is $4,500. Only medical costs above that $4,500 floor are deductible. For most healthy adults in average years, this threshold is never reached. But in years with significant medical events — surgery, a serious diagnosis, dental work, long-term care — the deduction can become quite meaningful.

Eligible expenses are broader than most people realize. They include health insurance premiums you paid out of pocket (not through an employer plan), prescription drugs, dental and vision care, hearing aids, wheelchairs and mobility equipment, mental health therapy, and medically necessary home modifications. Costs reimbursed by insurance or your HSA/FSA are not deductible. If you have a year with unusually high medical costs, consider "bunching" elective procedures into the same calendar year to exceed the 7.5% threshold and maximize your deduction.

docs.title: Charitable Giving Strategies
docs.text:
The Charitable Giving Deduction allows you to deduct donations to qualified 501(c)(3) organizations when you itemize on Schedule A. Cash donations are deductible up to 60% of your AGI. Donations of appreciated property — like stock you have held for more than a year — can be deducted at fair market value, which means you avoid the capital gains tax you would owe if you sold the stock first. Donating appreciated stock to charity is consistently one of the most tax-efficient charitable giving strategies available.

For taxpayers who take the standard deduction and would not benefit from itemizing even with charitable gifts added in, a strategy called "bunching" is worth considering. Instead of donating $2,000 per year for five years, you donate $10,000 in one year — exceeding the standard deduction threshold — then take the standard deduction in other years. Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) are the tool that makes bunching practical: you contribute a lump sum to the DAF, take the full deduction in that year, and then distribute grants to your chosen charities over multiple years at your own pace.

For taxpayers who are 70.5 or older, there is an extraordinary strategy called the Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD). A QCD allows you to transfer up to $105,000 per year (2025 limit, indexed for inflation) directly from your IRA to a qualified charity. That transfer counts toward your Required Minimum Distribution (RMD) for the year — but it is never included in your taxable income. This is better than taking the RMD as income and then donating it, because even if you itemize, income included in AGI can trigger phase-outs of other deductions and credits. The QCD removes the income from AGI entirely. For retirees who are charitably inclined, this is one of the most powerful strategies in the tax code.

docs.title: Part 3: Credits for Specific Situations
docs.text: Tax credits are worth more than deductions because they directly reduce your tax bill rather than reducing the income that gets taxed. A $1,000 deduction saves you $220 if you are in the 22% bracket. A $1,000 tax credit saves you exactly $1,000, regardless of your bracket.

docs.title: Child and Dependent Care Credit
docs.text:
The Child and Dependent Care Credit helps offset the cost of childcare or adult dependent care that allows you to work (or look for work). Eligible expenses include daycare, nursery school, after-school programs, summer day camps (but not overnight camps), and care for a qualifying adult dependent who is physically or mentally incapable of self-care.

The credit is worth between 20% and 35% of your qualifying care expenses, depending on your income — lower incomes receive the higher percentage. The expense cap is \(3,000 for one qualifying person** and **\)6,000 for two or more. At the maximum 35% rate, the credit is worth up to $1,050 for one child or $2,100 for two or more. At the 20% rate (for incomes above $43,000), the maximum credit is $600 or $1,200.

Important: if you also have a Dependent Care FSA through your employer, the expenses you use for the FSA cannot also be claimed for this credit. The FSA reduces your taxable wages; the credit is a separate mechanism. You can use both, but you cannot double-count the same expenses. A married couple with two children and $5,000 in a Dependent Care FSA might apply the FSA to the first $5,000 of care expenses and use the remaining $1,000 in eligible expenses toward the Child and Dependent Care Credit.

docs.title: Energy Credits: Home and Vehicle
docs.text:
The federal government provides significant tax incentives for energy-efficient upgrades — and these are non-refundable credits, meaning they reduce your tax bill directly, though they cannot reduce your tax below zero.

Energy Efficiency Credits (Home — Section 25C): The Inflation Reduction Act significantly expanded the home energy credit. You can claim up to \(1,200 per year** for qualifying improvements like insulation, exterior windows and doors, energy audits, and certain HVAC systems. Heat pumps and biomass stoves qualify for a separate, higher credit of up to **\)2,000 per year. These two credits can stack: a homeowner who installs new insulation (\(500 credit) and a heat pump (\)2,000 credit) in the same year can claim up to $2,500 in credits. The $1,200 cap on the first category is an annual limit, not a lifetime limit — you can claim it again in future years for additional qualifying improvements.

Electric Vehicle Tax Credit (Section 30D): New electric vehicles purchased in 2025 may qualify for a credit of up to $7,500. The actual credit amount depends on the vehicle's battery capacity and where it was assembled. Income limits apply: $150,000 for single filers, $225,000 for heads of household, and $300,000 for married filing jointly. There are also MSRP caps: $55,000 for most vehicles and $80,000 for SUVs, vans, and pickup trucks. Beginning in 2024, you can also transfer the credit to the dealer at point of sale, effectively receiving the discount upfront rather than waiting until you file. Used EVs qualify for a separate credit of up to $4,000. Before purchasing an EV, verify the specific vehicle's qualification status at fueleconomy.gov, as not all electric vehicles qualify.

Energy Efficient Home Credit (Section 45L): This credit is primarily designed for builders and developers who construct or substantially improve energy-efficient homes for sale. As a typical homeowner or renter, this credit generally does not apply to you — but if you are building new construction or are a real estate developer, it is worth investigating.

docs.title: Diagram: Tax Savings Calculator — Traditional vs. Roth IRA
docs.text:
IRA Comparison Calculator — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply

Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Students will be able to calculate the after-tax dollar difference between choosing a Traditional IRA vs. a Roth IRA at different income levels and time horizons, and identify which option produces a better outcome in their specific scenario.

Layout:

  • Two-panel layout: input controls on the left, results visualization on the right
  • Top input row: Current Age (slider: 22–65), Retirement Age (slider: 50–75), Annual IRA Contribution (\(1,000–\)7,000 in $500 steps), Annual Expected Return (5%–10% slider)
  • Second input row: Current Tax Bracket dropdown (10%, 12%, 22%, 24%, 32%, 35%, 37%), Expected Retirement Tax Bracket dropdown (same options)
  • Right panel: Side-by-side bar chart showing projected account values at retirement — one bar for Traditional IRA (labeled with after-tax value assuming retirement bracket applied to all withdrawals), one bar for Roth IRA (tax-free balance)
  • Below chart: Summary table with three rows: Gross Account Value (both same), Tax Owed on Withdrawal (Traditional: balance × retirement bracket; Roth: $0), After-Tax Value (the key comparison)
  • Color coding: Traditional IRA = #4472C4 (blue), Roth IRA = #ED7D31 (orange)
  • Highlight banner: "Roth is better by $X,XXX" or "Traditional is better by $X,XXX" depending on bracket comparison

Interactions:

  • All sliders and dropdowns update the chart in real time
  • Hovering over bars shows exact dollar amounts
  • An annotation line marks the crossover income level at which Roth becomes advantageous
  • A "Reset to Defaults" button sets: Age 30, Retire 65, $6,000 contribution, 7% return, 22% current bracket, 22% retirement bracket

Key insight to communicate: When current bracket > retirement bracket, Traditional wins. When retirement bracket > current bracket, Roth wins. When equal, Roth often still wins due to tax-free growth and no RMDs.

Implementation: Chart.js for bar charts, vanilla HTML/CSS/JS for sliders and controls, no external dependencies beyond Chart.js CDN.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
This chapter covered a broad set of tools, and it can feel like a lot to hold in mind at once. Here is the condensed version of the most important points:

  • Max your employer match first. Capturing your full 401(k) employer match is an immediate 50–100% return on your contribution. Nothing else comes close.
  • The HSA is the best tax-advantaged account in the tax code for those who qualify. If you have an HDHP, contribute to your HSA every year, invest the balance, and save receipts for future reimbursement.
  • Traditional or Roth IRA depends on your tax rate trajectory. If you expect a lower tax rate in retirement, Traditional is usually better. If you expect a higher tax rate later, Roth wins.
  • Above-the-line deductions help everyone, including standard deduction filers. Self-employed individuals have especially powerful above-the-line options.
  • Most people should take the standard deduction. Only about 10% of taxpayers itemize. Before spending hours tracking itemized deductions, verify your total itemized amount actually exceeds the standard deduction threshold.
  • The SALT cap is a real constraint. At $10,000, high-tax-state homeowners may already be at or near their SALT ceiling before adding any mortgage interest.
  • Charitable giving is more powerful with appreciated assets. Donating stock you have held more than a year avoids capital gains and gives you a full fair-market-value deduction.
  • QCDs are exceptional for retirees. If you are 70.5+ and charitably inclined, using IRA funds for a QCD is better than taking the distribution and donating separately.
  • Energy credits are real money. Up to $2,000 for a heat pump plus $1,200 for other improvements equals up to $3,200 in direct tax reduction — and you can claim the improvement categories again in future years.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
You do not need to act on all of this at once. Use this prioritized action list to identify your next step:

  1. Confirm you are capturing your full employer 401(k) match. Log in to your 401(k) portal and verify your contribution rate. Increase it if needed.
  2. Check your HSA eligibility. Are you enrolled in an HDHP? If yes, open an HSA if you do not have one, and start contributing — even a small monthly amount adds up.
  3. Decide Traditional vs. Roth IRA. Compare your current bracket to what you realistically expect in retirement. Open an IRA account at any major brokerage (Fidelity, Vanguard, Schwab) if you do not already have one.
  4. Run the itemized vs. standard deduction comparison. Add up your potential itemized deductions: SALT (capped at $10,000), mortgage interest, charitable giving, and any medical costs above 7.5% of your AGI. Compare to the standard deduction for your filing status.
  5. Review above-the-line deductions you may be missing. Student loan interest, educator expenses, HSA contributions, self-employment deductions — these are easy to claim and available without itemizing.
  6. If you are 70.5+, consider a QCD for any planned charitable giving, especially if you have RMDs you do not need for living expenses.
  7. If you bought or plan to buy a new EV or made home energy improvements, verify credit eligibility and document your receipts and certifications. These credits expire or change with legislation, so timing matters.

Start with What You Can Control Today

You cannot change your income from January through March before you file. But you can make an IRA contribution for the prior tax year any time up to April 15. You can also open an HSA retroactively for the prior year if you had HDHP coverage. These are among the few genuine "time machine" opportunities in the tax code — use them.

Chapter 4 will cover self-employment income, Schedule C deductions, estimated quarterly taxes, and the Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction — the strategies that apply specifically to freelancers, contractors, and small business owners.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers everything you need to know if you earn any income outside of a traditional W-2 job. You'll learn how self-employment tax works, how to complete Schedule C, which business expenses you can deduct (home office, vehicle, equipment), how to make quarterly estimated tax payments so you don't get hit with penalties, and how to claim the 20% QBI deduction. Also covered: the self-employed retirement accounts (Solo 401k, SEP-IRA) and the health insurance deduction that most self-employed people miss.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 19 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Self-Employment Tax Rate

  17. Hobby Loss Limitation
  18. Qualified Business Income (QBI)
  19. QBI Deduction 20% Rule

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 3: Legal Tax Reduction Strategies

docs.title: The Side Hustle Tax Surprise
docs.text:
Imagine this: you drove for Uber on weekends and did some freelance design work on the side. At the end of the year, you've pulled in an extra $10,000. Nice work. Then April rolls around and your tax software says you owe $2,530 in self-employment tax — before income tax even enters the picture. If you're in the 22% federal bracket, you could easily owe another $1,800 in income tax on top of that. Suddenly that $10,000 feels more like $5,700.

Nobody warned you about self-employment tax. Your employer never mentioned it because they were quietly paying half of it on your behalf your entire working life. The moment you start earning income on your own — driving, freelancing, consulting, selling — you become both the employee and the employer, and the IRS expects you to pay both sides. The good news is that there are real, legal strategies to reduce that bill significantly. This chapter walks you through every one of them.

We'll cover the mechanics of self-employment tax, how to file Schedule C, every major deduction category, quarterly estimated tax payments, powerful retirement account options, and the 20% QBI deduction that many self-employed people leave on the table. By the end of this chapter, you'll have a complete playbook for turning your side hustle income into the smallest legal tax bill possible.

docs.title: Part 1: Self-Employment Tax — The Tax Nobody Warned You About
What Is Self-Employment Tax?
docs.text:
When you work for an employer, your paycheck stub shows FICA withholding — that's Social Security (6.2%) and Medicare (1.45%), totaling 7.65%. What the stub doesn't show you is that your employer matches that exact amount on your behalf, invisibly. The combined total going to the IRS is 15.3%. When you're self-employed, there's no employer. You are the employer. So the self-employment tax rate of 15.3% falls entirely on you.

The SE tax calculation method has a small built-in correction to account for this reality. The IRS doesn't make you pay 15.3% on every dollar of gross income. Instead, you multiply your net self-employment income by 0.9235 first, then apply the 15.3% rate. That 0.9235 factor (which is simply 1 minus 0.0765) represents the employer's half of FICA — the portion a real employer would have deducted as a business expense before figuring payroll taxes. It's the IRS's way of giving self-employed people an equivalent break. After you calculate your SE tax, you get to deduct half of it directly from your gross income as an above-the-line deduction on Schedule 1 of Form 1040, which lowers your AGI and your income tax bill.

The Social Security portion (12.4%) only applies to the first $176,100 of net SE income in 2025. Above that threshold, only the 2.9% Medicare rate applies — and if your total income from all sources exceeds $200,000 (single) or $250,000 (married filing jointly), you owe an additional 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax on the excess.

docs.title: The 1099-NEC: The Paper That Starts It All
docs.text:
The 1099-NEC form (Non-Employee Compensation) is what clients and payers send you when they've paid you $600 or more during the year for services. If you drove for a rideshare company, received consulting fees, or got paid as an independent contractor, you'll receive a 1099-NEC by January 31st of the following year. The payer also sends a copy directly to the IRS, so the IRS already knows about that income before you file.

You Owe Tax Even Without a 1099-NEC

The $600 threshold is the payer's reporting requirement — not your filing threshold. If you earn $400 or more in net self-employment income from any source, you are legally required to file a tax return and pay SE tax. No 1099-NEC required. The IRS does not care whether your client sent you paperwork. Income is income.

docs.title: Worked Example: $50,000 in Freelance Income
docs.text:
Let's say you earned $50,000 as a freelance web developer this year and had $8,000 in legitimate business expenses (software subscriptions, a new laptop, professional training). Your net profit on Schedule C is $42,000. Here's how your SE tax works:

Step 1 — Net SE income: \( \$42{,}000 \)

Step 2 — Apply the 0.9235 factor: \( \$42{,}000 \times 0.9235 = \$38{,}787 \)

Step 3 — Apply the SE tax rate: \( \$38{,}787 \times 0.153 = \$5{,}934 \)

Step 4 — Deduct half of SE tax from gross income: \( \$5{,}934 \div 2 = \$2{,}967 \) deducted above-the-line

Your SE tax bill is $5,934. You also get to subtract \(2,967 from your AGI, which reduces the income subject to your regular income tax rate. If you're in the 22% bracket, that AGI reduction saves you another **\)653** in income tax. The deduction doesn't eliminate SE tax, but it softens the blow.

docs.title: Part 2: Schedule C — Your Business Tax Return
What Is Schedule C?
docs.text:
Schedule C ("Profit or Loss From Business") is the IRS form where sole proprietors — freelancers, independent contractors, gig workers, and single-member LLC owners — report their business activity. It's a one-page form (with supporting schedules) that flows directly into your Form 1040. You are not filing a separate business tax return; Schedule C is part of your personal return.

You file one Schedule C per business. If you drive for Uber AND do freelance photography, you file two Schedule C forms — one for each distinct business activity. Schedule C asks for your business name, your activity code (a six-digit code identifying your industry), and then walks you through income and expenses.

docs.title: Reporting Business Income
docs.text: Business income reporting on Schedule C is straightforward: you list the gross receipts or sales your business received during the year. This includes every dollar clients paid you, whether or not you received a 1099-NEC. Add up all your invoices paid, PayPal transfers, Venmo payments for business services, check deposits — the total goes on Line 1 of Schedule C. If you had any returns or allowances (you refunded a client, for example), those reduce your gross income on Line 2.

docs.title: Business Expense Deductions: The Big Categories
docs.text:
This is where Schedule C gets powerful. Business expense deductions reduce your net profit, which lowers both your SE tax and your income tax simultaneously. Every dollar of legitimate business expenses saves you roughly 35–40 cents in combined taxes if you're in the 22% bracket (15.3% SE tax + 22% income tax, less the above-the-line deduction adjustment). The IRS requires that expenses be "ordinary and necessary" — meaning common in your industry and helpful for your business. Personal expenses do not qualify, and mixed-use expenses must be allocated.

The table below covers the major deduction categories available on Schedule C:

Deduction Category What Qualifies Schedule C Line Notes Advertising Paid ads, website hosting, business cards, logo design Line 8 Must be for the business, not personal branding Car and Truck Expenses Business miles or actual vehicle costs Line 9 Standard mileage or actual method — choose one Commissions and Fees Fees paid to subcontractors, agents, referral payments Line 10 Issue a 1099-NEC if you paid $600+ to an individual Contract Labor Payments to freelancers you hired Line 11 Requires 1099-NEC if $600+ per person Depreciation Business equipment, computers, furniture (Section 179) Line 13 First-year expensing available via Section 179 Insurance Business liability, professional E&O insurance Line 15 NOT health insurance — that goes on Schedule 1 Legal and Professional Accountant fees, attorney fees for the business Line 17 Tax prep fees allocable to your business qualify Office Expense Printer ink, paper, postage, office supplies Line 18 Small recurring supplies; large items may be depreciated Rent or Lease Studio space, equipment rental, co-working space Line 20 Home office deduction goes here via Form 8829 Repairs and Maintenance Equipment repairs, business property upkeep Line 21 Cannot be capital improvements — those get depreciated Supplies Industry-specific supplies used in your work Line 22 Distinct from general office supplies Taxes and Licenses Business licenses, state taxes, professional licenses Line 23 Does NOT include your federal income or SE tax Travel Airfare, hotels, meals (50%), transportation for business trips Line 24 Must be overnight and away from your tax home Utilities Proportion of phone/internet used for business Line 25 Keep records of business vs. personal use percentage Meals 50% of business meals with clients or while traveling Line 24b Must document who, where, when, and business purpose Education Courses, books, conferences in your current field Line 27a (Other) Must maintain or improve current trade skills Software Subscriptions Adobe, Quickbooks, Zoom, industry-specific tools Line 27a (Other) 100% deductible if used exclusively for business Home Office Dedicated workspace in your home Form 8829 Requires exclusive and regular business use

docs.title: Home Office Deduction: The Most Misunderstood Deduction
docs.text:
The home office deduction is one of the most valuable deductions available to self-employed people, and one of the most avoided — because people fear it will trigger an audit. The audit fear is largely a myth rooted in the 1990s. Today, the IRS's own simplified method makes claiming this deduction straightforward and defensible.

To qualify, you must use a specific area of your home regularly and exclusively for business. The space doesn't need to be a separate room with a door — a dedicated corner of a room used only for business qualifies. What does not qualify: a kitchen table where you sometimes work, a couch in the living room, or a room that doubles as a guest bedroom. The "exclusive use" test is the one people most often fail. If your "home office" is also where the kids do homework or where you watch Netflix, it doesn't qualify.

Two methods to calculate the home office deduction:

  • Simplified Method: Multiply the square footage of your home office by \(5, up to a maximum of 300 square feet. Maximum deduction: **\)1,500**. No depreciation recapture when you sell your home. Easy to calculate.
  • Actual Expenses Method: Calculate the percentage of your home used for business (office square footage ÷ total home square footage), then apply that percentage to your actual home expenses — mortgage interest or rent, utilities, homeowner's/renter's insurance, repairs, and depreciation. This method typically produces a larger deduction for people with higher housing costs but requires more recordkeeping and Form 8829.

Which Method Should You Choose?

Use the simplified method if your home office is under 200 square feet or if your housing costs are relatively low. Use actual expenses if your rent or mortgage is high and your office represents a meaningful percentage of your home — for example, a 200 sq ft office in a 1,000 sq ft apartment is 20%, and actual expenses on a $2,000/month rent would yield a $4,800 annual deduction versus $1,000 with the simplified method. Run both calculations and use whichever is larger.

docs.title: Vehicle Deductions: Standard Mileage vs. Actual Expenses
docs.text:
The vehicle business use deduction lets you deduct the cost of using your personal vehicle for business purposes. You have two methods, and you generally must choose one in the first year you use the vehicle for business.

The standard mileage rate for 2025 is 70 cents per mile for business driving. This is the simplest method: track every business mile with a mileage log (date, destination, business purpose, miles), multiply total business miles by $0.70, and deduct the result. If you drove 10,000 business miles this year, your deduction is $7,000. The standard rate already accounts for gas, oil, tires, insurance, and depreciation — you cannot deduct those separately if you use this method.

The actual expenses method lets you deduct the actual cost of gas, oil, insurance, registration fees, repairs, and depreciation — but only the business-use percentage. If you drove 15,000 total miles and 10,000 were for business, your business-use percentage is 66.7%. You'd apply that to your total vehicle costs. This method requires more recordkeeping and typically benefits people with expensive vehicles and high actual costs.

Regardless of which method you choose, you must keep a mileage log. The IRS requires contemporaneous records — meaning you track miles as you drive them, not from memory at tax time. Apps like MileIQ, TripLog, or even a simple spreadsheet work well.

docs.title: Part 3: Quarterly Estimated Taxes — Never Get Ambushed Again
Why You Owe Taxes Quarterly
docs.text:
The US tax system operates on a pay-as-you-go basis. W-2 employees satisfy this through payroll withholding every payday. Self-employed people have no automatic withholding, so the IRS requires you to make quarterly estimated tax payments throughout the year. These payments cover both your income tax and your self-employment tax as you earn the money — not all at once in April.

If you skip quarterly payments and owe more than $1,000 in taxes at filing, the IRS charges an underpayment penalty. In 2025, that penalty rate is 8% annualized — not catastrophic, but completely avoidable. Think of quarterly payments as forced savings toward a tax bill you already know is coming.

docs.title: Estimated Tax Due Dates 2025
docs.text:
The estimated tax due dates for the 2025 tax year are:

  • April 15, 2025 — covers income earned January 1 – March 31
  • June 16, 2025 — covers income earned April 1 – May 31 (note: this is not the end of Q2)
  • September 15, 2025 — covers income earned June 1 – August 31
  • January 15, 2026 — covers income earned September 1 – December 31

Pay online through IRS Direct Pay (free, instant confirmation) or through EFTPS (Electronic Federal Tax Payment System). You can also mail Form 1040-ES with a check, but electronic payments are faster and give you a receipt.

docs.title: The Safe Harbor Rule: Avoid Penalties Automatically
docs.text:
The safe harbor tax rule is the easiest way to ensure you never owe an underpayment penalty, regardless of how much your income fluctuates. Safe harbor means that if your quarterly payments total enough, the IRS won't penalize you even if you end up owing money in April. There are two safe harbor thresholds:

  • Pay 100% of your prior year's total tax liability, spread across the four payment dates — OR —
  • Pay 90% of your current year's actual tax liability

If your prior year's AGI exceeded $150,000 (or $75,000 if married filing separately), the prior year threshold rises to 110% of last year's tax. This prevents high-income earners from coasting on a low prior-year tax number.

Safe Harbor in Practice

Safe harbor is especially valuable when your income is unpredictable. If last year you paid $8,000 in total federal taxes, simply divide that by 4 and pay $2,000 per quarter this year. Even if your income doubles, you won't face a penalty. You'll just owe the balance when you file — but no penalty.

docs.title: Practical Example: Quarterly Schedule for a $60,000 Freelancer
docs.text:
Imagine you're a freelance copywriter projecting $60,000 in gross income for 2025, with about $10,000 in business expenses, giving you $50,000 in net profit. You're single and this is your only income. Here's a simplified quarterly plan:

Estimate total 2025 tax:

  • SE tax: \( \$50{,}000 \times 0.9235 \times 0.153 = \$7{,}065 \)
  • AGI after SE deduction: \( \$50{,}000 - \$3{,}533 = \$46{,}467 \)
  • Taxable income (after standard deduction of $15,000): \( \$31{,}467 \)
  • Estimated income tax (10%/12% bracket): approximately $3,560
  • Total estimated tax: \( \$7{,}065 + \$3{,}560 = \$10{,}625 \)
  • Quarterly payment: \( \$10{,}625 \div 4 \approx \$2{,}656 \) per quarter

If your prior year tax was $9,200, safe harbor requires \( \$9{,}200 \div 4 = \$2{,}300 \) per quarter — a lower and safer number to start with while you see how your income develops.

docs.title: Self-Employed Health Insurance Deduction
docs.text:
Before we get to retirement accounts, there's a deduction many self-employed people overlook: self-employed health insurance. If you paid premiums for health, dental, or vision insurance for yourself, your spouse, or your dependents — and you were not eligible for coverage through an employer or your spouse's employer — you can deduct 100% of those premiums directly from your gross income as an above-the-line deduction. This deduction appears on Schedule 1 of Form 1040, not on Schedule C.

The only limit: the deduction cannot exceed your net profit from self-employment. If your business lost money or broke even, you cannot use this deduction (though you may still be able to itemize the premiums as a medical expense). For a self-employed person paying $600/month in health insurance premiums, this deduction eliminates $7,200 of taxable income — saving roughly $2,520 in combined taxes at the 22% bracket level.

docs.title: Solo 401(k): The Most Powerful Option
docs.text:
The Solo 401(k) — also called the Individual 401(k) or Self-Employed 401(k) — is available to self-employed individuals with no full-time employees other than a spouse. It is the most powerful retirement account available to self-employed people because it allows contributions in two roles: as the employee and as the employer.

2025 Solo 401(k) Contribution Limits:

  • Employee (elective deferral) contribution: Up to **\(23,500** (\)31,000 if age 50 or older, due to the $7,500 catch-up contribution)
  • Employer (profit-sharing) contribution: Up to 25% of your net self-employment compensation
  • Combined total limit: $70,000 (or $77,500 if age 50+)

The employee contribution can be made as a traditional (pre-tax) contribution, reducing your taxable income dollar-for-dollar, or as a Roth contribution (after-tax, grows tax-free). The employer contribution is always pre-tax.

Example: You have $80,000 in net SE income. You contribute $23,500 as the employee. Then as the employer, you contribute 25% of your adjusted net SE income (approximately $19,000, after the SE deduction calculation). Total contribution: approximately $42,500. Every dollar of that contribution reduces your taxable income.

You must open a Solo 401(k) account by December 31st of the tax year you want to contribute for. Contributions themselves can be made up to your tax filing deadline (including extensions).

docs.title: SEP-IRA: The Simple Option
docs.text:
The SEP-IRA (Simplified Employee Pension Individual Retirement Account) is the easiest self-employed retirement account to set up and administer. There are no annual filings, no complicated rules about employee vs. employer contributions, and you can open one right up to your tax filing deadline (including extensions) and still count the contribution for the prior tax year.

2025 SEP-IRA Contribution Limit: The lesser of 25% of your net self-employment income (after the SE tax deduction) or $70,000.

Example: With \(50,000 in net SE income and after subtracting half of your SE tax (\)3,533), your net self-employment compensation for SEP purposes is approximately \(46,467. Twenty-five percent of that is about **\)11,617** — your maximum SEP-IRA contribution.

The SEP-IRA is simpler than the Solo 401(k) but has one key limitation: if you have employees, you must contribute the same percentage of compensation for them as you contribute for yourself. That requirement makes the SEP expensive for businesses with staff. If you're truly solo, it doesn't matter.

docs.title: Retirement Account Comparison
docs.text:
Feature Solo 401(k) SEP-IRA SIMPLE IRA 2025 Max Contribution \(70,000 (\)77,500 age 50+) $70,000 \(16,500 (\)20,000 age 50+) Employee Contribution Yes — up to $23,500 No Yes — up to $16,500 Employer Contribution Up to 25% of compensation Up to 25% of net SE income Up to 3% match Roth Option Yes No (Roth SEP exists but rare) Yes (Roth SIMPLE) Setup Deadline December 31 of tax year Tax filing deadline (+ extensions) October 1 of tax year Filing Requirements Form 5500-EZ if assets > $250k None None Employees Allowed No full-time non-spouse employees Yes, but must cover them equally Yes Best For High-income solo earners Simple, flexible solo earners Small businesses with employees

Which Account Wins?

For most self-employed people earning under \(100,000, both the Solo 401(k) and SEP-IRA produce similar contribution amounts — but the Solo 401(k) wins for higher earners because the flat employee contribution (\)23,500) allows you to shelter more income at lower profit levels. A Solo 401(k) owner with $40,000 in net SE income can still contribute up to $32,000, while a SEP-IRA is capped at roughly $9,500 for the same income.

docs.title: Part 5: The QBI Deduction — 20% Off Your Business Income
What Is Qualified Business Income?
docs.text:
The Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction, created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, is one of the most significant tax benefits available to self-employed people. It allows eligible business owners to deduct up to 20% of their qualified business income from their taxable income — on top of all other deductions. This is not a business deduction; it's a deduction taken on your personal Form 1040 that reduces the income on which your income tax (not SE tax) is calculated.

Qualified Business Income is your net profit from a qualified trade or business conducted as a sole proprietorship (Schedule C), partnership, S-Corp, or certain trust or estate — minus any deductions allocable to that business (like the SE tax deduction and the self-employed health insurance deduction). W-2 wages from your employer do not qualify. Investment income does not qualify. Only actual business income from a trade or business you materially participate in counts.

docs.title: The QBI Deduction 20% Rule
docs.text:
The QBI deduction 20% rule works like this: calculate your qualified business income, take 20% of it, and subtract that from your taxable income. The deduction is limited to 20% of your taxable income (excluding capital gains), so it can never exceed the income it applies to.

Worked Example:

You're a single freelance graphic designer with:

  • Net Schedule C profit: $60,000
  • SE tax deduction: \( \$60{,}000 \times 0.9235 \times 0.153 \div 2 = \$4{,}239 \)
  • Self-employed health insurance deduction: $7,200
  • Qualified Business Income: \( \$60{,}000 - \$4{,}239 - \$7{,}200 = \$48{,}561 \)
  • QBI deduction (20%): \( \$48{,}561 \times 0.20 = \$9{,}712 \)
  • Standard deduction: $15,000
  • Taxable income: \( \$60{,}000 - \$4{,}239 - \$7{,}200 - \$15{,}000 - \$9{,}712 = \$23{,}849 \)

Without the QBI deduction, taxable income would have been \(33,561. The QBI deduction alone saves this designer approximately **\)1,163 in income taxes** (at the 12% rate on that band of income) — for doing nothing more than filling out a form.

Phase-Out Limitations: The 20% QBI deduction phases out for higher earners in certain "Specified Service Trades or Businesses" (SSTBs) — fields like law, medicine, consulting, financial services, and performing arts. The phase-out begins at \(197,300 taxable income for single filers** and **\)394,600 for married filing jointly in 2025. If your taxable income is below those thresholds, you generally get the full 20% deduction regardless of your profession. Above those thresholds, SSTBs lose the deduction entirely, while other businesses face W-2 wage and property limitations.

Most Self-Employed People Qualify for the Full QBI Deduction

If your taxable income is under $197,300 (single) or $394,600 (MFJ), the SSTB limitations do not apply to you. A freelance lawyer earning $80,000 a year gets the full 20% QBI deduction just like a freelance plumber. The phase-out rules are aimed at high-income service professionals, not the typical side hustler or mid-income freelancer.

docs.title: Business vs. Hobby: The IRS Line You Cannot Cross
docs.text: There is one classification that can erase all of your business deductions in a single audit finding: the IRS declaring your "business" is actually a hobby. Understanding the difference between a business and a hobby — and the rules that govern that distinction — is essential if you're deducting losses from an activity you genuinely enjoy.

docs.title: Business vs. Hobby Rules
docs.text:
Under the business vs. hobby rules, the IRS looks at whether you operate your activity with a genuine profit motive. You don't have to be profitable every year, but you must be running the activity in a businesslike manner with a reasonable expectation of profit. The IRS considers nine factors:

  1. The manner in which you carry on the activity (do you keep books, have a separate bank account, etc.)
  2. Your expertise or that of your advisors
  3. The time and effort you put into the activity
  4. Whether you depend on this income for your livelihood
  5. Whether your losses are due to startup costs or unusual events (vs. ongoing losses)
  6. Your history of income or losses from this activity
  7. The amount of occasional profits, if any
  8. Your overall financial status (do you have other income that could absorb the losses?)
  9. Elements of personal pleasure or recreation

No single factor is determinative. The IRS looks at the totality of the circumstances.

The Presumption Rule: The IRS presumes a profit motive — and therefore a legitimate business — if your activity is profitable in at least 3 out of 5 consecutive years (2 out of 7 years for horse breeding). If you meet this threshold, the burden shifts to the IRS to prove otherwise. If you don't meet it, you may still be treated as a business if you can demonstrate a genuine profit motive through the nine factors above.

docs.title: Hobby Loss Limitation
docs.text:
If the IRS reclassifies your activity as a hobby, the hobby loss limitation rules kick in and the consequences are severe. Under current law (post-2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act):

  • You must still report all hobby income on Schedule 1 of Form 1040
  • You cannot deduct any hobby expenses — not against the income, not anywhere else
  • The old pre-2018 rule that allowed hobby expenses as a miscellaneous itemized deduction was eliminated

This means if the IRS reclassifies your freelance photography "business" as a hobby, you report $8,000 in income but cannot deduct the $5,000 camera equipment, $1,200 in editing software, or $600 in travel costs. You're taxed on the full $8,000 with no offsets. This is the most punishing outcome of a hobby loss determination.

How to Protect Your Business Status

Keep a separate business bank account. Maintain a profit and loss statement. Keep a log of hours worked. Have a written business plan, even a simple one. Market your services actively. Seek professional advice. Raise your prices if you're consistently losing money. These practices demonstrate profit intent — and they're exactly what the IRS looks for when it reviews a business with repeated losses.

docs.title: Diagram: Quarterly Estimated Tax Calculator
docs.text:
Quarterly Estimated Tax Calculator — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply

Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Given freelance income and expense inputs, the student will be able to calculate their four quarterly estimated tax payment amounts and total annual tax liability.

Layout and Components:

Input Panel (left or top section): - Slider or number input: "Annual Gross Business Income" — range $0 to $300,000, default $60,000 - Slider or number input: "Annual Business Expenses" — range $0 to $150,000, default $10,000 - Dropdown: "Filing Status" — Single, Married Filing Jointly, Head of Household - Checkbox: "Age 50+" (affects catch-up contribution display) - Number input: "Prior Year Total Tax Paid" — for safe harbor calculation - Number input: "Self-Employed Health Insurance Premiums" — annual amount

Output Panel (right or bottom section): - Calculated fields (auto-updating): - Net Schedule C Profit (Gross - Expenses) - SE Tax Amount (Net × 0.9235 × 0.153) - SE Tax Deduction (half of SE tax) - QBI Deduction (20% of QBI) - Estimated Federal Income Tax - Total Annual Tax - Recommended Quarterly Payment (Total ÷ 4) - Safe Harbor Quarterly Amount (Prior Year Tax ÷ 4, flagged if AGI > $150k: use 110%) - Payment schedule table showing four 2025 due dates with recommended payment amounts

Visualization: - Horizontal stacked bar or donut chart showing income breakdown: - Business Expenses (gray) - SE Tax (red) - Income Tax (orange) - QBI Deduction Savings (green highlight) - Take-Home / After-Tax Income (blue)

Behavior: - All outputs update in real-time as sliders move - If calculated quarterly payment exceeds safe harbor amount, highlight the safe harbor option in green with a tooltip explaining the choice - If net profit is negative, display a message: "No SE tax owed — verify your expense deductions" - Color scheme: IRS blue (#003087), white, with green for savings/deductions and red for taxes owed

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript for calculations

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The self-employment tax rate is 15.3% on 92.35% of your net SE income. You pay both the employee and employer sides of FICA when you're self-employed.
  • Every 1099-NEC you receive is also sent to the IRS. Report all income — even income under $600 that didn't generate a 1099-NEC.
  • Schedule C is where you subtract legitimate business expenses from gross income. Meticulous recordkeeping is the difference between a big deduction and an audit problem.
  • The home office deduction is real and legal. Use the $5/sq ft simplified method (max $1,500) or actual expenses — whichever produces the larger deduction.
  • The 2025 standard mileage rate is 70 cents per mile. Keep a mileage log for every business trip.
  • Make quarterly estimated tax payments by April 15, June 16, September 15, and January 15. The safe harbor rule lets you base payments on prior year tax to avoid all underpayment penalties.
  • Self-employed health insurance premiums are 100% deductible above-the-line (up to your net profit).
  • The Solo 401(k) allows up to $70,000 in total 2025 contributions — far more than any other plan for high earners.
  • The SEP-IRA is simpler: up to 25% of net SE income, max $70,000, openable until your filing deadline.
  • The QBI deduction gives most self-employed people a 20% discount on their qualified business income — claim it on Form 8995.
  • A hobby cannot deduct losses. Protect your business status by operating like a real business with records, accounts, and profit intent.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
The following actions will put this chapter's lessons to work immediately. Complete these in order:

  1. Open a dedicated business checking account if you don't have one. Commingling personal and business funds is the fastest way to destroy your deduction records and invite scrutiny.

  2. Download a mileage tracking app (MileIQ, TripLog, or Everlane) and start logging every business drive today. You can't recover miles from memory after the fact.

  3. Measure your home office and calculate both the simplified deduction ($5 × sq ft) and the actual expenses method. Choose the larger one.

  4. Look up your prior year total federal tax liability (Form 1040, Line 24). Divide by 4. That's your safe harbor quarterly payment. Set a calendar reminder for April 15, June 16, September 15, and January 15.

  5. Open a Solo 401(k) or SEP-IRA before December 31 (Solo 401k) or before your filing deadline (SEP-IRA). Even a $1,000 contribution this year builds the habit and reduces your bill.

  6. Run the QBI deduction calculator above with your actual projected numbers. If you're under the income phase-out thresholds, you're likely leaving a 20% income discount unclaimed.

  7. Save 25–30% of every payment you receive in a separate "tax savings" sub-account. This single habit eliminates the April panic that catches most new self-employed people by surprise.

If you take these seven actions, you will enter next filing season as someone who planned — not someone who panics. The tax code genuinely rewards self-employed people who understand it. Now you do.

Next: Chapter 5 — Investments, Capital Gains, and the Forms That Come With Them
