docs.text: title: Chapter 5: Year-Round Tax Planning description: Proactive tax planning strategies including W-4 optimization, Roth conversions, deduction bunching, income timing, and retirement account deadlines. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-21 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter shifts from reactive (filing season) to proactive tax management. You'll learn how to optimize your W-4 withholding so you stop giving the IRS an interest-free loan, how to use Roth conversions strategically, how to bunch deductions in alternating years, how to time income and deductions to manage your tax bracket, and how to build a personal tax calendar. You'll also finalize your retirement account strategy with deadlines and a holistic retirement plan design.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 12 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. W-4 Withholding Form
  2. Withholding Adjustment Steps
  3. Over-Withholding Downsides
  4. Under-Withholding Penalties
  5. Tax Calendar Key Dates
  6. Roth Conversion Strategy
  7. Bunching Deductions Strategy
  8. Income Timing Strategy
  9. Year-End Tax Checklist
  10. Tax Bracket Management
  11. Retirement Account Deadlines
  12. Retirement Plan Design

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 3: Legal Tax Reduction Strategies
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes

docs.title: The Mindset Shift: From Filer to Planner
docs.text:
Most people think about taxes once a year — in a panic between February and April. That approach is expensive. By the time you sit down with your W-2 and a coffee in late March, virtually every decision that determines your 2024 tax bill has already been made. You're just recording history at that point.

Year-round tax planning flips the script. Instead of reacting to your tax situation, you design it. Small, deliberate choices made in March, June, and October can easily be worth $1,000 to $5,000 in legitimate savings for a median-income household — and significantly more for higher earners. None of this requires aggressive strategies or gray-area maneuvers. It requires knowing the levers available to you and pulling them at the right time.

This chapter gives you those levers: your withholding, your retirement accounts, the timing of income and deductions, and the specific dates that matter. By the end, you'll have a personal tax calendar and a clear action plan — not a vague intention to "be more strategic next year."

docs.title: The W-4 Withholding Form
docs.text:
Your employer doesn't know your tax situation. They know your salary and whatever you tell them on Form W-4. The W-4 is your instruction sheet to payroll — it tells them how much federal income tax to withhold from each paycheck. If the instructions are wrong, your withholding will be wrong, and you'll either owe a lump sum at filing or receive a refund that represents money you've already earned sitting idle with the IRS.

The current W-4 (redesigned in 2020) no longer uses "allowances." Instead, it has five steps:

  1. Step 1 — Personal information and filing status
  2. Step 2 — Multiple jobs or a working spouse (this is where most people underpay)
  3. Step 3 — Dependents and the Child Tax Credit
  4. Step 4a — Other income not subject to withholding (dividends, self-employment)
  5. Step 4b — Deductions (if you plan to itemize)
  6. Step 4c — Any additional flat-dollar amount to withhold per pay period

Steps 1 and 5 are required. The rest are optional adjustments that improve accuracy.

When to Update Your W-4

File a new W-4 with your employer whenever your situation changes: marriage, divorce, a new child, a second job, a significant side income, or buying a home. You don't have to wait for open enrollment — you can submit a new W-4 to payroll any time.

docs.title: Withholding Adjustment Steps
docs.text:
Getting your withholding right is a two-step process: estimate your actual tax liability for the year, then set your withholding to match it. The IRS Tax Withholding Estimator (available at irs.gov) automates most of this calculation, but understanding the logic helps you catch errors.

The basic formula for annual withholding target is:

\[ \text{Annual Withholding Target} = \text{Estimated Tax Liability} - \text{Expected Tax Credits} \]

Where estimated tax liability uses the progressive bracket math you learned in Chapter 1:

\[ \text{Tax Liability} = \sum_{i} \left( \text{Income in Bracket}_i \times \text{Rate}_i \right) \]

Here is the practical adjustment workflow:

  1. Gather your last pay stub — find year-to-date federal withholding and gross wages
  2. Project full-year income — multiply current YTD wages by (52 / pay periods elapsed), then add any other expected income (side income, investment gains, rental income)
  3. Run the IRS Withholding Estimator — enter projected income, filing status, deductions, and credits
  4. Compare — if projected withholding exceeds your liability, reduce withholding on Step 4c (or claim the deduction on Step 4b). If it falls short, add a flat dollar amount on Step 4c
  5. Recheck mid-year — especially after any income change, new job, or life event

docs.title: Over-Withholding Downsides
docs.text:
Every April, roughly 75% of American taxpayers receive a refund. The average refund hovers around $3,000. People celebrate this. They shouldn't — at least not unconditionally.

A tax refund is not a windfall. It is the return of money you overpaid throughout the year. The IRS held it interest-free. If you had kept that $3,000 in a high-yield savings account at 4.5% interest, you would have earned approximately $135 in interest over the year. That's not life-changing, but it's yours to keep. More importantly, over-withholding creates a false sense of financial security — a psychological bonus that disguises twelve months of cash-flow shortfall.

The Refund Trap

A large refund feels good but signals that your withholding is miscalibrated. The goal is a refund close to zero — or a small, controlled underpayment. Redirect that monthly cash-flow difference toward debt paydown, an emergency fund, or retirement contributions and you come out meaningfully ahead.

The real cost of over-withholding is opportunity cost: money that could have been invested, used to pay down high-interest debt, or simply available in your checking account each month is instead sitting with the IRS. For someone with credit card debt at 22% APR, over-withholding $3,000 over the year costs them roughly $330 in avoidable interest — money paid to a credit card company instead of going into their own pocket.

docs.title: Under-Withholding Penalties
docs.text:
The opposite problem — under-withholding — carries actual financial penalties. The IRS expects you to pay your taxes as you earn income, not in one lump sum on April 15. If you don't, you may owe an underpayment penalty even if you pay your full balance due by the filing deadline.

The safe harbor rules let you avoid this penalty if:

  • You pay at least 90% of the current year's tax liability through withholding or estimated payments, OR
  • You pay at least 100% of last year's tax liability (110% if your prior-year AGI exceeded $150,000)

The penalty is calculated using the federal short-term interest rate plus 3 percentage points, applied to the amount underpaid for each quarter. In recent years this has been around 7–8% annualized. It's not catastrophic, but it's real money and entirely avoidable.

Who Is Most at Risk for Underpayment?

  • Freelancers and gig workers with no employer withholding
  • Employees who received a large bonus or commission not reflected in their W-4
  • Investors who realized large capital gains
  • Anyone who changed jobs mid-year and had a gap in withholding
  • Retirees whose pension and Social Security withholding doesn't cover their full liability

docs.title: Tax Calendar Key Dates
docs.text:
Tax planning without a calendar is just intention. The table below maps every critical federal tax date throughout the year. Mark these in your calendar now — missing them costs money.

Date What's Due January 15 Q4 estimated tax payment (for prior year Oct–Dec income) January 31 Employers must mail W-2s; 1099-NEC due to contractors April 15 Individual return due (or extension); Q1 estimated tax payment; IRA, HSA, and SEP-IRA contribution deadlines for prior year June 16 Q2 estimated tax payment (for Apr–May income) September 15 Q3 estimated tax payment (for Jun–Aug income) October 15 Extended return due (if extension filed by April 15) December 31 Last day for 401(k) contributions; Roth conversion deadline; charitable donation deadline; last day to harvest tax losses

The Quarterly Estimated Tax Pattern Is Weird — Here's Why

Notice that "Q2" covers only two months (April–May) and is due June 16, while "Q1" covers three months and is due April 15. The IRS didn't design this for taxpayer convenience — the calendar reflects historical legislative patches. Plan accordingly: your June payment may need to be proportionally larger if your income is spread evenly.

For most W-2 employees, the only dates that actively require action are the retirement contribution deadlines and the April 15 filing deadline. But if you have any self-employment income, rental income, significant investment income, or income from which taxes aren't withheld, the quarterly estimated payment dates must be on your calendar.

docs.title: Tax Bracket Management
docs.text:
The U.S. tax system is progressive and marginal — each dollar of income is taxed at the rate of the bracket it falls into, not your entire income. This creates specific "bracket zones" where strategic action is most valuable: the space between your current income and the top of your current bracket, and the gap between the bottom of the next bracket and where you currently sit.

For a single filer in 2025, the 12% bracket runs from $11,926 to $48,475. After the $15,000 standard deduction, someone with $63,475 in gross income has exactly $48,475 in taxable income and sits right at the top of the 12% bracket. Their effective marginal rate on the next dollar of income jumps to 22%. That bracket boundary is a planning target.

The key insight: it's not just about your rate today — it's about your rate now versus your rate in the future. If you expect to be in a higher bracket in retirement (unusual but not rare, especially with required minimum distributions), deferring income to retirement could actually hurt you. If you expect lower income in retirement, deferring makes sense. Tax bracket management is the discipline of deliberately shaping which year income lands in.

Common bracket management tactics:

  • Contribute to a Traditional IRA or 401(k) to reduce taxable income into a lower bracket
  • Recognize income (Roth conversions, selling appreciated assets) in years when your bracket is unusually low — such as early retirement before RMDs begin, or a year with large deductions
  • Defer income to next year (for self-employed taxpayers) when this year's bracket is higher
  • Accelerate deductions into high-income years and defer them out of low-income years

docs.title: Roth Conversion Strategy
docs.text:
A Roth conversion is simple in mechanics and powerful in effect: you move money from a Traditional IRA (or 401(k)) into a Roth IRA, paying ordinary income tax on the converted amount now, in exchange for tax-free growth and tax-free withdrawals forever after.

There is no income limit to do a Roth conversion. You can convert at any age, in any amount. The full converted amount is added to your taxable income in the year of conversion. This means conversions must be sized carefully to avoid pushing yourself into a higher bracket than intended.

The strategic case for conversion:

  • Your current tax rate is lower than your expected future rate
  • You have years of low income (early retirement, career gap, large deductible losses) where your bracket is unusually depressed
  • Your Traditional IRA has grown large and future Required Minimum Distributions (RMDs, mandatory starting at age 73) will force taxable income at potentially higher rates
  • You want to leave tax-free assets to heirs

Example: Suppose you're single with $70,000 in gross income before any retirement deductions. Your taxable income after the $15,000 standard deduction is $55,000, putting you in the 22% bracket. The top of the 22% bracket is $103,350 of taxable income — you have \( \$103,350 - \$55,000 = \$48,350 \) of "room" in the 22% bracket. Converting up to $48,350 of your Traditional IRA to Roth means you pay 22% on that money now — and never again.

Is a Roth Conversion Right for You?

Conversions make the most mathematical sense when: (1) you can pay the tax from non-IRA funds rather than the converted amount itself, (2) you have at least 10 years of tax-free growth ahead of the money, and (3) your current rate is meaningfully lower than your expected future rate. If all three conditions aren't met, run the numbers carefully — the math can go either way.

Roth conversions must be completed by December 31 of the tax year. Unlike IRA contributions, there's no "deadline extension" to April 15. If you want a 2025 conversion, it must happen by December 31, 2025.

docs.title: Bunching Deductions Strategy
docs.text:
If your itemized deductions hover close to the standard deduction every year, you're likely getting no benefit from itemizing — you'd take the standard deduction anyway. The bunching strategy solves this by deliberately concentrating two or more years' worth of deductible expenses into a single calendar year, itemizing that year and taking the standard deduction the next.

For a single filer, the 2025 standard deduction is $15,000. If your "natural" itemizable deductions — mortgage interest, state and local taxes (SALT, capped at $10,000), and charitable giving — total $13,000 per year, you lose \(2,000 of potential deduction every single year by splitting them evenly. But if you bunch two years of charitable giving into one year (\)6,000 instead of $3,000), your itemized deductions jump to $16,000 in Year 1 and you claim $14,000 more than the standard deduction (wait — let me be precise):

Year 1 (bunch): SALT $10,000 + mortgage interest $4,000 + doubled charitable $6,000 = $20,000 itemized. Benefit over standard: \( \$20,000 - \$15,000 = \$5,000 \) extra deduction.

Year 2 (standard): No charitable giving, just take the $15,000 standard deduction.

Two-year total deductions with bunching: \( \$20,000 + \$15,000 = \$35,000 \) Two-year total without bunching: \( \$13,000 + \$13,000 = \$26,000 \) — wait, you'd take standard each year: \( \$15,000 + \$15,000 = \$30,000 \)

Net bunching advantage: \( \$35,000 - \$30,000 = \$5,000 \) additional deduction over two years. At a 22% marginal rate, that's $1,100 in tax savings that cost you nothing — you gave the same amount to charity, just timed it differently.

Tools that make bunching work:

  • Donor-Advised Funds (DAFs) — contribute multiple years of charitable giving in one lump sum, get the full deduction this year, distribute to charities over future years at your pace
  • Prepaying property taxes — pay January's installment in December (subject to SALT cap limits)
  • Timing medical expenses — if you have significant unreimbursed medical costs, scheduling elective procedures in the same year as other itemizable events can push you over the floor (7.5% of AGI)

docs.title: Income Timing Strategy
docs.text:
For employees with fixed salaries, income timing options are limited. But for freelancers, business owners, investors, and anyone with discretionary income sources, the calendar year boundary is a planning tool.

Deferring income to next year makes sense when: - This year's income is unusually high (pushing you into a higher bracket) - Next year's income is expected to be lower - You can delay invoicing clients until late December so payment arrives in January - You can elect installment treatment on a business asset sale

Accelerating income into this year makes sense when: - This year's bracket is lower (early retirement, sabbatical, large deductions) - Tax rates are expected to rise in future years - You're doing a Roth conversion and have room in your current bracket - You're in a low-income year and want to "fill up" your 0% or 12% bracket with capital gains (the 0% long-term capital gains rate applies up to $48,350 for single filers in 2025)

On the deduction side, the logic is inverted:

  • Accelerate deductions into high-income years (pay the January property tax bill in December; make retirement contributions now rather than later)
  • Defer deductions to future years when you expect higher income (makes more sense to deduct at 32% than 22%)

Tax-Loss Harvesting: A December Ritual

Review your taxable investment accounts every November and December. If any positions are sitting at a loss, consider selling them before December 31 to realize the loss. You can immediately reinvest in a similar (but not "substantially identical") security. Capital losses offset capital gains dollar for dollar; excess losses offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year, with the remainder carried forward indefinitely. This is one of the cleanest free dollars in tax planning.

docs.title: Retirement Account Deadlines
docs.text:
Retirement accounts are the single most powerful tax-reduction tool available to most working adults, but each account type has its own deadline — and confusing them can cost you a year's worth of tax-advantaged growth.

Account Type 2025 Contribution Limit Catch-Up (Age 50+) SECURE 2.0 Catch-Up (Age 60–63) Deadline 401(k) / 403(b) $23,500 +$7,500 = $31,000 +$11,250 = $34,750 December 31 Traditional / Roth IRA $7,000 +$1,000 = $8,000 N/A April 15 (tax filing deadline) SEP-IRA 25% of net self-employment income, up to $70,000 N/A N/A April 15 (+ extensions) HSA (self-only) $4,300 +$1,000 N/A April 15 (tax filing deadline) HSA (family) $8,550 +$1,000 N/A April 15 (tax filing deadline)

The critical distinction: 401(k) contributions must be made by December 31 — your employer's payroll system processes them, so practically speaking, your last contribution opportunity is your final paycheck of the year. If you realize in January that you under-contributed, you cannot make a prior-year 401(k) contribution. IRAs and HSAs can be contributed to all the way through April 15 (or the extended filing deadline for SEP-IRAs if you file an extension).

This means: - You can make your 2025 IRA contribution any time from January 1, 2025, through April 15, 2026 - But your 2025 401(k) contribution must clear payroll by December 31, 2025

The 401(k) Deadline Is Real — And Often Missed

Many people intend to increase their 401(k) contribution toward year-end and forget. Set a calendar reminder for October 1 to review your year-to-date 401(k) contributions and adjust your payroll election if you haven't maxed out. You have about 3 pay periods in Q4 — do the math now rather than in December.

docs.title: Retirement Plan Design
docs.text:
Choosing which retirement account to prioritize is one of the highest-leverage financial decisions you make. The right answer depends on your current tax bracket, your expected retirement tax bracket, your employer match situation, and your access to an HSA. Here is the general priority sequence that maximizes tax efficiency for most working adults:

The Optimal Retirement Contribution Order:

  1. 401(k) up to the employer match — This is a 50–100% immediate return on investment. Never leave match money on the table.
  2. HSA to the maximum (if you have a qualifying high-deductible health plan) — The HSA is the only triple-tax-advantaged account: contributions are pre-tax, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. After age 65, withdrawals for any purpose are taxed as ordinary income — essentially a second Traditional IRA with no RMDs and a healthcare superpower.
  3. Roth IRA to the maximum (if income eligible; use backdoor Roth if over the limit) — After-tax contribution, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawals, no RMDs, maximum flexibility
  4. 401(k) from match up to the full limit — If you still have contribution capacity after the above
  5. Taxable brokerage account — No tax advantages but no restrictions either; prioritize tax-efficient index funds and ETFs here

docs.title: Diagram: Retirement Plan Optimizer
docs.text:
Retirement Plan Optimizer — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply

Bloom Verb: Optimize

Learning Objective: Given user-provided income, filing status, employer match, and account access, the learner can determine the optimal sequence and amount of retirement contributions to minimize current-year tax liability while maximizing long-term tax-advantaged growth.

Layout: Two-panel layout. Left panel contains inputs. Right panel shows a dynamic stacked bar chart ("Your Contribution Waterfall") and a summary table.

Inputs (left panel): - Filing status: dropdown (Single / Married Filing Jointly / Head of Household) - Gross annual income: number slider ($30,000 – $500,000, step \(1,000) - Employer 401(k) match: dropdown (None / 50% up to 3% / 100% up to 3% / 100% up to 6%) - Has HDHP / HSA access: toggle (Yes / No) - Age: number input (18–75) — unlocks catch-up contributions at 50 and 60 - Other deductions (mortgage interest, SALT, etc.): number slider (\)0 – \(30,000) - Current 401(k) YTD contribution: number input (\)0 – \(34,750) - Current IRA YTD contribution: number input (\)0 – $8,000)

Outputs (right panel): - Stacked horizontal bar labeled "Your Contribution Waterfall" showing: - Step 1: 401(k) to employer match (green) - Step 2: HSA contribution (teal) - Step 3: Roth IRA contribution (blue) - Step 4: Additional 401(k) to max (dark green) - Remaining income available (gray) - Summary table showing: - Recommended contribution per account - Estimated tax savings (Traditional contributions × marginal rate) - Effective tax rate before and after contributions - Whether itemized or standard deduction applies - Color-coded alert banners: - "You're leaving $X in employer match on the table" (red) if 401(k) below match threshold - "HSA available — consider maximizing for triple tax benefit" (teal) if HSA not maxed - "You have $X of room in your current bracket" (blue) showing Roth conversion opportunity

Behavior: - All outputs update in real time as sliders/inputs change - If age >= 50: catch-up limits automatically applied to 401(k) and IRA - If age 60–63: SECURE 2.0 enhanced 401(k) catch-up ($34,750) applies - Marginal rate is computed from taxable income after standard/itemized deduction and all Traditional contributions - The optimizer suggests Traditional vs. Roth IRA based on current marginal rate: Traditional if 22%+, Roth if 12% or below, "either could work" if 22% exactly

Colors: - 401(k) match segment: #2E7D32 (forest green) - HSA segment: #00838F (teal) - Roth IRA segment: #1565C0 (deep blue) - Additional 401(k) segment: #558B2F (olive green) - Remaining income: #E0E0E0 (light gray) - Background: white; accent color #37474F (dark blue-gray) - Alert banners: red (#C62828), teal (#00695C), blue (#1565C0)

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

The retirement contribution framework above applies to most people, but it's not dogma. A few situations that alter the priority order:

  • High-income earners above the Roth IRA income limit ($150,000+ single, $236,000+ MFJ in 2025): Use the backdoor Roth IRA — contribute to a nondeductible Traditional IRA and immediately convert to Roth. If you have existing Traditional IRA balances, the "pro-rata rule" complicates this; consult a tax professional before executing.
  • Self-employed without access to a 401(k): A SEP-IRA allows contributions up to 25% of net self-employment income (capped at $70,000 for 2025). Solo 401(k)s allow even higher contributions and have Roth options; they require more paperwork to establish.
  • State income taxes: Some states don't recognize Roth conversion advantages or treat IRA distributions differently. If you live in a high-income-tax state, the Traditional vs. Roth calculus changes.

docs.title: Year-End Tax Checklist
docs.text:
Use this checklist every November and December to close out your tax year deliberately rather than reactively. Many of these items have hard December 31 deadlines.

Withholding and Estimated Payments

  • Review year-to-date federal withholding against estimated tax liability — are you on track to meet safe harbor?
  • If under-withheld, submit a new W-4 to payroll or make a Q4 estimated payment by January 15
  • Check state withholding separately — state safe harbor rules vary

Retirement Accounts

  • Confirm your 401(k) / 403(b) YTD contributions — do you want to increase before year-end?
  • If age 50+: confirm catch-up contributions are being made
  • If age 60–63: confirm SECURE 2.0 enhanced catch-up ($34,750 limit) is being applied
  • Execute any planned Roth conversions before December 31
  • Open a Solo 401(k) before December 31 if self-employed (the account must exist by year-end; contributions can follow)

Deductions and Credits

  • Determine whether to itemize or take the standard deduction for this year
  • If itemizing: make charitable contributions before December 31; get acknowledgment letters for gifts over $250
  • If bunching deductions: confirm this is your "itemize" year or your "standard" year and act accordingly
  • Prepay January mortgage payment or property tax installment if it pushes you over the itemizing threshold (subject to SALT cap)
  • Pay any outstanding state estimated taxes before December 31 (deductible in current year under SALT cap)

Investment Accounts

  • Review taxable accounts for unrealized losses — consider harvesting before December 31
  • Confirm you haven't violated the wash-sale rule (you cannot buy substantially identical securities within 30 days before or after the sale)
  • Rebalance inside tax-advantaged accounts (no tax event inside an IRA or 401(k))
  • If you have a large capital gain this year, check if any losses can offset it

Income Timing

  • If self-employed: should you defer December invoices to January, or accelerate them?
  • Review any pending bonuses — some employers allow deferral to next year
  • If you're near the ACA premium tax credit income cliff, check your MAGI before any Roth conversion or income acceleration

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Your W-4 is a tool, not a form you fill out once and forget. Update it when your life changes and review it annually using the IRS Withholding Estimator.

  • A large tax refund is not a win — it is an interest-free loan to the IRS. Target withholding that gets you close to zero owed or a small refund.

  • Under-withholding triggers real penalties. Know the safe harbor rules: 90% of current-year liability or 100% of prior-year liability (110% if AGI exceeded $150,000).

  • Tax planning happens on a calendar. The four quarterly estimated tax dates, the December 31 contribution and conversion deadline, and the April 15 IRA/HSA contribution deadline are the skeleton of your annual tax plan.

  • Bracket management is the art of deciding which year income lands in. The brackets don't change; your choices about timing do.

  • Roth conversions are most valuable when your current rate is lower than your expected future rate. Size conversions to fill your current bracket without crossing into the next.

  • Bunching deductions — especially charitable giving via a Donor-Advised Fund — can generate meaningful additional deductions without changing how much you give.

  • The retirement contribution priority order: 401(k) to match → HSA to max → Roth IRA to max → 401(k) to max → taxable account. This sequence optimizes tax efficiency for most people.

  • 401(k) contributions must clear payroll by December 31. IRA and HSA contributions have until April 15. Knowing the difference prevents a costly missed deadline.

  • Year-end is not just about wrapping up — it's your best planning window. Use the checklist above every November to identify actions before hard deadlines arrive.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Pull up your most recent pay stub and find your year-to-date federal income tax withheld. Divide by your YTD gross wages to find your current effective withholding rate.

  2. Run the IRS Tax Withholding Estimator at irs.gov/W4app with your projected full-year income. If your projected liability differs from projected withholding by more than $500, submit a new W-4 to your employer this week.

  3. Log in to your 401(k) account and check your YTD contributions. If you're not on pace to maximize your employer match, increase your deferral percentage now — even a 1–2% increase for the rest of the year adds up.

  4. Determine whether you'll itemize or take the standard deduction this year. Add up your likely SALT, mortgage interest, and charitable contributions. If you're within $3,000 of the standard deduction, evaluate whether bunching deductions this year or next year is worthwhile.

  5. Add the four quarterly estimated tax dates to your calendar (April 15, June 16, September 15, January 15) and the December 31 retirement deadline.

  6. Open or confirm your IRA — if you haven't contributed for this tax year yet, you still have until April 15 of next year. Decide Traditional vs. Roth based on your current marginal rate: Roth if you're in the 12% bracket, Traditional if 24%+, and consider both or a CPA's input at 22%.

  7. Schedule a November "tax review" on your calendar to work through the Year-End Tax Checklist before December 31 deadlines hit.

  8. If you have a Traditional IRA and expect lower income this year than in retirement, run a rough Roth conversion calculation: how much can you convert without crossing the next bracket boundary? Even converting \(10,000–\)20,000 per year during low-income years can significantly reduce future RMD-driven tax burdens.

docs.title: 6. Tax Forms Deep Dive
docs.text: title: Chapter 6: Tax Forms Deep Dive description: Box-by-box guide to the W-2, all major 1099 variants, Form 1040 walkthrough, and deep dives into Schedules A and SE plus Forms 2441, 8606, and 5329. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-21 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter demystifies every common tax form you'll encounter. You'll get a box-by-box explanation of the W-2, plain-English breakdowns of the 1099 variants you're most likely to receive (NEC, INT, R, G, K), a line-by-line walkthrough of the Form 1040, and deep dives into Schedule A (itemized deductions) and Schedule SE (self-employment tax). Also covered: Form 2441 (child care), Form 8606 (nondeductible IRA contributions), and Form 5329 (retirement penalties). Note: investment and rental property forms are covered in Chapters 7 and 8 respectively.

docs.text:

  13. Form 2441 Child Care Credit
  14. Form 8606 Nondeductible IRA
  15. Form 5329 Retirement Penalties

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 3: Legal Tax Reduction Strategies
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes
  • Chapter 5: Year-Round Tax Planning

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Tax forms are the IRS's way of collecting the facts about your financial life in a standardized format. Once you understand what each box means and where each number flows, you stop feeling like you're filling out a mystery document and start feeling like you're just transcribing information you already know. This chapter is your decoder ring. We'll work through the forms in a logical order — starting with the documents your employer sends you, then the 1099s that arrive from banks, clients, and brokers, and finally the core filing forms themselves.

Think of the W-2 and 1099s as raw ingredients. Form 1040 is the recipe. The schedules are what you add when your financial life gets more complex. By the end of this chapter, you'll be able to pick up any of these forms, know exactly what you're looking at, and know precisely where that number belongs on your return.

docs.title: Part 1: The W-2 — Your Employer's Annual Report on You
W-2 Box by Box Guide
docs.text:
Your employer must mail your W-2 by January 31. If you worked for multiple employers during 2025, you'll get one from each. The form looks dense, but most boxes are either straightforward or irrelevant to most filers. Here's what actually matters:

Box Label What It Means 1 Wages, tips, other compensation Your taxable wages — already reduced by pre-tax 401(k), health insurance, FSA, and HSA contributions 2 Federal income tax withheld What your employer sent to the IRS on your behalf 3 Social Security wages Wages subject to SS tax — may be higher than Box 1 (some pre-tax deductions reduce Box 1 but not Box 3) 4 Social Security tax withheld Always 6.2% of Box 3, up to the SS wage base ($176,100 for 2025) 5 Medicare wages Wages subject to Medicare tax — usually same as or higher than Box 3 6 Medicare tax withheld 1.45% of Box 5 (plus 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax if you hit \(200k single/\)250k MFJ) 12 Coded amounts See Box 12 table below — several important items live here 14 Other Informational items — see Box 14 section below 15–17 State boxes State wages and withholding — flows to your state return

Box 1 is the single most important number on your W-2. It's what you report on line 1a of Form 1040. If you had pre-tax deductions — a traditional 401(k), a health insurance premium, an FSA or HSA — your Box 1 is already lower than your gross salary. That's intentional and correct. You don't get to deduct those items again on your return.

Check Your Box 3 and Box 4

If you switched jobs mid-year, each employer withholds Social Security tax independently. If combined Box 3 wages across all W-2s exceeded $176,100 in 2025 and combined Box 4 withholding exceeded $10,918.20, you overpaid Social Security tax. Claim the excess as a credit on Schedule 3, line 11. The IRS will refund it — but only if you catch it.

docs.title: W-2 Box 12 Codes
docs.text:
Box 12 uses single or double-letter codes to report amounts that affect your tax return in specific ways. Your employer can report up to four items in Box 12 on a single W-2.

Code What It Represents Tax Implication D Traditional 401(k) contributions Pre-tax; reduces Box 1. Informational — don't deduct again. E 403(b) contributions (teachers, nonprofits) Same as D G 457(b) contributions (government employees) Same as D W Employer HSA contributions Excluded from income. Flows to Form 8889. DD Cost of employer-sponsored health coverage Informational only — not taxable, not deductible AA Roth 401(k) contributions After-tax; does not reduce Box 1. Reported for record-keeping. C Taxable cost of group-term life over $50,000 Added to Box 1 — imputed income P Excludable moving expense reimbursements Active military only S SIMPLE IRA contributions Similar to Code D

The codes you'll most commonly see are D (or E or G), W, DD, and AA. If you see Code D for $8,500, that's your traditional 401(k) contribution — it already reduced your Box 1, so you're done. If you see Code AA for $5,000, that's your Roth 401(k) — it did NOT reduce Box 1, and you'll never pay tax on those earnings in retirement.

docs.title: W-2 Box 14 Codes
docs.text:
Box 14 is a catch-all that employers use to communicate information that doesn't fit elsewhere. It has no standardized coding system — employers can label entries however they like. Common entries include:

  • Union dues — generally deductible only if you itemize and your state allows it (federal no longer allows misc. itemized deductions)
  • State SDI (State Disability Insurance) — California, New Jersey, and other states withhold this. It counts toward your state and local taxes (SALT) on Schedule A, subject to the $10,000 cap.
  • PUCC (Personal Use of Company Car) — the taxable value of personal miles driven in a company vehicle. This amount is already included in Box 1.
  • Imputed income — domestic partner health benefits, life insurance over $50,000 (though that's technically Box 12, Code C). Also already in Box 1.
  • Educational assistance — amounts over $5,250 may appear here and may already be included in Box 1.

Box 14 Is Usually Just FYI

Most Box 14 entries are informational or already captured elsewhere on your W-2. Read the label carefully, but don't panic. If you see "SDI" and a dollar amount, that number might be deductible on Schedule A as a state tax — add it to your other state/local taxes, subject to the $10,000 SALT cap.

docs.title: 1099-NEC Explained
docs.text:
The 1099-NEC (Nonemployee Compensation) is what businesses send you when they paid you $600 or more for services rendered as an independent contractor. "NEC" stands for Nonemployee Compensation. If you freelance, consult, do gig work, or provide any paid services outside of a traditional W-2 employment relationship, you'll receive this form.

The key number is Box 1: Nonemployee Compensation. That amount flows to Schedule C (Profit or Loss from Business), where you subtract your business expenses to arrive at net profit. That net profit is then subject to both income tax and self-employment tax (covered in the Schedule SE section below). You'll receive a 1099-NEC from each business that paid you $600 or more in 2025. But here's the critical point: even if a client paid you only $400 and didn't send a 1099-NEC, that income is still taxable and you must still report it.

No 1099 Doesn't Mean No Tax

The $600 threshold only determines who has to send you the form. You owe tax on every dollar of self-employment income, regardless of whether you received a 1099-NEC. If you earned $400 doing freelance design work and no form arrived in January, you still report $400 on Schedule C.

docs.title: 1099-MISC vs 1099-NEC
docs.text:
The IRS split what used to be a single 1099-MISC into two forms in 2020, and people still mix them up. Here's the clean distinction:

Form Used For Key Box 1099-NEC Payments to independent contractors for services Box 1: Nonemployee Compensation 1099-MISC Everything else — rents, prizes, awards, medical payments, crop insurance Box 3: Other Income; Box 6: Medical payments; Box 10: Crop insurance

If you won a prize at a conference ($500 Amazon gift card for best pitch), that's a 1099-MISC Box 3, not a 1099-NEC. If you rented your building to a business and received $10,000 in rent, that's 1099-MISC Box 1. If a business paid you $5,000 to redesign their website, that's 1099-NEC. The practical rule: freelance work and services go on the NEC. Almost everything else uses the MISC.

docs.title: 1099-INT Explained
docs.text:
Banks, credit unions, and brokers send the 1099-INT when you earned $10 or more in interest during 2025. The form has two lines you need to care about:

  • Box 1: Interest Income — taxable interest from savings accounts, CDs, money market accounts, and most bonds. This flows directly to Form 1040, line 2b (or Schedule B if total interest exceeds $1,500).
  • Box 8: Tax-Exempt Interest — interest from municipal bonds. This is NOT taxable federally, but you must still report it on line 2a of Form 1040. Why? Because it affects certain calculations like your Modified AGI for the purpose of determining Roth IRA eligibility and the taxation of Social Security benefits.

Other boxes on the 1099-INT (bond premium, market discount, investment expenses) are relevant if you're trading bonds actively — those are covered in Chapter 7.

docs.title: 1099-R Explained
docs.text:
The 1099-R reports distributions from retirement accounts — traditional IRAs, 401(k)s, 403(b)s, pensions, and annuities. If money came out of any tax-advantaged retirement account in 2025, you got one of these.

The most important boxes:

  • Box 1: Gross Distribution — the total amount distributed
  • Box 2a: Taxable Amount — the portion subject to income tax (may differ from Box 1 if you made nondeductible contributions — that's where Form 8606 comes in)
  • Box 4: Federal Income Tax Withheld — what the custodian already sent to the IRS
  • Box 7: Distribution Code — this is the critical one; it tells the IRS why money left the account
Box 7 Code Meaning Default Penalty? 1 Early distribution, no known exception 10% early withdrawal penalty applies 2 Early distribution, exception applies No penalty (disability, substantially equal payments, etc.) 7 Normal distribution (age 59½ or older) No penalty G Direct rollover Not taxable, no penalty H Direct rollover from Roth account Not taxable, no penalty

If you see Code 1, the 10% penalty will be calculated on Form 5329 (covered below). If you rolled over a 401(k) from an old job to an IRA and received Code G, you owe nothing — but you still need to report the 1099-R on your return so the IRS can match it.

docs.title: 1099-G Explained
docs.text:
The 1099-G covers government payments. The two boxes most people encounter:

  • Box 1: Unemployment Compensation — fully taxable at the federal level. Every dollar of unemployment benefits you received in 2025 goes on Schedule 1, line 7, and ultimately lands on your 1040. There is no exclusion this year — unlike the temporary COVID-era break in 2021.
  • Box 2: State or Local Income Tax Refunds — this is only taxable if you itemized deductions in the prior year AND deducted those state taxes. If you took the standard deduction last year, your state refund is not taxable. This is the "tax benefit rule" — you only owe tax on the refund to the extent you got a deduction for it.

Withholding from Unemployment

You can request federal income tax withholding from unemployment benefits using Form W-4V, withholding 10% flat. Most people who don't do this are unpleasantly surprised at tax time. If you were on unemployment in 2025 and didn't withhold, budget for an April bill or make a Q4 estimated payment.

docs.title: 1099-K Reporting Rules
docs.text:
The 1099-K reports payment card and third-party network transactions. For 2025, the threshold is $600 in gross payments — meaning PayPal, Venmo (for goods/services), eBay, Etsy, Stripe, and similar platforms must send you a 1099-K if they processed $600 or more to you during the year.

This is a source of significant confusion. The 1099-K reports gross receipts — it does not subtract your costs, refunds, or fees. If you sold used furniture on Facebook Marketplace for $800 and paid $500 for it originally, you don't owe tax on $800. You owe tax on the $300 gain (or nothing, if you sold it for less than you paid). The 1099-K is just a reporting trigger, not a tax bill.

  • If the payments were for a business (freelance, side hustle, selling goods), report on Schedule C.
  • If the payments were from selling personal items at a loss (used clothes, furniture you paid more for), you have no taxable gain — but document your original purchase prices.
  • If you received money via Venmo for splitting a dinner, that is not taxable income. Request that friends designate it as "personal" — personal transfers don't trigger 1099-K.

Don't Ignore a 1099-K

The IRS receives a copy of your 1099-K directly from the payment processor. If the amount on your 1099-K doesn't appear somewhere on your return, an automated notice is likely. Even if you owe no tax (because you sold items at a loss), you need to show your math on your return so the IRS can match it.

docs.title: 1040 Line by Line Guide
docs.text:
Form 1040 is two pages and approximately 79 lines. It doesn't do calculations — it aggregates numbers from your W-2s, 1099s, and supporting schedules into a single running total that yields your tax liability or refund. Here's the logical flow:

Page 1 — Income and Adjustments:

  • Lines 1a–1z — Wages, salaries, tips. Line 1a is your W-2 Box 1 total. Other lines cover household employee wages, tip income, scholarship income, etc.
  • Lines 2a–2b — Tax-exempt interest (2a, informational) and taxable interest (2b, from 1099-INT Box 1)
  • Line 3b — Ordinary dividends (from 1099-DIV — Chapter 7)
  • Line 4b — IRA distributions (taxable portion from 1099-R)
  • Line 5b — Pension and annuity income (taxable portion from 1099-R)
  • Line 6b — Social Security benefits (taxable portion, 0–85% depending on combined income)
  • Line 7 — Capital gain or loss (from Schedule D — Chapter 7)
  • Line 8 — Additional income from Schedule 1 (self-employment income, alimony, gambling winnings, unemployment, etc.)
  • Line 9 — Total income (sum of all above)
  • Line 10 — Adjustments to income from Schedule 1, Part II (student loan interest, HSA deductions, self-employed health insurance, etc.)
  • Line 11 — Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) = Line 9 minus Line 10. This is the most important number on your return.

Page 2 — Tax Calculation and Credits:

  • Line 12 — Standard deduction OR itemized deductions (Schedule A)
  • Line 13 — Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction, if applicable
  • Line 15 — Taxable Income = AGI minus deductions
  • Line 16 — Tax (from the tax tables or tax computation worksheets)
  • Line 17 — Alternative Minimum Tax (from Form 6251, if applicable)
  • Line 18 — Total tax before credits
  • Lines 19–24 — Non-refundable credits (child tax credit, education credits, etc.)
  • Line 25 — Federal withholding from W-2s and 1099s
  • Line 26 — Estimated tax payments made
  • Line 27 — Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC), if eligible
  • Line 33 — Total payments
  • Line 37 or 38 — Amount you owe OR refund

The formula is clean:

\[ \text{Tax Owed} = \text{Tax on Taxable Income} - \text{Credits} - \text{Withholding} - \text{Estimated Payments} \]

If the result is negative, you get a refund. If positive, you owe the difference by April 15.

docs.title: Diagram: 1040 Tax Flow Simulator
docs.text:
1040 Tax Flow Simulator — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: The learner can trace a dollar of income from a W-2 through the Form 1040 structure — applying the standard deduction, reading from the tax brackets, applying a basic credit, and arriving at a net tax owed or refund — using realistic 2025 numbers.

Layout: The sim has two panels side by side on desktop, stacked on mobile. Left panel: input controls (sliders and dropdowns). Right panel: a vertical "waterfall" diagram showing how income flows down through the 1040 calculation, with each row labeled with the corresponding 1040 line number.

Inputs (left panel): - Wages (W-2 Box 1): slider from $20,000 to $200,000, default $65,000, step $1,000 - Filing status: dropdown — Single, Married Filing Jointly, Head of Household - Federal tax withheld: slider from $0 to $40,000, default $8,000, step $500 - Other income (1099s, etc.): numeric input, default $0 - Pre-tax deductions (reduces Box 1 already, informational only): numeric display - Child Tax Credit eligible children: integer 0–4, default 0

Outputs (right panel waterfall): Each step is a labeled row with the dollar amount on the right: 1. Gross Wages (line 1a) 2. Other Income (line 8) 3. Total Income (line 9) [subtotal bar] 4. Adjustments to Income (line 10) — shown as \(0 for simplicity 5. AGI (line 11) [highlighted subtotal bar] 6. Standard Deduction (line 12) — auto-populated from filing status (\)15,000 / $30,000 / $22,500) 7. Taxable Income (line 15) [highlighted] 8. Income Tax (line 16) — computed from 2025 tax brackets 9. Child Tax Credit (lines 19–24) — $2,000 per child, non-refundable 10. Tax After Credits (line 18 adjusted) 11. Federal Withholding (line 25) 12. Net Tax Owed / Refund (line 37 or 38) [large colored result: red for owe, green for refund]

Behavior: - All values update in real-time as sliders/inputs change. - The waterfall diagram uses arrows showing flow downward. - Negative values (deductions, credits, withholding) shown in a muted color with a minus sign. - Net result row uses bold text; green background if refund, red/orange background if owe. - Tax bracket calculation uses 2025 rates: 10%, 12%, 22%, 24%, 32%, 35%, 37% applied to taxable income per filing status. - Tooltip on each row explains what that line represents on the real Form 1040.

Colors: - Income rows: dark teal (#2E7D6C) - Deduction rows: slate blue (#4A5568) - Tax rows: dark orange (#C05621) - Credit rows: dark green (#276749) - Result row: green (#22543D) for refund, red (#9B2335) for owe - Background: light gray (#F7FAFC) - Waterfall bars: proportional width to maximum value in view

Implementation: HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

docs.title: Part 4: Schedules That Do the Heavy Lifting
Schedule A — Itemized Deductions
docs.text:
Schedule A is where you list deductions to replace the standard deduction — but only if your itemized total exceeds the standard deduction amount for your filing status. For 2025, those thresholds are $15,000 (Single), $30,000 (Married Filing Jointly), and $22,500 (Head of Household). You'll only itemize if your deductions clear those bars.

The major categories on Schedule A:

  • Medical and Dental Expenses — only the amount exceeding 7.5% of your AGI is deductible. If your AGI is $80,000, only medical expenses above $6,000 count. Most people under 65 with typical AGI won't clear this floor.
  • State and Local Taxes (SALT) — capped at **\(10,000** total (\)5,000 if Married Filing Separately). This includes state income tax withheld (or sales tax in no-income-tax states), local income tax, and property taxes. The $10,000 cap remains in place for 2025.
  • Mortgage Interest — interest on acquisition debt up to $750,000 (for loans after December 15, 2017). Home equity interest is deductible only if the loan was used to buy, build, or substantially improve the home.
  • Charitable Contributions — cash donations (up to 60% of AGI), non-cash donations (fair market value, Form 8283 if over $500), and qualified conservation easements.

The math test for itemizing:

\[ \text{Itemize if: } (\text{SALT} + \text{Mortgage Interest} + \text{Charitable} + \text{Medical excess}) > \text{Standard Deduction} \]

For most W-2 earners in moderate cost-of-living areas, the $30,000 MFJ standard deduction is hard to beat. But homeowners with large mortgages in high-tax states often clear it easily. The practical exercise: add up your property tax, state income tax (capped at $10k total), mortgage interest statements (Form 1098), and charitable receipts every January.

SALT Cap in Action

Maya lives in New Jersey. She paid $8,500 in NJ state income tax and $6,200 in property tax — a combined $14,700 in state and local taxes. Her SALT deduction is capped at $10,000. She also paid $12,000 in mortgage interest. Her itemized total is $22,000. She files as Single. Her standard deduction is $15,000. She should itemize — she saves an extra $7,000 in deductions over the standard amount.

docs.title: Schedule SE — Self-Employment Tax
docs.text:
If you have net self-employment income of $400 or more, you must file Schedule SE. This form calculates the self-employment (SE) tax — the equivalent of both the employee and employer sides of Social Security and Medicare taxes that a regular employee splits with their employer.

The formula in two steps:

Step 1 — Net SE Income: [ \text{Net SE Income} = \text{Net Profit from Schedule C} \times 0.9235 ]

The 0.9235 factor (which equals 1 minus 0.0765) adjusts for the fact that employees only pay half of FICA taxes. You get a comparable adjustment as a self-employed person.

Step 2 — SE Tax: [ \text{SE Tax} = \text{Net SE Income} \times 0.153 ]

The 15.3% rate is 12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare. Social Security applies only up to the wage base ($176,100 in 2025).

Example: You freelanced and netted $60,000 on Schedule C.

[ \text{Net SE Income} = $60{,}000 \times 0.9235 = $55{,}410 ] [ \text{SE Tax} = $55{,}410 \times 0.153 = $8{,}478 ]

That $8,478 goes on Schedule 2, line 4, and flows to Form 1040, line 17. But here's the good news: you deduct half of your SE tax as an adjustment to income on Schedule 1, line 15. That deduction reduces your AGI — and your income tax bill — by roughly $4,239 (half of $8,478), though not your SE tax itself.

docs.title: Part 5: Specialized Forms You Need to Know
Form 2441 — Child and Dependent Care Credit
docs.text:
If you paid someone to watch your child (under age 13) or a dependent who can't care for themselves so that you (and your spouse, if married) could work or look for work, you may qualify for the Child and Dependent Care Credit. This is not the Child Tax Credit — it's a separate credit specifically for care expenses.

Key rules for 2025:

  • Maximum eligible expenses: $3,000 for one qualifying person, $6,000 for two or more
  • Credit rate: 20%–35% of eligible expenses, depending on your AGI. The rate phases down as income rises. At AGI above $43,000, the rate is a flat 20%.
  • Maximum credit: $600 (one person at 20%) to $1,050 (one person at 35%) or $1,200 to $2,100 (two or more)
  • Earned income requirement: Both spouses must have earned income (wages, self-employment) — unless one is a full-time student or disabled
  • Dependent Care FSA coordination: If your employer offers a Dependent Care FSA and you contributed pre-tax dollars, those dollars reduce the eligible expenses you can claim on Form 2441. You can't double-dip.

The employer's Dependent Care Assistance Program (DCAP/FSA) is usually the better deal for most people — you get a pre-tax reduction at your marginal rate rather than a 20% credit. But if your care expenses exceed your FSA limit ($5,000 for MFJ), you can claim the credit on the remaining eligible expenses.

AGI Range Credit Rate Max Credit (2 dependents) Up to $15,000 35% $2,100 \(15,001–\)43,000 Phases down from 35% to 20% \(1,200–\)2,100 Over $43,000 20% $1,200

docs.title: Form 8606 — Nondeductible IRA Contributions
docs.text:
Form 8606 is one of the most underused and most important forms in the tax code. You file it whenever you make a nondeductible contribution to a traditional IRA — meaning you contributed after-tax dollars because you were covered by a workplace retirement plan and your income was too high to deduct the contribution.

Why does this matter enormously? Because without Form 8606, the IRS assumes all money in your traditional IRA is pre-tax. When you take distributions in retirement, they'll tax the entire amount. But if you tracked your nondeductible basis with Form 8606, only the earnings are taxable — not the principal you already paid tax on.

Form 8606 also drives the backdoor Roth IRA strategy:

  1. Contribute $7,000 (or $8,000 if 50+) to a traditional IRA (nondeductible — file Form 8606 Part I)
  2. Convert the traditional IRA to a Roth IRA (report on Form 8606 Part II)
  3. If done with no other pre-tax IRA balances, the conversion is tax-free

The Pro-Rata Rule and Form 8606

If you have a mix of pre-tax and after-tax dollars in traditional IRAs when you do a Roth conversion, you can't just convert the after-tax portion. The IRS requires you to treat all traditional IRA money as one pool. The taxable fraction of any conversion equals: pre-tax IRA balance ÷ total IRA balance. Form 8606 Part II does this calculation for you — but get it wrong and you'll pay tax twice on the same dollars.

Form 8606 accumulates over years. Every nondeductible contribution you make gets added to your "basis" — tracked in Part I, line 14. This carryforward continues until all your nondeductible contributions are either converted to Roth or distributed. File Form 8606 every single year you make a nondeductible IRA contribution, even if you owe no additional tax. Losing track of your basis is expensive.

docs.title: Form 5329 — Additional Taxes on Retirement Accounts
docs.text:
Form 5329 is the IRS's mechanism for collecting penalties related to retirement accounts. You'll encounter it in two main situations:

Situation 1 — Early Withdrawal Penalty (Part I)

If you took money out of a traditional IRA or 401(k) before age 59½ (or 55 for a qualified plan if you separated from service), a 10% penalty applies on top of ordinary income tax. The penalty is calculated on Form 5329, Part I.

\[ \text{Early Withdrawal Penalty} = \text{Taxable Distribution} \times 0.10 \]

However, there are exceptions that zero out the penalty even if your 1099-R shows Code 1 (early, no known exception). You claim the exception on Form 5329 by entering the exception code and the exempt amount:

  • Exception 01: Death
  • Exception 02: Disability
  • Exception 03: Substantially Equal Periodic Payments (SEPP / 72(t))
  • Exception 05: Qualified higher education expenses (IRA only)
  • Exception 09: Health insurance premiums while unemployed (IRA only)
  • Exception 10: First-time home purchase (IRA only, up to $10,000 lifetime)
  • Exception 12: Qualified disaster distribution (if applicable)

Situation 2 — Excess Contribution Penalty (Part III)

If you contributed more than the annual limit to an IRA (or Roth IRA) in 2025 ($7,000, or $8,000 if age 50+), the excess is hit with a 6% excise tax per year until it's corrected.

\[ \text{Excess Contribution Penalty} = \text{Excess Amount} \times 0.06 \]

This penalty compounds — you pay 6% every year the excess remains in the account. The fix: withdraw the excess contribution plus earnings attributable to it before the tax filing deadline (including extensions). If you catch it in time, no penalty applies. If you don't, Form 5329 calculates the damage.

Excess Contribution Scenario

Jordan contributed $7,000 to a Roth IRA in 2025 but her income turned out to be too high — she was above the Roth IRA phase-out limit. The entire $7,000 is an excess contribution. If she doesn't remove it by April 15, 2026 (or October 15 with an extension), she owes $420 (6% × $7,000) for 2025, and another $420 for 2026 if it's still there. She should withdraw the $7,000 plus any earnings now. Or, if eligible, recharacterize it as a traditional IRA contribution.

docs.title: Putting It All Together
docs.text:
Every document covered in this chapter connects to a specific line on Form 1040. Here's the complete flow at a glance:

Source Document Key Box Where It Goes on 1040 W-2 Box 1 Line 1a W-2 Box 2 Line 25a (withholding) 1099-NEC Box 1 Schedule C → Schedule 1, Line 3 → Line 8 1099-MISC Box 3 Schedule 1, Line 8z (other income) 1099-INT Box 1 Line 2b 1099-INT Box 8 Line 2a (tax-exempt, informational) 1099-R Box 2a Line 4b or 5b 1099-G Box 1 Unemployment Schedule 1, Line 7 → Line 8 1099-G Box 2 State refund Schedule 1, Line 1 → Line 8 (if taxable) 1099-K Gross receipts Schedule C (business) or capital gain/loss Schedule A Total itemized deductions Line 12 (if greater than standard deduction) Schedule SE SE tax Schedule 2, Line 4 → Line 17 Form 2441 Child care credit Schedule 3, Line 2 → Line 20 Form 8606 Taxable IRA conversion Line 4b (IRA distributions, taxable amount) Form 5329 Additional taxes Schedule 2, Line 8 → Line 17

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Your W-2 Box 1 is already reduced by pre-tax contributions. Check Box 12 for the codes that explain what was deducted.
  • Box 14 is informational — read the label, but most entries are already captured elsewhere or simply FYI.
  • The 1099-NEC (not the 1099-MISC) is the form for freelance and contractor pay. All service income over $600 triggers one; all service income under $600 is still taxable.
  • 1099-K reports gross payment volume from platforms — it's a reporting trigger, not a tax bill. You must still show the IRS where those gross receipts appear on your return.
  • Form 1040 has one job: aggregate all your income, subtract your deductions and credits, and compare the resulting tax to what you already paid. The difference is your refund or your bill.
  • Schedule A is worth using only when your real deductions — SALT (capped at $10k), mortgage interest, and charitable giving — exceed your standard deduction.
  • Schedule SE calculates your self-employment tax at 15.3% of 92.35% of net profit. Half is deductible against income tax.
  • Form 2441 gives you a credit for dependent care expenses — but coordinate with any Dependent Care FSA you used.
  • Form 8606 tracks your nondeductible IRA basis for life. Every missed filing year costs you.
  • Form 5329 handles early withdrawal penalties (10%) and excess contribution penalties (6%). Exceptions to the early withdrawal penalty must be claimed proactively on this form.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Gather every tax document that has arrived so far — W-2s, all 1099s. Create a checklist matching each document to the section in this chapter.
  2. Open your W-2 and verify that Box 1 is lower than your gross salary by the amount of your pre-tax 401(k) and health insurance contributions. If the math doesn't work, call your payroll department.
  3. Check Box 12 on your W-2. If you see Code D or AA, verify the amount matches your 401(k) statement for the year.
  4. If you received a 1099-NEC, list every business expense you paid related to that work. You'll need those for Schedule C.
  5. If you received a 1099-K, document the original cost of any items you sold. Gather purchase receipts now before you forget.
  6. Run the 1040 Tax Flow Simulator above with your actual numbers. Notice where your biggest tax drivers are — wages, self-employment income, or other sources.
  7. Do the Schedule A math: add your property tax + state income tax (capped at $10,000) + Form 1098 mortgage interest + charitable receipts. Compare to your standard deduction. Decide whether to itemize.
  8. If you made any IRA contributions for 2025 that were nondeductible, confirm you have (or will file) Form 8606. If you're not sure whether your contributions were deductible, check the IRA deductibility worksheet in Publication 590-A.
  9. If you took an early distribution from a retirement account and your 1099-R shows Code 1, check the exception list in the Form 5329 section. If an exception applies, claim it — don't pay a penalty you don't owe.
  10. Note any forms in this chapter that apply to your situation for next tax year, and set a calendar reminder in December to verify your withholding covers your expected liability.

docs.title: 7. Investments & Capital Gains
docs.text: title: Chapter 7: Investments & Capital Gains Taxes description: Tax rules for stocks, bonds, mutual funds, ETFs, crypto, and stock options — including capital gains rates, cost basis methods, wash sale rule, tax-loss harvesting, and investment tax forms. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-21 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers the tax rules for anyone who holds stocks, bonds, mutual funds, ETFs, cryptocurrency, or stock options. You'll learn the difference between short-term and long-term capital gains rates, how qualified dividends are taxed, how to track cost basis using FIFO or specific identification, how the wash sale rule works (and how to avoid triggering it), and how to use tax-loss harvesting to offset gains. Also covered: cryptocurrency and NFT tax treatment, municipal bond exemptions, and incentive vs. non-qualified stock options. The investment-related forms — 1099-DIV, 1099-B, and Schedule B — are covered here alongside the concepts they document.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 24 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Short-Term Capital Gains Tax
  2. Long-Term Capital Gains Tax
  3. Capital Gains Tax Rates 2025
  4. Capital Loss Deduction Rules
  5. Capital Loss Carryforward
  6. Wash Sale Rule
  7. Qualified Dividends Tax Rate
  8. Ordinary Dividends Tax Rate
