docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 5: Year-Round Tax Planning
  • Chapter 6: Tax Forms Deep Dive

docs.title: The Core Idea: You're Taxed on the Profit, Not the Sale Price
docs.text:
When you sell an investment for more than you paid, the IRS wants a share of that profit. The profit itself is called a capital gain, and the tax you owe on it is the capital gains tax. Sounds simple enough — but the rate you pay depends heavily on how long you held the investment before selling. Get that timing wrong and you could pay two or three times as much tax as necessary.

The foundational formula is:

\[ \text{Capital Gain (or Loss)} = \text{Sale Proceeds} - \text{Cost Basis} \]

Your cost basis is what you originally paid for the asset, including commissions and fees. If you bought 100 shares of a stock at $40 per share, your cost basis is $4,000. If you later sell those shares for $6,000, you have a $2,000 capital gain. Whether that $2,000 is taxed at 10% or 23.8% depends on one critical factor: the holding period.

docs.title: Part 1: Short-Term vs. Long-Term Capital Gains
Short-Term Capital Gains Tax
docs.text:
If you sell an asset you've held for one year or less, the gain is short-term. Short-term capital gains are taxed as ordinary income — meaning they stack on top of your wages and get taxed at the same marginal rate as your salary. In 2025, that could be anywhere from 10% to 37% depending on your total income.

This is the IRS's way of discouraging quick trading in favor of long-term investing. If you bought a stock in January and sold it in October of the same year with a $5,000 profit, that $5,000 gets added to your W-2 income and taxed accordingly. For someone in the 24% bracket, that's $1,200 in tax. For someone in the 32% bracket, it's $1,600.

docs.title: Long-Term Capital Gains Tax
docs.text:
Hold the asset for more than one year — meaning at least one year and one day — and you qualify for long-term capital gains treatment. These rates are substantially lower than ordinary income rates and are one of the most valuable tax preferences in the entire tax code for individual investors.

The holding period clock starts the day after you buy the asset and ends on the day you sell it. Buy on March 5, 2024; sell on March 6, 2025 — that's long-term. Sell on March 5, 2025 — that's still short-term (you need that one extra day).

docs.title: Capital Gains Tax Rates 2025
docs.text:
Here are the 2025 long-term capital gains tax brackets. These are separate from the ordinary income brackets and are applied only to your long-term gains and qualified dividends.

Rate Single Filers Married Filing Jointly 0% Up to $48,350 Up to $96,700 15% $48,351 – $533,400 $96,701 – $600,050 20% Above $533,400 Above $600,050

The 0% Rate Is Real — and Often Overlooked

If your total taxable income (including the gain) falls below the 0% threshold, you owe zero federal tax on long-term capital gains. A retired couple with $60,000 in Social Security and pension income might still qualify for the 0% rate on gains if their taxable income is low enough. Run the numbers before assuming you owe anything.

In addition to the standard rates, high earners face a Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT) of 3.8% on the lesser of: (a) their net investment income, or (b) the amount by which their Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) exceeds $200,000 (Single) or $250,000 (MFJ). This means the effective top federal rate on capital gains can reach 23.8% (20% + 3.8%) for high-income taxpayers.

docs.title: Part 2: Capital Losses — When Investments Go Wrong
Capital Loss Deduction Rules
docs.text:
Losses happen. When you sell an investment for less than your cost basis, you have a capital loss. The good news: the IRS lets you use those losses to offset your gains — and if your losses exceed your gains, you can even deduct up to $3,000 per year against ordinary income (wages, salary, etc.).

The netting process works like this:

  1. First, short-term gains and losses are netted against each other.
  2. Then, long-term gains and losses are netted against each other.
  3. If both nets are the same sign (both gains, or both losses), you simply report each.
  4. If one is a net gain and the other is a net loss, they offset each other.
  5. Any remaining net capital loss can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income.

Capital Loss Offset in Action

You sold Stock A at a $8,000 short-term gain, Stock B at a $5,000 long-term gain, and Stock C at a $15,000 long-term loss. After netting: your $5,000 long-term gain is wiped out by part of the $15,000 loss, leaving a $10,000 net long-term loss. That wipes out the $8,000 short-term gain entirely, leaving a $2,000 net capital loss. You deduct $2,000 against ordinary income this year. Zero carryforward.

docs.title: Capital Loss Carryforward
docs.text:
If your total net capital loss exceeds $3,000 in a given year, you don't lose the excess. It carries forward indefinitely to future tax years. If you have a $25,000 net capital loss this year, you use $3,000 now and carry $22,000 forward. Next year, that $22,000 can offset future gains first, then up to another $3,000 against ordinary income — and so on until the carryforward is exhausted.

The carryforward preserves its character: a long-term loss carryforward remains long-term, and a short-term loss carryforward remains short-term. This matters because long-term losses first offset long-term gains (at the preferential rate), while short-term losses first offset short-term gains (taxed as ordinary income).

docs.title: Wash Sale Rule
docs.text:
The IRS figured out a long time ago that investors would love to sell a losing stock on December 31, claim the tax loss, and immediately repurchase the same stock on January 1 — maintaining their position while manufacturing a tax deduction. Congress closed this loophole with the wash sale rule.

A wash sale occurs when you sell a security at a loss and buy a "substantially identical" security within 30 days before or after the sale — a 61-day window total. If you trigger the wash sale rule, the loss is disallowed for the current year. Instead, the disallowed loss is added to the cost basis of the replacement shares, so you don't lose the loss permanently — you just defer it.

Common Wash Sale Traps

  • Selling a stock at a loss and buying it back within 30 days — obvious, but people still do it.
  • Selling a mutual fund and buying a nearly identical fund from the same fund family (if the IRS considers them "substantially identical," it's a wash sale).
  • Selling in a taxable account and buying the same security in an IRA within the 30-day window — this does trigger the wash sale, but the deferred loss is permanently lost because it can't be added to the IRA's basis.
  • Automatic dividend reinvestments that purchase shares within the 30-day window can create partial wash sales.

The fix is straightforward: wait 31 days before repurchasing the same security, or replace it with a similar but not identical investment. For example, if you sell an S&P 500 index fund at a loss, you can immediately buy a total market index fund and stay fully invested while preserving the loss deduction.

docs.title: Diagram: Wash Sale Rule Calculator
docs.text:
Wash Sale Rule Calculator — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Given a sale date, cost basis, sale price, repurchase date, and share counts, determine whether the 61-day wash sale window is triggered, calculate the disallowed and deductible loss, and compute the adjusted cost basis of replacement shares.

Layout: - Left panel (inputs): Sale date, shares sold, cost basis per share, sale price per share; repurchase date, shares bought, repurchase price per share - Right panel (outputs): Verdict badge (Wash Sale / Clean Sale / No Loss), SVG timeline showing the 61-day window with sale and repurchase date markers, results table with loss breakdown and adjusted basis calculation, educational info note

Inputs: - Sale date: date picker - Shares sold: number input - Cost basis per share: number input (dollars) - Sale price per share: number input (dollars) - Repurchase date: date picker - Shares bought: number input - Repurchase price per share: number input (dollars)

Outputs: - Verdict badge: color-coded (red for wash sale, green for clean sale, gray for no loss) - SVG timeline: 61-day window shaded in red, sale date as blue dot, repurchase date as red/green dot, date labels - Results table: realized gain/loss, days from sale, window status, disallowed loss, deductible loss, adjusted basis per replacement share, total adjusted basis - Info note: contextual explanation (IRA trap warning for wash sales; replacement security tip for clean sales)

Behavior: - Wash sale triggered when: loss exists AND repurchase date within 30 days before or after sale date AND shares repurchased > 0 - Partial wash sale: disallowed portion = loss per share × min(shares sold, shares repurchased) - Adjusted basis = repurchase price per share + (disallowed loss ÷ shares repurchased) - All calculations update in real time

Colors: - Header: linear-gradient(135deg, #1e3a5f, #2563eb) - Wash sale window band: #fee2e2 fill, #fca5a5 border - Sale date dot: #1e40af - Wash sale repurchase dot: #dc2626 - Clean repurchase dot: #16a34a

Implementation: Vanilla HTML/CSS/JavaScript with inline SVG timeline drawn dynamically

docs.title: Ordinary Dividends Tax Rate
docs.text: When a corporation pays out profits to shareholders, those payments are called dividends. The default treatment is ordinary dividends, taxed at your regular marginal income tax rate — the same rates as your wages. Box 1a of your 1099-DIV reports your total ordinary dividends.

docs.title: Qualified Dividends Tax Rate
docs.text:
Dividends that meet certain requirements get preferential qualified dividend treatment — taxed at the same lower rates as long-term capital gains (0%, 15%, or 20%). To be "qualified," a dividend must be:

  • Paid by a U.S. corporation or a qualified foreign corporation
  • You must have held the underlying stock for more than 60 days during the 121-day period centered on the ex-dividend date

Box 1b of your 1099-DIV shows your qualified dividends (a subset of box 1a). Most dividends from large U.S. companies traded on major exchanges are qualified. REIT dividends, money market dividends, and some foreign company dividends are typically not qualified.

Qualified Dividends vs. Ordinary: A Quick Dollar Comparison

Suppose you receive $10,000 in dividends. If you're in the 22% ordinary income bracket and all dividends are ordinary, you owe $2,200. If those same dividends are qualified and you're in the 15% long-term gains bracket, you owe $1,500 — a $700 difference on the same income. Over decades of investing, this adds up significantly.

docs.title: Cryptocurrency as Property (IRS Treatment)
docs.text:
Since IRS Notice 2014-21, the IRS has treated cryptocurrency as property, not currency. This single classification has enormous consequences for how crypto is taxed. Every time you sell, trade, or spend cryptocurrency, you have a taxable event — you must calculate gain or loss just as if you had sold stock.

This means: - Selling Bitcoin for dollars: taxable event - Trading Bitcoin for Ethereum: taxable event (you "sold" Bitcoin for its fair market value, then "bought" Ethereum) - Buying a pizza with Bitcoin: taxable event (the IRS sees this as selling Bitcoin at its current value) - Receiving crypto as payment for work: taxable as ordinary income at the fair market value when received - Mining or staking rewards: generally taxable as ordinary income when received

The only events that are not taxable: transferring crypto between your own wallets, buying crypto with dollars (the purchase itself isn't taxable — but it starts your cost basis clock).

docs.title: Crypto Cost Basis Tracking
docs.text:
Because every transaction is a taxable event, tracking your cost basis across potentially thousands of transactions is one of the biggest practical challenges in crypto taxation. The IRS allows the same cost basis methods used for stocks (FIFO, specific identification), but crypto exchanges often don't provide the clean 1099-B reports that stock brokers do. You may need to use dedicated crypto tax software (CoinTracker, Koinly, TaxBit, etc.) that pulls data from exchange APIs and computes gains/losses automatically.

DeFi and Cross-Chain Complexity

If you've used decentralized exchanges (DEX), liquidity pools, or bridged assets across blockchains, your tax situation becomes significantly more complex. Each token swap on a DEX is a taxable sale. Providing liquidity and receiving LP tokens may be a taxable exchange. There is limited IRS guidance on some DeFi activities — consult a tax professional familiar with crypto if your situation involves these transactions.

docs.title: NFT Tax Treatment
docs.text:
Non-fungible tokens (NFTs) are taxed as collectibles by the IRS, not as ordinary capital assets. The practical difference: long-term capital gains on collectibles are taxed at a maximum rate of 28%, which is higher than the 20% maximum rate that applies to most long-term capital gains. If you bought an NFT, held it over a year, and sold it at a profit, you're looking at up to 28% federal tax on that gain — not 20%.

Short-term NFT gains are still taxed as ordinary income (the same as any short-term gain), and the NIIT may apply on top of the regular rate for high earners. If you create and sell NFTs as part of a business, the income is self-employment income, subject to both income tax and self-employment tax.

docs.title: Cost Basis Methods Overview
docs.text: When you own multiple lots of the same security (bought at different times and prices) and sell some shares, you need to decide which shares you're selling. The IRS allows several methods, and the choice can have a dramatic impact on your tax bill.

docs.title: FIFO Cost Basis Method
docs.text:
FIFO (First In, First Out) is the IRS default. If you don't specify otherwise, the IRS assumes you sold your oldest shares first. This makes sense for record-keeping simplicity, but it's not always optimal — your oldest shares may have the lowest basis and the largest gains, and they may already be long-term while newer shares are still short-term.

FIFO in Action

You bought 100 shares of XYZ at $20 (January 2022), then 100 more at $50 (June 2024). Now the stock is at $60, and you sell 100 shares. Under FIFO, you sold the January 2022 shares: gain = $60 - $20 = $40 per share × 100 = $4,000 long-term gain. If you had sold the June 2024 shares instead, the gain would be only $10 per share × 100 = $1,000 — but short-term.

docs.title: Specific Identification Method
docs.text:
Specific identification lets you choose exactly which lot of shares you're selling, allowing precise tax optimization. You could select the shares with the highest cost basis (to minimize gain), or specifically choose long-term shares over short-term shares to get the preferential rate.

The catch: you must make the election at the time of the sale, not after the fact. You tell your broker (in writing or through the brokerage's online interface) which specific lot you're selling before the trade settles. Your broker must confirm the instruction. If you wait until tax time and then try to pick the lot, the IRS won't allow it.

The average cost method is available only for mutual funds — you take the total cost of all shares divided by the total number of shares to get a uniform per-share basis. Once elected for a fund, it applies to all future sales of that fund at that broker (with some exceptions).

Method Best For Tax Optimization Election Required FIFO Simplicity, default Low (oldest shares first) No Specific ID Maximum flexibility High (choose any lot) Yes, at time of sale Average Cost Mutual funds Moderate Yes (for mutual funds)

docs.title: Mutual Fund Capital Gains
docs.text:
Here's a tax quirk of mutual funds that surprises many investors: even if you don't sell any shares of your mutual fund, the fund itself buys and sells securities throughout the year. When those internal transactions generate net capital gains, the fund is required to distribute those gains to shareholders — and you owe tax on them even if you automatically reinvested the distribution.

These capital gains distributions are reported in box 2a of your 1099-DIV (total capital gain distributions) and must be reported on your return. The distributions retain their character — most are long-term gains at the preferential rate — but you still owe tax in the year of distribution regardless of your fund's overall performance. It's entirely possible to receive a capital gains distribution from a fund that lost money that year.

If you're investing in a taxable account (not a 401(k) or IRA), this is a meaningful disadvantage of actively managed mutual funds compared to their index ETF counterparts.

docs.title: ETF Tax Efficiency
docs.text:
Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs) have a structural advantage over mutual funds when it comes to capital gains distributions. ETFs use an in-kind creation and redemption mechanism: large institutional investors (called "authorized participants") can create new ETF shares by delivering a basket of the underlying securities to the ETF, or redeem ETF shares by receiving those securities back — all without any cash changing hands inside the fund.

This means the ETF can shed low-basis shares by handing them out in-kind during redemptions, avoiding a taxable sale entirely. As a result, most broad market ETFs distribute little or no capital gains annually. You control when you realize gains (when you sell your ETF shares), which is far more tax-efficient for long-term investors in taxable accounts.

ETF vs. Mutual Fund: Taxable Account Rule of Thumb

For money held in taxable (non-retirement) accounts, prefer index ETFs over equivalent mutual funds when possible. You'll get similar market exposure with significantly fewer unexpected tax bills from internal fund trading. In a 401(k) or IRA, this distinction matters much less since gains inside the account aren't currently taxable.

docs.title: Municipal Bond Tax Exemption
docs.text:
Interest paid on municipal bonds (bonds issued by state and local governments to fund public projects) is exempt from federal income tax. This exemption is written directly into the tax code and makes municipal bonds particularly attractive to high-income investors.

Additionally, if you hold bonds issued by your own state (or a locality within your state), that interest is often also exempt from your state income tax — making it triple-tax-exempt if you're also exempt from local taxes. For an investor in the 37% federal bracket plus a 10% state bracket, a municipal bond paying 4% effectively yields the same after-tax return as a taxable bond paying over 7%.

The tax-equivalent yield formula lets you compare:

\[ \text{Tax-Equivalent Yield} = \frac{\text{Municipal Bond Yield}}{1 - \text{Marginal Tax Rate}} \]

For example, a 3.5% muni yield for someone in the 32% federal bracket:

\[ \frac{0.035}{1 - 0.32} = \frac{0.035}{0.68} \approx 5.15\% \]

That 3.5% muni is equivalent to a taxable bond yielding 5.15% for that investor.

Munis Aren't Always Better

For investors in lower tax brackets, the lower pre-tax yield of municipal bonds may not compensate for the tax exemption. Do the math using your actual marginal rate. Also note: municipal bond interest from private-activity bonds may be subject to the Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT), and any capital gain from selling a muni at a profit is fully taxable.

docs.title: Stock Options Tax Overview
docs.text: If your employer grants you stock options as part of your compensation, you're sitting on a potentially valuable benefit — and a genuinely complex tax situation. There are two fundamentally different types of stock options, and they're taxed in completely different ways. Mixing them up is a costly mistake.

docs.title: Incentive Stock Options (ISO)
docs.text:
Incentive Stock Options (ISOs) are the more tax-favored variety. The key points:

  • At grant: No tax.
  • At exercise (when you buy the shares at the strike price): No regular income tax. However, the "spread" (the difference between the fair market value and the strike price) is an AMT preference item and may trigger the Alternative Minimum Tax.
  • At sale: If you hold the shares for at least 1 year after exercise and at least 2 years after the grant date, the entire gain gets long-term capital gains treatment — taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20%.
  • If you sell before meeting those holding periods (a disqualifying disposition), the spread at exercise becomes ordinary income.

ISOs and the AMT: A Hidden Tax Surprise

Exercising ISOs late in the year in a year when the stock price has risen significantly can create a large AMT liability. The AMT spread is calculated on the difference between the exercise price and the fair market value at exercise — even if you haven't sold the shares yet. Some employees have exercised ISOs on stock that later crashed, leaving them with a large AMT bill and shares worth far less than the tax owed. If you're planning to exercise a large number of ISOs, model the AMT impact before year-end.

docs.title: Non-Qualified Stock Options (NSO)
docs.text:
Non-Qualified Stock Options (NSOs) — also called NQSOs or NQOs — are the more common type. The tax treatment is simpler but less favorable:

  • At grant: No tax.
  • At exercise: The spread (FMV at exercise minus the strike price) is taxed as ordinary income in the year of exercise. Your employer is required to withhold payroll taxes and income tax on this amount. Your W-2 will include it, though it may be buried in box 1.
  • Basis: Your cost basis in the shares becomes the FMV at exercise (not the strike price), so you've already been taxed on the spread.
  • At sale: Any additional gain or loss after exercise is a capital gain or loss, long-term or short-term depending on how long you hold the shares after exercise.
Feature ISO NSO Tax at grant None None Tax at exercise None (but AMT possible) Ordinary income on spread Employer withholding No Yes Sale tax treatment LTCG if holding periods met Capital gain/loss on post-exercise appreciation Who can receive Employees only Employees, contractors, directors

docs.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting Timing
docs.text:
Tax-loss harvesting is the practice of strategically selling investments that are currently trading below your cost basis to realize a capital loss — then using that loss to offset capital gains (and up to $3,000 of ordinary income). Done properly, it's one of the most powerful and accessible tax planning strategies available to individual investors.

The mechanics:

  1. Identify holdings in your taxable accounts with unrealized losses.
  2. Sell those positions to realize the loss.
  3. Immediately reinvest the proceeds in a similar but not identical fund or security to stay invested in the market (avoiding the wash sale rule).
  4. The realized loss offsets your gains this year; any excess carries forward.

The key is the "similar but not identical" replacement. You want to maintain your investment exposure and asset allocation, but you can't buy back the exact same security within 31 days. Common pairings that work: Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) ↔ iShares Core S&P Total U.S. Stock Market ETF (ITOT). They track different indexes but have highly similar performance.

docs.title: Diagram: Capital Gains Rate Optimizer
docs.text:
Capital Gains Rate Optimizer — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Given a user's filing status, ordinary income, and investment gains/losses, calculate the applicable long-term capital gains rate and total tax owed, showing where the gain falls within the 0%/15%/20% brackets and the impact of NIIT.

Layout: - Left panel (inputs): Filing status selector (Single / MFJ), ordinary taxable income slider ($0–$600,000), long-term capital gain input ($0–$500,000), short-term capital gain input ($0–$200,000), qualified dividends input ($0–$100,000) - Right panel (outputs): Color-coded bracket visualization showing the income stack (ordinary income in blue, then LTCG/qualified dividends in green stacked on top), tax breakdown table showing: LTCG rate applied, LTCG tax, short-term gain tax (ordinary rate), NIIT calculation and amount, total investment tax, effective rate on investment income - Below inputs: A "What-If: Defer $X of gain to next year" toggle that shows side-by-side tax cost

Inputs: - Filing status: radio buttons (Single, Married Filing Jointly) - Ordinary taxable income: slider + number input, range $0–$600,000, step $1,000 - Long-term capital gain: number input, range $0–$500,000 - Short-term capital gain: number input, range $0–$200,000 - Qualified dividends: number input, range $0–$100,000

Outputs: - A stacked bar showing: ordinary income (gray), 0% LTCG zone (light green), 15% LTCG zone (medium green), 20% LTCG zone (dark green), NIIT zone (red highlight overlay) - Tax Summary table with rows: Short-term gain tax, LTCG tax (at 0%), LTCG tax (at 15%), LTCG tax (at 20%), NIIT (3.8%), Total additional tax, Effective rate on investment income - Dynamic narrative: "Your $[X] long-term gain is taxed: $[Y] at 0%, $[Z] at 15%, $[W] at 20%"

Behavior: - Uses 2025 brackets: 0% LTCG threshold Single $48,350 / MFJ $96,700; 15% up to Single $533,400 / MFJ $600,050; 20% above - NIIT threshold: $200,000 Single / $250,000 MFJ on MAGI (approximated as ordinary income + LTCG + qualified dividends) - LTCG fills up the 0% bucket first before spilling into 15%, then 20% - All calculations update in real time as sliders/inputs change

Colors: - Ordinary income bar: #64748b (slate gray) - 0% LTCG zone: #86efac (light green) - 15% LTCG zone: #22c55e (medium green) - 20% LTCG zone: #15803d (dark green) - NIIT overlay: #fca5a5 (light red) - Background: white, labels in #1e293b

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

Tax-loss harvesting is most valuable when:

  • You have short-term gains (taxed as ordinary income) — offsetting those with losses saves the most per dollar of loss
  • You're in a high tax bracket
  • You have significant unrealized losses but want to stay invested in the market
  • It's late in the year and you can see your full-year gain picture

It's least valuable (or irrelevant) when:

  • All your investments are inside tax-advantaged accounts (401k, IRA) — no taxable gains to harvest against
  • Your income is low enough that you're already in the 0% LTCG bracket
  • The replacement investment you'd buy has materially different risk/return characteristics than what you're selling

docs.title: 1099-DIV Explained
docs.text:
The 1099-DIV is the form your brokerage or mutual fund company sends you (and the IRS) when you've received dividends or capital gain distributions during the year. You need this form to fill out your Schedule B and ultimately your Form 1040.

Key boxes to know:

Box What It Reports Tax Treatment 1a Total ordinary dividends Ordinary income rates 1b Qualified dividends LTCG rates (0%/15%/20%) 2a Total capital gain distributions Generally LTCG rates 2b Unrecaptured Section 1250 gain 25% maximum rate 2d Collectible gains 28% maximum rate 3 Nondividend distributions (return of capital) Reduces cost basis; not currently taxable 4 Federal income tax withheld Direct credit against your tax bill

Box 1b is always a subset of box 1a — qualified dividends are a portion of ordinary dividends that happen to qualify for the lower rate. When you enter your 1099-DIV in tax software, it handles the rate difference automatically.

docs.title: 1099-B Explained
docs.text:
The 1099-B is the primary form for reporting capital gains and losses. Your broker sends one for every year in which you had any sales of securities. It reports each individual transaction with:

  • Proceeds (what you received from the sale)
  • Cost basis (what you paid, if the broker has it)
  • Holding period (short-term or long-term)
  • Whether basis was reported to the IRS (covered securities) or not (noncovered securities)

The covered/noncovered distinction matters. For covered securities (stocks purchased after January 1, 2011; mutual funds and ETFs after January 1, 2012), the broker is required to report your cost basis to the IRS on the 1099-B. For noncovered securities (bought before those dates, or certain other situations), the broker reports proceeds only, and you must provide basis from your own records.

Don't Let the IRS Calculate Your Basis

If you have noncovered securities and file without providing your cost basis, the IRS will treat your basis as zero — meaning you'll owe tax on the full sale proceeds. Always track your basis for noncovered positions. Old brokerage statements, personal records, or transfer documents are acceptable.

Your 1099-B feeds directly into Schedule D (Capital Gains and Losses) and Form 8949 (Sales and Other Dispositions of Capital Assets). Most tax software imports your 1099-B electronically and populates these automatically.

docs.title: Schedule B Interest and Dividends
docs.text:
Schedule B is a supplemental form that you attach to your Form 1040 when:

  • Your total taxable interest income exceeds $1,500, or
  • Your total ordinary dividend income exceeds $1,500, or
  • You had a financial interest in or signature authority over a foreign financial account, or
  • You received income from a foreign trust

Part I of Schedule B lists each payer of interest income and the amounts. Part II does the same for dividends. Part III asks the foreign account and trust questions — even if your total is under $1,500, you still need Schedule B if you had foreign accounts (and potentially FBAR filing obligations, which are separate from your tax return).

If all your interest and dividends total under $1,500 and you had no foreign accounts, you simply enter the totals directly on Form 1040 without Schedule B.

docs.title: Putting It All Together: A Year-End Investment Tax Checklist
docs.text:
By mid-November each year, you have enough information to make smart moves before December 31. Here's how the concepts in this chapter connect into action:

  • Review unrealized gains and losses in all taxable accounts. Identify candidates for tax-loss harvesting.
  • Check your holding periods. If you're close to one year on a winning position, consider waiting the few extra days to convert a short-term gain to long-term.
  • Match gains and losses. If you've already realized gains this year, selling losers can offset those gains dollar-for-dollar.
  • Watch the wash sale window. If you harvested losses in early December, remember the 30-day clock — don't repurchase the same security before January.
  • Check for mutual fund distributions. Many funds distribute capital gains in November or December. If you're considering buying a mutual fund in a taxable account late in the year, check if it's about to make a large distribution — you might be buying a tax bill.
  • Review your stock options. If you have ISOs, model the AMT impact before exercising. If you have NSOs, understand that exercising creates ordinary income in that year.
  • Calculate your estimated LTCG rate. Use the 2025 brackets to see if any of your gains will fall in the 0% zone — which changes your harvesting priorities.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Holding period is everything. More than one year and one day qualifies for long-term capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20%), which are far lower than ordinary income rates that apply to short-term gains.
  • Capital losses are valuable. They offset gains dollar-for-dollar, and up to $3,000 per year can offset ordinary income. Excess losses carry forward indefinitely.
  • The wash sale rule is strict but navigable. Don't repurchase substantially identical securities within 31 days of selling at a loss — but you can immediately replace with similar (not identical) investments.
  • Qualified dividends get the same preferential rates as long-term capital gains. Ordinary dividends do not. Check box 1b vs. box 1a on your 1099-DIV.
  • Crypto is property, not currency. Every sale, trade, or spending transaction is a taxable event requiring gain/loss calculation.
  • NFTs are collectibles and face a 28% maximum long-term rate — higher than standard LTCG rates.
  • Your cost basis method matters. FIFO is the default; specific identification gives you control to minimize taxes, but you must elect it at the time of sale.
  • ETFs are more tax-efficient than mutual funds in taxable accounts due to the in-kind redemption mechanism that minimizes capital gains distributions.
  • Municipal bond interest is federal-tax-free and often state-tax-free if issued in your state — use the tax-equivalent yield formula to compare against taxable bonds.
  • ISOs offer long-term capital gains treatment if holding period requirements are met, but can trigger AMT at exercise. NSOs create ordinary income at exercise — no holding period work-around.
  • Tax-loss harvesting is a practical year-round (not just year-end) strategy to reduce your tax bill without abandoning your investment strategy.
  • 1099-DIV, 1099-B, and Schedule B are the three primary forms for investment income — understand what each box means so you can spot errors and enter data correctly.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Log in to your brokerage account and look for a "tax center" or "cost basis" section. Confirm your cost basis method election (FIFO vs. specific identification) before your next sale.
  2. Download last year's 1099-DIV and 1099-B and find boxes 1a, 1b, 2a. Compare: how much of your dividend income was qualified vs. ordinary? Did you have any capital gain distributions from mutual funds?
  3. Review your holdings for unrealized gains and losses. If you have significant unrealized losses, note them — you may want to harvest them before year-end.
  4. Check your holding periods. For any position you're thinking of selling that's close to the one-year mark, calculate the exact date you need to wait to qualify for long-term treatment.
  5. If you own cryptocurrency, tally your transactions from the past year. If you used multiple exchanges, consider connecting them to crypto tax software before you're scrambling in April.
  6. If you have employer stock options, identify whether they are ISOs or NSOs (check your option grant agreement or ask HR). Model the tax impact of exercising before doing so.
  7. Calculate your tax-equivalent yield on any bonds or CDs you're evaluating. If you're in the 24% bracket or higher, municipal bonds deserve a serious look for your taxable accounts.
  8. If your interest + dividends may exceed $1,500, set a reminder to attach Schedule B to your return and verify you haven't had any foreign financial accounts to report.

docs.title: 8. Real Estate & Rental Taxes
docs.text: title: Chapter 8: Real Estate & Rental Property Taxes description: Tax rules for rental property owners and home sellers — covering rental income, depreciation, passive loss rules, primary residence exclusion, 1031 exchanges, and real estate tax forms. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-21 version: 0.04

docs.title: Chapter 8: Real Estate & Rental Property Taxes
Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers the tax rules for rental property owners, landlords, and anyone selling a home. You'll learn how to report rental income and deduct rental expenses, how depreciation works under MACRS, when passive activity loss rules limit your deductions, the \(250k/\)500k primary residence exclusion, how 1031 like-kind exchanges defer capital gains, and how short-term rentals (Airbnb-style) are taxed differently. The rental-related tax forms — Schedule D, Schedule E, and Form 8949 — are introduced here alongside the real estate concepts they report.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 20 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Rental Income Reporting
  2. Rental Expense Deductions
  3. Rental Property Depreciation
  4. MACRS Depreciation Schedule
  5. Depreciation Recapture Tax
  6. Passive Activity Loss Rules
  7. Real Estate Professional Status
  8. Short-Term Rental Tax Rules
  9. Primary Residence Sale Exclusion
  10. \(250K/\)500K Exclusion Rules
  11. 1031 Like-Kind Exchange
  12. Installment Sale Method
  13. Rental Mortgage Interest
  14. Vacation Home Tax Rules
  15. Mixed Personal Rental Use
  16. Home Equity Loan Deduction
  17. Qualified Improvement Property
  18. Schedule D Capital Gains
  19. Schedule E Rental Income
  20. Form 8949 Asset Sales

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 3: Legal Tax Reduction Strategies
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes
  • Chapter 7: Investments & Capital Gains Taxes

docs.title: Introduction: Real Estate Has Its Own Tax Universe
docs.text:
Owning rental property puts you in a different tax world from wage earners. You get deductions most people never see — depreciation on a building you may be watching appreciate in value, interest on a mortgage that you're building equity through, and a host of operating expenses that reduce your taxable rental income to well below what you actually collected in rent. But real estate also comes with rules that can bite you if you're not paying attention: passive activity loss limits that may defer your deductions for years, depreciation recapture that can hit you with a 25% tax rate when you sell, and a web of special rules for vacation homes, short-term rentals, and home sales.

This chapter walks through all of it in practical order — starting with the basics of what counts as rental income, moving through deductions and depreciation, then tackling the bigger-picture questions around selling property and exchanging it tax-free. Along the way, you'll meet the three tax forms that do the heavy lifting for real estate: Schedule E (rental income and expenses), Schedule D (capital gains from property sales), and Form 8949 (individual asset sales). By the end, you'll understand why a landlord can show a tax loss on paper while pocketing positive cash flow every month — and whether that loss actually reduces your tax bill.

docs.title: Part 1: Reporting Rental Income and Deductions
Rental Income Reporting
docs.text:
The IRS's rule on rental income is simple: if a tenant pays you money, it's taxable in the year you receive it. That applies whether you deposit the check, cash it, or have it sent directly to your mortgage servicer. Prepaid rent — say, a tenant pays January rent in December — is taxable when received, not when the period covers. The one major exception is the security deposit: as long as you intend to return it and are legally required to, it is not income when collected. However, the moment you apply a security deposit to unpaid rent or damages, that amount becomes taxable income in the year you keep it.

What Counts as Rental Income

  • All monthly rent payments received
  • Advance rent and prepaid rent (taxable when received)
  • Security deposits that are forfeited or applied to rent/damages
  • Lease cancellation payments received from tenants
  • Services received instead of rent (taxed at fair market value)
  • Expenses paid by the tenant on your behalf (e.g., they pay the utility bill)

Not income: Security deposits held in trust that you intend to return.

docs.title: Rental Expense Deductions
docs.text:
The flip side of rental income is a robust set of deductions. You can deduct ordinary and necessary expenses for managing, conserving, and maintaining your rental property. The key distinction that trips people up is repairs vs. improvements: a repair restores something to its original working condition and is deductible in full in the year you pay it; an improvement adds value, adapts the property to a new use, or extends its useful life, and must be capitalized and depreciated over time.

Here is a table of the most common rental deductions and their treatment:

Expense Deductible Notes Mortgage interest (rental property) Yes — fully No $750k cap; deducted on Schedule E Property taxes Yes State and local taxes on the rental property Homeowner's / landlord's insurance Yes Premiums for the rental period Repairs and maintenance Yes Must restore, not improve Property management fees Yes If you hire a manager or management company Advertising / listing fees Yes Craigslist, Zillow, Airbnb listing fees Legal and professional fees Yes Lease drafting, eviction attorney, tax prep Depreciation Yes Calculated separately; see next section Capital improvements No (directly) Capitalized; depreciated over asset life Travel to inspect/manage property Yes Actual expense or standard mileage

docs.title: Rental Mortgage Interest
docs.text: Rental property mortgage interest is fully deductible on Schedule E with no dollar-cap limitation. This is a meaningful contrast to your primary residence mortgage, where interest deductibility is capped on debt up to $750,000 (or $1,000,000 for older mortgages). On a rental property, if your mortgage balance is $1.5 million and you're paying $90,000 in interest per year, every dollar of that interest is deductible against your rental income. This makes leveraged real estate particularly tax-efficient on the income side — though depreciation recapture complicates the exit.

docs.title: Home Equity Loan Deduction
docs.text:
If you take out a home equity loan or line of credit (HELOC), the interest is deductible only if the loan proceeds are used to buy, build, or substantially improve the property that secures the loan. Using HELOC proceeds to fund vacations, pay off credit cards, or invest in the stock market? That interest is not deductible at all. Using the HELOC on your primary home to fund improvements on a separate rental property? Also not deductible as mortgage interest — though it may be deductible as a rental expense if the purpose qualifies. Keep meticulous records of how you use loan proceeds; the IRS expects you to trace the money.

HELOC Interest Trap

Many taxpayers mistakenly deduct home equity interest simply because the loan is secured by their home. The IRS requires that proceeds be traceable to buying, building, or substantially improving the secured property. Using HELOC funds for other purposes — even other investment properties — disqualifies the interest deduction under the mortgage interest rules.

docs.title: Part 2: Depreciation — Your Biggest Phantom Deduction
Rental Property Depreciation
docs.text: Depreciation is the tax system's acknowledgment that buildings wear out over time. Even if your property is going up in value every year, the IRS lets you deduct a portion of its cost as though it were depreciating. This is called a non-cash deduction — it reduces your taxable income without requiring you to write a check. For residential rental property, you depreciate the building (never the land) over 27.5 years using the straight-line method under MACRS.

docs.title: MACRS Depreciation Schedule
docs.text:
The Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System (MACRS) is the IRS's mandated depreciation system for most property placed in service after 1986. Residential rental property uses the straight-line method over a 27.5-year recovery period, which means you deduct an equal fraction of the building's adjusted cost basis each year. Land is never depreciable — you must allocate the purchase price between land and building before calculating depreciation. A common method is to use the county assessor's ratio of land-to-building value, or to hire an appraiser.

The depreciation formula for residential rental property:

\[ \text{Annual Depreciation} = \frac{\text{Purchase Price} - \text{Land Value}}{27.5} \]

Example: You buy a duplex for $385,000. The county assessment allocates $60,000 to land and $325,000 to the building.

\[ \text{Annual Depreciation} = \frac{\$385{,}000 - \$60{,}000}{27.5} = \frac{\$325{,}000}{27.5} \approx \$11{,}818 \text{ per year} \]

That's nearly $12,000 in deductions every year — a real reduction in taxable income — even if your property is gaining $30,000 in market value annually.

Partial-Year Rule

In the year you place the property in service (and the year you sell it), you don't get a full year of depreciation. Residential rental property uses the mid-month convention: you get a half-month of depreciation for the month you place it in service, regardless of which day of the month you close.

docs.title: Qualified Improvement Property
docs.text: Not all building-related depreciation follows the 27.5-year residential schedule. Qualified Improvement Property (QIP) applies to interior improvements made to the interior of nonresidential (commercial) buildings after the building was first placed in service. QIP has a 15-year MACRS life and is eligible for bonus depreciation — meaning you can potentially deduct the entire cost in year one rather than spreading it over 15 years. If you own a commercial rental building and renovate the interior, QIP rules can dramatically accelerate your deduction. Note: structural components, elevators, escalators, and the building's internal structural framework are excluded from QIP.

docs.title: Depreciation Recapture Tax
docs.text:
Here is the catch with depreciation: the IRS gives with one hand and takes with the other when you sell. When you sell a rental property, you must pay tax on all the depreciation you took (or were entitled to take) over your holding period. This is called depreciation recapture, and it is taxed at a maximum federal rate of 25% — higher than the 15% most taxpayers pay on long-term capital gains. The relevant tax rule is Section 1250 unrecaptured gain.

Example continued: You sell the duplex after 10 years. You've taken $118,180 in depreciation ($11,818 x 10). Even if you'd qualify for 0% or 15% long-term capital gains rates on the rest of the gain, that $118,180 of recaptured depreciation is taxed at up to 25%. That's potentially a $29,545 federal tax bill from depreciation recapture alone — before any state taxes.

You Owe Recapture Even If You Didn't Take the Deduction

The IRS calculates recapture based on depreciation "allowed or allowable." If you forgot to claim depreciation for three years, you still owe recapture tax on those three years when you sell. File amended returns (Form 3115) to claim missed depreciation before you sell.

docs.title: Part 3: The Passive Activity Loss Rules
Passive Activity Loss Rules
docs.text:
The IRS separates your income and losses into buckets. Rental activities are, by default, passive activities. The fundamental rule: passive losses can only offset passive income. You cannot use a rental property loss to offset your W-2 salary, business income, or investment income — unless you qualify for an exception. This rule was enacted in 1986 specifically to prevent high-income taxpayers from sheltering wages with real estate losses. Unused passive losses are suspended and carried forward to future years, where they can offset future passive income or are released in full when you dispose of the property in a taxable sale.

There are two main exceptions that allow rental losses to offset ordinary income:

  • The $25,000 Special Allowance: If your Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) is under $100,000 and you actively participate in the rental activity (making management decisions like setting rents, approving tenants, authorizing repairs), you can deduct up to $25,000 of rental losses against ordinary income. This allowance phases out by 50 cents for every dollar of AGI over $100,000 and disappears entirely at $150,000 AGI.
  • Real Estate Professional Status: If you qualify as a real estate professional (covered next), your rental activities are reclassified as active, and losses are fully deductible against all income.

The phase-out math for the $25,000 allowance:

\[ \text{Allowable Loss} = \$25{,}000 - 0.50 \times (\text{AGI} - \$100{,}000) \]

At AGI = $130,000: Allowable loss = $25,000 - 0.50 × $30,000 = $25,000 - \(15,000 = **\)10,000**. At AGI = $150,000: Allowable loss = $0. Losses are fully suspended.

docs.title: Real Estate Professional Status
docs.text:
The Real Estate Professional designation is a powerful tax status that removes the passive activity limitation entirely for rental activities. To qualify, you must meet two tests in the tax year:

  1. You spend more than 750 hours in real property trades or businesses in which you materially participate.
  2. More than 50% of all the personal services you perform during the year are in real property trades or businesses.

Real property trades or businesses include development, construction, acquisition, conversion, rental, operation, management, leasing, and brokerage. A full-time employee who also owns rentals on the side typically cannot qualify — a 2,000-hour/year job already makes it impossible to meet the 50% test unless the job itself is in real estate. Qualifying allows you to fully deduct rental losses against all income, which can produce substantial tax savings if you have large depreciation deductions.

Hourly Log is Non-Negotiable

If you claim Real Estate Professional status, keep a contemporaneous log of every hour spent on real estate activities. The IRS frequently challenges this designation, and "I think I worked about 800 hours" won't hold up in court. Use a spreadsheet, calendar entries, or property management software with timestamps.

docs.title: Diagram: Passive Loss Decision Tree
docs.text:
Passive Loss Decision Tree — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Determine

Learning Objective: Given a user's AGI, rental loss amount, active participation status, and hours worked in real estate, the learner can determine how much of their rental loss is deductible in the current year versus suspended.

Layout: - Left panel (40%): Input controls stacked vertically - Slider: AGI ($0 – $300,000, step $5,000), default \(80,000 - Slider: Rental loss this year (\)0 – $50,000, step $1,000), default $15,000 - Toggle: "Actively participate in management?" (Yes/No), default Yes - Toggle: "Real Estate Professional?" (Yes/No), default No - Number input: Hours in real estate this year (0–2000), default 0 - Number input: Total hours worked this year (0–4000), default 2000 - Right panel (60%): Output display - Large colored result box at top: "Deductible This Year: $X,XXX" in green if >0, gray if 0 - Secondary result: "Suspended (Carried Forward): $X,XXX" - Decision path: Step-by-step explanation showing which rule applied (REP test, $25k allowance, phase-out, full suspension) - Bar chart: horizontal bars showing Total Loss, Deductible Amount, Suspended Amount in distinct colors

Behavior: - If REP = Yes AND hours >= 750 AND hours > (total hours * 0.5): classify as active; full loss is deductible; show "Real Estate Professional — full loss deductible" - If AGI <= $100,000 AND actively participates: allowable = min(rental loss, $25,000); show full deduction - If $100,000 < AGI < $150,000 AND actively participates: apply phase-out formula; show partial deduction - If AGI >= $150,000 OR no active participation (and not REP): deductible = $0; full amount suspended - All calculations update instantly on any input change

Colors: - Deductible amount: #2E7D32 (dark green) - Suspended amount: #B71C1C (dark red) - Phase-out range indicator: #F57F17 (amber) - Background: #F5F5F5, panel cards: white with subtle shadow

Implementation: HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

docs.title: Part 4: Short-Term Rentals and Vacation Homes
Short-Term Rental Tax Rules
docs.text:
Airbnb, VRBO, and similar platforms have created a category of rental property that the IRS treats fundamentally differently from traditional long-term rentals. The critical test is the average rental period. If the average rental period for your property is 7 days or fewer (think nightly and weekly rentals), the IRS considers it an active business — not a passive rental activity. This means:

  • Income and expenses go on Schedule C (not Schedule E)
  • Losses are not subject to passive activity limitations
  • BUT profits are subject to self-employment (SE) tax of 15.3% (subject to the SE tax deduction)
  • You may also need to collect and remit state/local occupancy taxes

The short-term rental classification can be beneficial if you're generating losses (no passive limitation) but costly if you're profitable (SE tax on top of income tax). Many short-term rental owners do not realize they're operating a business — and that their Schedule C net income is subject to SE tax just like any other self-employment income.

Short-Term vs. Long-Term Rental

Priya owns two condos. Condo A is rented to a long-term tenant on a 12-month lease — average rental period 365 days. Income and expenses go on Schedule E; losses subject to passive activity rules. Condo B is listed on Airbnb with an average stay of 3 nights. Income goes on Schedule C; losses freely offset other income but profits are subject to SE tax. Same owner, two properties, two completely different tax treatments.

docs.title: Vacation Home Tax Rules
docs.text:
A vacation home — a property you both rent out and use personally — gets its own special ruleset. The IRS draws two bright lines:

  • Rented fewer than 15 days per year: The rental income is completely tax-free and does not need to be reported. But you also cannot deduct any rental expenses. This is the "master bedroom exception" that benefits homeowners who rent their home during a major local event (Super Bowl week, for example) — rent the place out for 10 days, pocket the income tax-free.
  • Rented 15 or more days per year: You must report all rental income and must allocate expenses between the rental use and personal use. This is where the allocation method matters.

docs.title: Mixed Personal Rental Use
docs.text:
When a property is used for both personal enjoyment and rental, you must split every shared expense (mortgage interest, insurance, property taxes, utilities, depreciation) between the two uses. Two allocation methods exist:

Method How Expenses Are Allocated Who Benefits IRS Method By total days used (rental days ÷ total days the property was used) IRS — produces smaller rental deduction Bolton Method By rental days ÷ total days in the year (365) Taxpayer — produces larger rental deduction

Example: Your beach house was rented for 90 days and used personally for 30 days. Total days used = 120.

  • IRS Method rental ratio: 90/120 = 75% of shared expenses allocated to rental
  • Bolton Method rental ratio: 90/365 = 24.7% of shared expenses allocated to rental

Wait — the Bolton method produces a smaller rental allocation. Why is it "more favorable"? Because your personal deductions (mortgage interest and property taxes) get a larger share of those expenses when you file on Schedule A, while the rental side still picks up 100% of expenses that are purely rental-related (advertising, rental management fees). The net effect is more total deductions across both categories. Tax courts have accepted both methods; the Bolton method is generally more taxpayer-favorable in practice.

Vacation Home Expense Ordering Rule

When a vacation home has mixed use, you must deduct rental expenses in a specific order: 1. Mortgage interest and property taxes (deductible in full on Schedule A if not taken as rental) 2. Operating expenses (insurance, utilities) — allocated by your chosen method 3. Depreciation — allocated last; cannot create a rental loss that exceeds gross rental income

docs.title: Primary Residence Sale Exclusion
docs.text: When you sell your home for a profit, the IRS offers the single most generous tax break in the entire tax code for individual taxpayers: you can exclude up to $250,000 of gain from taxable income (or $500,000 if you're married filing jointly). This is not a deferral like a 1031 exchange — it's a permanent exclusion. The gain simply disappears from your tax return. A couple who bought their home for $300,000 and sells for $750,000 with $450,000 of gain pays zero capital gains tax on the first $500,000 — and only owes tax (at long-term capital gains rates) on the $0 of gain above exclusion if their gain fits within the cap.

docs.title: \(250K/\)500K Exclusion Rules
docs.text:
To qualify for the exclusion, you must meet two tests:

  • Ownership test: You owned the home for at least 2 of the last 5 years before the sale date.
  • Use test: You used the home as your primary residence for at least 2 of the last 5 years before the sale date.

The two years do not need to be consecutive, and they do not need to overlap with each other. You can claim the exclusion once every 2 years. Additional rules:

  • A reduced exclusion is available if you sell due to a job change, health reasons, or unforeseen circumstances, even if you haven't met the full 2-year tests.
  • If the home was previously used as a rental, the exclusion applies only to the portion of gain that accrued during qualifying residential use. Depreciation claimed during rental periods is always recaptured at 25% and cannot be excluded.
  • The exclusion applies to gains, not proceeds. You must calculate your adjusted cost basis (original purchase price + capital improvements - depreciation taken during rental periods) to determine the actual gain.

Primary Residence Sale with Rental History

Marcus bought a condo for $200,000 in 2018. He rented it from 2018–2021, claiming $10,909 in depreciation. He moved in as his primary residence in 2021 and sold in 2025 for $400,000. His adjusted basis = $200,000 - $10,909 = $189,091. His total gain = $400,000 - $189,091 = $210,909. The $10,909 depreciation recapture is taxed at 25% (not excludable). The remaining $200,000 gain qualifies for the primary residence exclusion (well under $250k) and is excluded. Marcus pays recapture tax on the depreciation portion only.

docs.title: Schedule D Capital Gains
docs.text:
Schedule D is where all capital gains and losses from property sales are summarized on your tax return. When you sell real estate, stock, cryptocurrency, or virtually any capital asset, the net gain or loss flows through Schedule D. Schedule D separates short-term gains (assets held 1 year or less, taxed as ordinary income) from long-term gains (assets held more than 1 year, taxed at 0%, 15%, or 20% depending on your income). Real property held more than a year generates long-term capital gains — except that the portion attributable to depreciation recapture is carved out and taxed at up to 25% regardless of holding period.

Schedule D is not where you itemize each individual transaction. That detail lives on Form 8949, and the totals roll up to Schedule D.

docs.title: Form 8949 Asset Sales
docs.text:
Form 8949 is the transaction-level detail sheet for asset sales. Every individual property sale (and stock sale, crypto sale, etc.) gets its own line on Form 8949 — listing the description, acquisition date, sale date, proceeds, cost basis, and any adjustments. The transactions are separated into Part I (short-term) and Part II (long-term), and the net totals from each Part flow to the corresponding lines on Schedule D.

For real estate sales, the details required on Form 8949 include your adjusted basis (which accounts for depreciation) and the selling expenses (agent commissions, title fees, transfer taxes). These expenses reduce your realized gain and must be carefully documented.

Form 8949 vs. Schedule D — Division of Labor

Think of Form 8949 as the worksheet and Schedule D as the summary. Every property or asset sale gets reported on Form 8949 first. Form 8949 totals flow to Schedule D. Schedule D totals flow to Schedule 1, which flows to your Form 1040. For real estate, you'll also need to account for the 25% recapture portion separately using the Unrecaptured Section 1250 Gain Worksheet in the Schedule D instructions.

docs.title: Schedule E Rental Income
docs.text:
Schedule E (Supplemental Income and Loss) is the reporting form for all rental property income and expenses. Each property gets its own column — up to three properties per page, with additional pages if needed. For each property, you enter the address, fair rental days, personal use days, gross rental income, and a breakdown of deductible expenses. The bottom line of Schedule E feeds into Schedule 1 (Additional Income and Adjustments), which ultimately appears on your Form 1040.

Schedule E Section What Goes There Part I, Column A/B/C Each rental property (up to 3 per page) Income Gross rents received Expenses Advertising, auto/travel, depreciation, insurance, legal, management fees, mortgage interest, taxes, repairs, supplies, utilities Depreciation From Form 4562 (Depreciation and Amortization); enter total here Total income or loss Flows to Schedule 1, Line 5

docs.title: 1031 Like-Kind Exchange
docs.text:
If you sell an investment property at a gain, you can defer all capital gains tax — including depreciation recapture — by reinvesting the proceeds into another "like-kind" real property through a Section 1031 exchange. "Like-kind" in real estate is broadly defined: you can exchange a residential rental for a commercial building, raw land for a shopping center, or a single-family rental for a multi-family complex. What matters is that both properties are held for investment or business use — your personal residence does not qualify.

The rules are strict on timing and execution:

  • You must use a qualified intermediary (QI) — a neutral third party who holds the sale proceeds. You cannot touch the money.
  • You have 45 days from the sale of your relinquished property to formally identify potential replacement properties (written identification to the QI).
  • You have 180 days from the sale (or your tax return due date, whichever is earlier) to close on the replacement property.
  • You must reinvest all of the net proceeds and replace the debt with equal or greater debt on the new property to defer 100% of the gain. Any cash you receive ("boot") is taxable.

The 1031 exchange defers — not forgives — the gain. Your basis in the new property is reduced by the gain you deferred, which means the deferred gain will eventually be recognized when you sell the replacement property (unless you do another 1031 exchange, or hold until death, when your heirs receive a stepped-up basis).

The Estate Planning Angle on 1031 Exchanges

Savvy real estate investors use 1031 exchanges repeatedly throughout their lifetime, deferring gains into ever-larger properties. At death, heirs receive the property at a stepped-up basis equal to fair market value, completely eliminating the deferred gain. This "swap until you drop" strategy is one of the most powerful wealth transfer techniques in the tax code.

docs.title: Installment Sale Method
docs.text:
When you sell property and receive payments over multiple years — rather than a lump sum at closing — you can use the installment sale method to spread the taxable gain across the years you receive payments. This is reported on Form 6252 (Installment Sale Income). Each payment you receive is divided into three components: return of basis (not taxable), interest income (taxable as ordinary income), and gain (taxable as capital gain).

The gross profit percentage drives the gain calculation:

\[ \text{Gross Profit Percentage} = \frac{\text{Gross Profit}}{\text{Contract Price}} \] \[ \text{Gain in Each Year} = \text{Principal Received} \times \text{Gross Profit Percentage} \]

Example: You sell a rental property for $500,000 with a $200,000 adjusted basis, receiving $100,000 down and $100,000 per year for four years. Your gross profit is $300,000, and your gross profit percentage is $300,000 / $500,000 = 60%. Each $100,000 payment generates $60,000 of taxable gain in the year received. The depreciation recapture portion must all be recognized in the year of sale (not spread out), so installment sales work best when recapture is relatively small compared to overall gain.

Installment Sale Election

The installment method is the default when you receive at least one payment after the tax year of sale. You can opt out by reporting the entire gain in the year of sale. Opting out makes sense if you expect tax rates to rise significantly, or if you have large capital losses to offset the gain in the current year.

docs.title: Diagram: Rental Property Annual Tax Summary
docs.text:
Rental Property Annual Tax Summary — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Learning Objective: Given inputs for a rental property (purchase price, land value, gross rent, operating expenses, mortgage interest, AGI), the learner can calculate annual depreciation, net rental income or loss, the deductible portion under passive activity rules, and estimate the tax impact.

Layout: - Top bar: Chapter title "Rental Property Tax Calculator" with subtitle "See how depreciation and passive loss rules affect your taxes" - Left panel (45%): Property Inputs section - Number input: Purchase price (default $350,000) - Number input: Land value (default $60,000) - Number input: Gross annual rent collected (default $24,000) - Number input: Operating expenses (insurance, repairs, management — exclude mortgage interest, default $5,000) - Number input: Annual mortgage interest (default $14,000) - Number input: Your AGI (before rental income/loss, default $90,000) - Radio buttons: Active participation? (Yes / No), default Yes - Radio buttons: Real Estate Professional? (Yes / No), default No - Right panel (55%): Calculated Results section - Card 1 — "Annual Depreciation": show formula result with building value and 27.5 years - Card 2 — "Net Rental Income / (Loss)": gross rent minus all expenses minus depreciation - Card 3 — "Deductible This Year": apply passive loss rules based on AGI and participation status - Card 4 — "Suspended Loss Carryforward": any loss not currently deductible - Card 5 — "Estimated Tax Savings": deductible loss × estimated marginal rate (assume 22% if not specified, allow dropdown: 10/12/22/24/32/35%) - Full calculation breakdown shown in collapsible "Show Work" section with step-by-step arithmetic

Edge cases: - If land value >= purchase price: show error "Land value cannot exceed purchase price" - If REP = Yes but student hasn't entered hours: show reminder tooltip "Remember: REP requires 750+ hours and 50%+ of working time"

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
Real estate taxation rewards preparation and penalizes surprises. Here are the most important ideas from this chapter:

  • All rents received are taxable in the year collected, including prepaid rent. Security deposits are not income unless forfeited.
  • Rental expenses are broad — mortgage interest (no cap on rental property), taxes, insurance, repairs, management fees, and depreciation all reduce your taxable rental income.
  • Depreciation is a non-cash deduction calculated as (Purchase Price - Land Value) / 27.5 for residential rentals. You must take it while renting; you pay recapture tax when you sell.
  • MACRS depreciation for residential rental property uses straight-line method over 27.5 years. Commercial property uses 39 years. QIP (interior improvements to nonresidential property) uses 15 years and qualifies for bonus depreciation.
  • Depreciation recapture is taxed at a maximum of 25% under Section 1250 rules — higher than typical long-term capital gains rates. It applies even if you forgot to claim depreciation.
  • Rental losses are passive by default. They can only offset passive income, unless your AGI is under $100,000 (then up to $25,000 can offset ordinary income, phasing out by $150,000) or you qualify as a Real Estate Professional.
  • Real Estate Professional status requires 750+ hours in real property businesses AND that real estate accounts for more than 50% of all your work hours in the year. Detailed hourly logs are essential.
  • Short-term rentals (average stay ≤ 7 days) are treated as active businesses — Schedule C, not Schedule E; no passive loss limits, but profits face self-employment tax.
  • Vacation homes rented fewer than 15 days per year generate tax-free income with no deductions. Rented 15+ days, income is taxable and expenses must be allocated between rental and personal use.
  • The \(250,000/\)500,000 primary residence exclusion requires owning and living in the home as your primary residence for 2 of the last 5 years. Depreciation recapture from rental periods is not excludable.
  • 1031 like-kind exchanges defer all capital gains and depreciation recapture when you reinvest in another investment property. Strict rules: qualified intermediary required, 45-day identification window, 180-day closing window.
  • Installment sales spread gain recognition across multiple years as payments arrive. Use Form 6252. Depreciation recapture must be recognized in the year of sale regardless.
  • Schedule E reports rental income and expenses. Schedule D summarizes capital gains. Form 8949 itemizes each individual sale. All three work together for real estate transactions.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Pull last year's Schedule E. Confirm you claimed depreciation on all rental properties. If any year was missed, consult a tax professional about filing Form 3115 to catch up — you'll need it before you sell.

  2. Calculate your current depreciation deduction. Use the formula (Purchase Price - Land Value) / 27.5. Check your county assessor's website for the land-to-building value ratio if you don't have it.

  3. Check your AGI against the passive loss thresholds. If your AGI is under $100,000 and you actively manage your rental, your losses (up to $25,000) are currently deductible. If you're between $100,000 and $150,000, calculate your allowable loss using the phase-out formula. If you're over $150,000, your losses are suspended until you have passive income or sell the property.

  4. Count your real estate hours if you're trying to qualify as a Real Estate Professional. You need 750+ hours and more than 50% of your total work time in real estate. Start keeping a contemporaneous log now if you haven't already.

  5. Review your short-term rental setup. Calculate the average rental period across all Airbnb/VRBO bookings this year. If it's 7 days or fewer, confirm you're reporting on Schedule C and accounting for self-employment tax — not just assuming it's passive rental income.

  6. Plan for any property sale this year. If you're selling a rental, estimate your depreciation recapture liability (total depreciation taken × 25%). If you're considering a 1031 exchange, contact a qualified intermediary before you close — the QI must be in place before settlement.

  7. Check the primary residence exclusion for your home. Verify that your ownership and use periods satisfy the 2-of-5-year tests. If you previously rented the home, calculate your adjusted basis (reducing for depreciation claimed during rental periods) to determine whether your gain exceeds the exclusion.

  8. Document HELOC and home equity loan use. If you have a HELOC, trace the proceeds to confirm they were used to buy, build, or substantially improve the secured property. If proceeds were used elsewhere, stop deducting the interest as mortgage interest.

docs.title: 9. Advanced Strategies
docs.text: title: Chapter 9: Advanced Strategies & Loopholes description: Advanced legal tax minimization strategies including Backdoor Roth, S-Corp election, Augusta Rule, donor-advised funds, QOZ investing, NOL rules, cost segregation, and bonus depreciation. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-21 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter is for people who want to go beyond the basics and legally minimize taxes at a higher level. You'll learn the Backdoor Roth IRA and Mega Backdoor Roth for high earners, the Augusta Rule for renting your home tax-free, S-Corp election to reduce self-employment tax, donor-advised funds for charitable giving, Qualified Opportunity Zones for deferring capital gains, net operating loss (NOL) rules, cost segregation and bonus depreciation for real estate investors, Section 179 expensing, and strategies like hiring family members. Every strategy includes a plain-English explanation of who it applies to and what the real dollar savings look like.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 18 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Backdoor Roth IRA Strategy
  2. Mega Backdoor Roth
  3. Augusta Rule Section 280A
  4. Qualified Opportunity Zone
  5. Donor Advised Fund
  6. Charitable Remainder Trust
  7. S-Corp Election Strategy
  8. S-Corp Reasonable Salary

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 3: Legal Tax Reduction Strategies
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes
  • Chapter 7: Investments & Capital Gains Taxes
  • Chapter 8: Real Estate & Rental Property Taxes

docs.title: Introduction: The Tax Code Rewards the Informed
docs.text:
Most Americans leave significant money on the table every year — not because they're doing anything wrong, but because they don't know the rules. The strategies in this chapter are not loopholes in the pejorative sense. They are legal provisions written into the tax code, often deliberately, to encourage specific behaviors: saving for retirement, investing in distressed communities, supporting charities, starting businesses, and creating jobs. The IRS isn't hiding these rules. They're sitting in plain sight in the Internal Revenue Code, used every day by people who know where to look.

What separates this chapter from a basic tax primer is the practical focus on who each strategy applies to, what the actual dollar savings look like, and what can go wrong if you execute incorrectly. Each strategy has a qualifying population, a set of mechanics, and a failure mode. Understanding all three is the difference between a legal tax reduction and an audit red flag.

We'll move from retirement accounts and real estate to business structures to investments, covering all 18 concepts in a logical sequence. Some of these strategies require a CPA or attorney to implement. Others you can do yourself with the right knowledge and a little paperwork. The chapter will tell you which is which.

docs.title: Part 1: Retirement Account Strategies for High Earners
Backdoor Roth IRA Strategy
docs.text:
The Roth IRA is one of the best retirement accounts in the tax code: contributions grow tax-free and withdrawals in retirement are completely untaxed. The problem is the income limit. In 2025, if your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) exceeds $161,000 (single) or $240,000 (married filing jointly), you cannot contribute directly to a Roth IRA. The contribution phases out starting at $146,000 (single) and $230,000 (MFJ).

The Backdoor Roth IRA is a two-step workaround that high earners use to get money into a Roth regardless of income:

  1. Make a non-deductible contribution to a traditional IRA — $7,000 in 2025, or $8,000 if you're 50 or older. Because you're over the income limit for deductibility, you contribute after-tax dollars and file Form 8606 to document the non-deductible basis.
  2. Convert that traditional IRA balance to a Roth IRA. You can do this immediately — there is no waiting period required, though some advisors suggest waiting a few weeks to avoid the appearance of a "step transaction."

When you convert, you owe ordinary income tax on any pre-tax amounts in the IRA. If the only money in your traditional IRA is the $7,000 you just contributed after-tax, and it hasn't grown materially, the conversion is essentially tax-free.

The Pro-Rata Rule Will Bite You If You Have Other IRA Money

The backdoor Roth only works cleanly if you have zero pre-tax dollars in any traditional, SEP, or SIMPLE IRA. If you have a $93,000 pre-tax traditional IRA and you add $7,000 in after-tax money and then convert just $7,000, the IRS does not let you pick and choose which dollars you're converting. Under the pro-rata rule, the $7,000 conversion is treated as coming 93% from the pre-tax pool and 7% from your after-tax contribution. You'd owe tax on $6,510 of the conversion. The fix: roll your pre-tax IRA money into your employer's 401(k) plan before doing the backdoor Roth.

Form 8606 is mandatory. Every year you make a non-deductible IRA contribution, file Form 8606 to track your cumulative after-tax basis. Without this paper trail, you'll end up paying tax on money you already paid tax on when you eventually take distributions.

docs.title: Mega Backdoor Roth
docs.text:
If the $7,000 backdoor limit feels modest against a serious savings goal, the Mega Backdoor Roth pushes the envelope dramatically further. This strategy allows you to contribute significantly more to a Roth account by exploiting the gap between the standard 401(k) employee deferral limit and the overall Section 415 limit for 401(k) plans.

Here is how the math works in 2025:

  • Section 415 total limit: $70,000 per year
  • Standard employee deferral: $23,500 (or $31,000 if 50+)
  • Employer match: varies (say $10,000)
  • Gap available for after-tax contributions: $70,000 - $23,500 - \(10,000 = **\)36,500**

You contribute that $36,500 as after-tax (non-Roth) contributions to your 401(k). Then — and this is the critical piece — you convert those after-tax contributions to Roth either via an in-plan Roth conversion (converting within the 401(k) to its Roth account) or an in-service withdrawal (withdrawing the after-tax dollars while still employed and rolling them to a Roth IRA).

Two Requirements Your Plan Must Meet

The Mega Backdoor Roth only works if your 401(k) plan document allows both of these features:

  1. After-tax (non-Roth) contributions — many plans don't permit these
  2. In-service withdrawals or in-plan Roth conversions — allowing you to convert before you leave the employer

Check your Summary Plan Description (SPD) or ask your HR/benefits department. This strategy is common at larger companies and tech firms; less common at small businesses. If your plan doesn't allow it, you cannot use this strategy regardless of how much you want to.

The tax benefit: that $36,500 in after-tax contributions grows and — once converted to Roth — all future earnings come out completely tax-free in retirement. On a 20-year horizon, compounding tax-free can turn $36,500 of after-tax contributions into substantially more in retirement wealth than the same dollars in a taxable brokerage account.

docs.title: Augusta Rule Section 280A
docs.text:
Named for the city where the Masters golf tournament is held (local homeowners rent to spectators for enormous sums), the Augusta Rule lives in Section 280A(g) of the Internal Revenue Code and delivers a genuinely surprising result: if you rent your personal residence to your own business for 14 or fewer days per year, the rental income is completely tax-free to you personally and the rent payment is fully deductible by your business.

The mechanics: Your S-Corp or partnership pays you fair market rent to use your home for legitimate business purposes — board meetings, strategy retreats, client events.
