You deposit the rental income and never report it on your tax return. The business deducts the expense as an ordinary business cost.

Augusta Rule in Action

Dr. Chen runs her medical practice through an S-Corp. She holds 10 board of directors meetings at her home over the course of the year — documented with agendas, minutes, and attendance records. Comparable event space in her market rents for $1,500/day. She charges her S-Corp $15,000 in rent for those 10 days. The S-Corp deducts $15,000, reducing its taxable income (and her self-employment tax exposure). Dr. Chen receives $15,000 in rental income — which is completely excluded from her personal taxable income under Section 280A(g). Net tax benefit at a 32% marginal rate: approximately $4,800 in federal tax savings, plus state taxes.

Several requirements must be met precisely:

  • Rental must not exceed 14 days per year — day 15 blows the exclusion entirely
  • You must charge fair market rent (document this with comparable venue pricing, not just whatever number you want)
  • There must be a legitimate business purpose for each meeting (no "strategy retreat" that's actually a family vacation)
  • An S-Corp, partnership, or C-Corp structure works; a sole proprietor cannot pay rent to themselves
  • Keep documentation: rental agreement between you and the entity, meeting agendas and minutes, and evidence of fair market rate

docs.title: Qualified Opportunity Zone
docs.text:
When you sell a highly appreciated asset — stock, real estate, a business — and face a large capital gains tax bill, the Qualified Opportunity Zone (QOZ) program offers a structured way to defer and potentially reduce that tax.

The rules: Within 180 days of a capital gains-generating sale, you invest the gain (not the full proceeds — just the gain) in a Qualified Opportunity Zone Fund. In exchange:

  • The original capital gains tax is deferred until December 31, 2026, or until you sell your QOZ investment, whichever is earlier — meaning you'll owe that deferred tax when you file your 2026 return in 2027
  • If you hold the QOZ investment for 10 or more years, any appreciation in the QOZ fund itself is completely excluded from tax — a permanent exclusion, not just a deferral

The math on a large gain is compelling. Suppose you sold a rental property and recognized $500,000 in capital gains. Invest that $500,000 in a QOZ fund. Your tax is deferred (giving you use of those funds for 1–2 years). If the QOZ investment doubles to $1,000,000 over 10 years, that $500,000 of appreciation is completely tax-free. You will eventually owe the deferred tax on the original $500,000 gain, but the gains generated by the QOZ investment itself escape tax entirely.

QOZ Funds — What They Actually Invest In

Qualified Opportunity Zone Funds must invest at least 90% of their assets in Qualified Opportunity Zone Property — real estate or businesses located in designated low-income census tracts certified by the Treasury Department. There are over 8,700 such zones across the US. Investment quality varies enormously: some QOZ funds hold well-located urban redevelopment projects; others are in genuinely distressed areas with real investment risk. The tax benefit doesn't guarantee investment returns.

docs.title: Donor Advised Fund
docs.text:
If you give to charity and you're not using a Donor Advised Fund (DAF), you're almost certainly leaving money on the table. A DAF is a charitable account — offered by financial institutions like Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, and Vanguard Charitable — where you contribute assets, receive an immediate tax deduction, and then recommend grants to charities over time at your own pace.

The real power of a DAF is donating appreciated assets instead of cash:

  • You contribute appreciated stock (or real estate, or mutual fund shares) that you've held more than a year
  • You receive a charitable deduction for the full fair market value — up to 30% of your AGI for appreciated assets, with a 5-year carryforward for any excess
  • You avoid the capital gains tax that you would have owed if you had sold the asset and then donated the cash
  • The DAF sells the asset (tax-free inside the DAF) and the full proceeds are available to grant to qualified charities
Giving Method Deduction Capital Gains Tax Net to Charity Sell stock, donate cash FMV of cash You pay (up to 23.8%) FMV minus cap gains tax Donate appreciated stock to DAF Full FMV None Full FMV Donate cash directly Cash amount N/A Cash amount

The minimum to open a Fidelity Charitable DAF is $50. You can contribute any amount above that and let it sit invested until you're ready to recommend grants. This also enables bunching: in years when you have large deductible events (a big income year, a property sale), you can fund the DAF heavily to exceed the standard deduction threshold, then dole out grants to your favorite charities over multiple years.

docs.title: Charitable Remainder Trust
docs.text:
For those with very large appreciated assets and a desire for ongoing income, the Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) takes charitable giving to another level — but also to another level of complexity and cost.

Here is how a CRT works: You transfer appreciated assets (stock, real estate) into an irrevocable trust. The trust sells the assets without paying capital gains tax. The trust then pays you (or another named beneficiary) an income stream for life or a term of years — typically 5–8% of the trust's initial value annually. When you and any other income beneficiaries die (or the term ends), the remaining trust assets pass to designated charities.

Your tax benefits: - A charitable deduction in the year you fund the trust (based on the present value of the charity's remainder interest — usually 30–60% of the assets transferred) - No immediate capital gains tax on the appreciated assets (the trust handles the sale) - An income stream for life or term

The tradeoff: the assets in the CRT are gone from your estate permanently. This is an irrevocable decision. CRTs require an attorney to draft, a trustee to administer, and ongoing accounting. They make sense for assets exceeding \(500,000–\)1,000,000 in value where the owner is philanthropically motivated and values income more than passing the asset to heirs.

CRTs Are Not DIY — Use an Attorney

A Charitable Remainder Trust is one of the most powerful tax tools in this chapter and also one of the most complex. Improperly drafted trusts can lose their tax-exempt status, create unintended income recognition, or fail to qualify for the charitable deduction. Do not attempt this without an experienced estate planning attorney and a CPA who understands trust taxation.

docs.title: Part 4: S-Corp Strategies for the Self-Employed
S-Corp Election Strategy
docs.text:
If you're self-employed and earning meaningful net profit, the S-Corp election is likely the highest-impact tax strategy available to you. Here's the core problem it solves: as a sole proprietor, every dollar of net profit is subject to self-employment (SE) tax of 15.3% (on the first $176,100) in addition to ordinary income tax. On $100,000 of net profit, that's $15,300 in SE tax alone. The S-Corp election changes the taxation structure to limit how much of your income is subject to SE tax.

The mechanism: You file Form 2553 with the IRS to elect S-Corp status for your LLC or corporation. Once elected, you become both an employee and a shareholder of the business. The S-Corp pays you a W-2 salary for your services. The remaining profit — after salary, business expenses, and payroll taxes — is distributed to you as an S-Corp distribution. The critical difference: S-Corp distributions are not subject to self-employment tax.

The S-Corp tax structure:

\[ \text{SE Tax Savings} = (\text{Net Profit} - \text{Reasonable Salary}) \times 15.3\% \]

Example: You net $150,000 as a sole proprietor. Your SE tax is approximately $21,226. As an S-Corp with a $75,000 salary, you pay SE tax only on the \(75,000 salary portion (≈\)11,475). The \(75,000 distribution has no SE tax. SE tax savings: approximately **\)9,751** per year. Annual S-Corp costs (payroll service + state fees): roughly \(1,000–\)2,000. Net benefit: ~\(7,000–\)9,000.

docs.title: S-Corp Reasonable Salary
docs.text:
The IRS requires that S-Corp owner-employees pay themselves a reasonable salary — one that's commensurate with what a similarly qualified employee would earn for the same work in the same market. This rule exists precisely because of the strategy above: if you could pay yourself a $1 salary and take $199,999 as a distribution, no one would pay SE tax. The IRS will reclassify unreasonably low wages as W-2 income and assess back payroll taxes, penalties, and interest.

How to determine a reasonable salary:

  • Check Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Employment data for your occupation and geographic market
  • Search job postings on LinkedIn, Indeed, or Glassdoor for comparable roles
  • Document your finding in writing and keep it in your corporate records

A commonly used rule of thumb: pay yourself 40–60% of net profit as salary, with the remainder as distributions. This is not a safe harbor — it's a starting point for analysis. A surgeon netting $800,000 who pays herself a $50,000 salary will attract IRS scrutiny. A marketing consultant netting $80,000 who pays herself $45,000 is on much safer ground.

Document Your Salary Decision

Each year, document in your corporate minutes how you determined your W-2 salary. List the sources you consulted (BLS data, comparable job postings), the figures you found, and why your chosen salary is reasonable. This paper trail is invaluable if the IRS ever questions your compensation.

docs.title: S-Corp vs Sole Proprietor Tax
docs.text:
The S-Corp election is not free money — it comes with administrative costs that need to exceed the tax savings for the strategy to make sense. Here is a realistic comparison:

Sole Proprietor S-Corp SE/Payroll tax 15.3% on all net profit 15.3% only on W-2 salary Federal income tax Same Same Additional costs None Payroll service (\(500–\)1,500/yr), state fees (\(0–\)800/yr), accountant (\(500–\)2,000/yr) Tax forms Schedule C + SE Form 1120-S, W-2, payroll filings Breakeven net profit — ~\(40,000–\)60,000 Ideal for Net profit under $40k Net profit over $60k

Below roughly $40,000 in net profit, the administrative overhead of running an S-Corp typically exceeds the SE tax savings. Above \(60,000–\)80,000, the savings become substantial. At $120,000 net profit, the annual tax benefit commonly ranges from \(8,000–\)12,000 — far exceeding costs. The crossover point depends on your specific state, salary level, and actual costs.

docs.title: Health Reimbursement Account
docs.text:
One often-overlooked benefit of the S-Corp structure is the ability to use a Health Reimbursement Arrangement (HRA) to pay health insurance premiums and medical expenses tax-free. Two types are relevant:

  • ICHRA (Individual Coverage HRA): An S-Corp can reimburse owner-employees and other employees for individual health insurance premiums purchased on the open market. Reimbursements are tax-free to the employee (deductible to the S-Corp). This is particularly valuable for S-Corp owners who cannot participate in an employer group plan.
  • QSEHRA (Qualified Small Employer HRA): Available to employers with fewer than 50 full-time employees who don't offer a group health plan. Allows reimbursement up to $6,350/year (single) or $12,800/year (family) in 2025 for individual premiums and qualifying medical expenses.

Without an HRA structure, an S-Corp owner-employee's health insurance premiums are included in W-2 wages (creating an income inclusion) but are deductible on the owner's personal return as a self-employed health insurance deduction. The ICHRA approach can be more tax-efficient for owners and employees alike.

docs.title: Diagram: S-Corp vs. Sole Proprietor Tax Savings Calculator
docs.text:
S-Corp vs. Sole Proprietor Tax Savings Calculator — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Given self-employment net profit, estimated S-Corp salary, and annual S-Corp costs, the learner can calculate their net annual tax savings from electing S-Corp status versus operating as a sole proprietor.

Layout: - Top bar: "S-Corp vs. Sole Proprietor: Find Your Breakeven" with subtitle - Left panel (45%): Inputs - Slider: Annual Net Business Profit (\(0–\)400,000, step $5,000, default $100,000) - Slider: S-Corp Salary (as % of profit, 30%–80%, step 5%, default 50%) - Number input: Annual S-Corp costs (payroll + state + accountant, default \(2,500) - Dropdown: Filing status (Single / MFJ, default Single) - Right panel (55%): Results - Card 1 — "Sole Proprietor SE Tax": net profit × 92.35% × 15.3% - Card 2 — "S-Corp Payroll Tax": salary × 15.3% - Card 3 — "Gross SE Tax Savings": Card 1 minus Card 2 - Card 4 — "Annual S-Corp Costs": from input - Card 5 — "Net Annual Savings" (highlighted): Gross savings minus costs; green if positive, red if negative - Card 6 — "Breakeven Profit Level": calculate and display the net profit at which savings equal costs - Line chart below cards: x-axis = net profit (\)0–$400k), y-axis = net savings; shows sole proprietor SE tax line, S-Corp net savings line, and marks breakeven crossover point

Behavior: - All values recalculate on any input change - Salary slider shows dollar amount in addition to percentage (e.g., "50% = $50,000") - SE tax formula: (net profit × 0.9235) × 0.153 for sole proprietor; (salary × 0.153) for S-Corp - If net savings is negative: show red card with message "S-Corp costs exceed savings at this profit level" - Breakeven displayed as a vertical dotted line on the chart - Hover tooltip on chart shows exact values at each data point

Colors: - Sole proprietor line: #B71C1C (red/cost) - S-Corp savings line: #2E7D32 (green/savings) - Breakeven marker: #F57F17 (amber) - Positive savings card: #E8F5E9 background with #2E7D32 text - Negative savings card: #FFEBEE background with #B71C1C text - Background: #FAFAFA, card panels: white with 1px #E0E0E0 border

Implementation: HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

docs.title: Qualified Small Business Stock
docs.text: If you are investing in startups or early-stage companies — or founding one yourself — Section 1202 of the Internal Revenue Code offers one of the most extraordinary tax exclusions on the books. Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) can make the entire gain from selling your startup shares permanently tax-free.

docs.title: Section 1202 Exclusion
docs.text:
Under Section 1202, if you acquire stock in a Qualified Small Business and hold it for more than 5 years, you can exclude up to 100% of the capital gain from federal income tax when you sell. The exclusion cap is the greater of:

  • $10 million (lifetime per company), or
  • 10 times your adjusted basis in the stock

To qualify, the company and your investment must meet all of these requirements:

  • C-Corporation only — S-Corps, LLCs, and partnerships do not qualify
  • Stock must be originally issued to you (not bought in the secondary market)
  • Company's aggregate gross assets must be $50 million or less at the time of issuance (and immediately after)
  • Company must be an active qualified trade or business — not a service business in health, law, engineering, financial services, restaurants, hotels, or farming
  • You must acquire the stock in exchange for money, property, or services

Section 1202 Exclusion — Real Dollar Impact

You invest $100,000 in a seed-stage tech startup in 2020. You hold for 5 years. In 2025, you sell your stake for $2,100,000 — a $2,000,000 gain. Normally that gain would generate roughly $476,000 in federal capital gains tax (23.8% including NIIT). Under Section 1202, 100% of the \(2,000,000 gain is excluded from federal income tax. Tax owed: **\)0** federally. State tax treatment varies — California, for instance, does not conform to Section 1202, so California residents owe the full state capital gains tax. But the federal savings alone are extraordinary.

The 10x basis limit means the exclusion scales with investment size. Invest $500,000, and you can exclude up to $5,000,000 of gain (or $10 million, whichever is greater). Some planning strategies involve multiple family members each holding QSBS from the same issuance, multiplying the available exclusion.

docs.title: Net Operating Loss (NOL)
docs.text:
When your business deductions exceed your business income in a tax year, you have a Net Operating Loss. An NOL is not just a bad year to forget — it is a tax asset with real future value. The NOL reduces your taxable income in the year it occurs (often to zero or below), and the unused portion can be applied to future profitable years.

The basic calculation:

\[ \text{NOL} = \text{Business Deductions} - \text{Business Income} \quad \text{(when result is negative)} \]

Example: You run a consulting firm. In 2025, you have $30,000 in revenue but $85,000 in deductible business expenses (including salaries, rent, and equipment). Your NOL is $55,000. That $55,000 doesn't just disappear — it carries forward to future years.

docs.title: NOL Carryforward Rules
docs.text:
The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA) significantly changed NOL rules starting in 2018. Under current law:

  • No carryback: With limited exceptions (farming losses can be carried back 2 years), NOLs generated in 2018 and later cannot be carried back to prior years. You cannot go back and amend a profitable prior year's return.
  • Indefinite carryforward: Your NOL carries forward indefinitely — there's no 20-year expiration window like the old rules.
  • 80% limitation: In any future year, your NOL deduction cannot exceed 80% of your taxable income in that year.

The 80% limitation means you cannot use NOL carryforwards to zero out your taxable income in a profitable year if the NOL is large enough. You will always owe at least 20% of your taxable income even if you have a giant NOL bank.

\[ \text{NOL Deduction in Year} = \min(\text{NOL Carryforward}, \; 0.80 \times \text{Taxable Income}) \]

Example continued: In 2026, your consulting firm earns $120,000 net profit. You have a $55,000 NOL carryforward. The 80% limit: $120,000 × 0.80 = \(96,000. Your NOL carryforward (\)55,000) is less than $96,000, so you can deduct the full $55,000. Taxable income for 2026: $120,000 - $55,000 = $65,000. The NOL is fully used.

NOLs from Real Estate

Real estate owners can generate significant NOLs from depreciation and other deductions exceeding rental income. If you qualify as a Real Estate Professional (see Chapter 8), those losses are not passive and can create genuine NOLs. If you do not qualify, real estate losses remain passive and suspended — they don't create an NOL. Track this distinction carefully.

docs.title: Part 7: Real Estate Tax Acceleration Strategies
Cost Segregation Study
docs.text:
When you buy a commercial or residential rental property, the IRS presumes the entire building depreciates over 27.5 years (residential) or 39 years (commercial) using straight-line depreciation. But many components of a building qualify for shorter depreciation lives — 5, 7, or 15 years — if properly identified and reclassified. A cost segregation study is an engineering analysis that does exactly that: it identifies which portions of a building qualify for accelerated depreciation.

What gets reclassified:

  • 5-year property: Carpeting, appliances, certain fixtures, decorative lighting, specialty flooring
  • 7-year property: Office furniture and fixtures attached to the building
  • 15-year property: Land improvements (parking lots, landscaping, fencing, sidewalks), outdoor lighting

The impact is enormous. Instead of depreciating the entire \(1,000,000 building over 39 years (\)25,641/year), a cost segregation study might reclassify $200,000 as 5-year property and $100,000 as 15-year property. Those components can be depreciated — or bonus depreciated — much faster, generating large deductions in the early years of ownership.

Cost segregation studies typically cost \(5,000–\)15,000 for a commercial property. They generally make economic sense for properties with a purchase price or improvement cost of $500,000 or more. The tax deferral (or permanent savings if you're in a lower bracket when the recapture happens) easily justifies the cost on larger properties.

docs.title: Bonus Depreciation 2025 Rules
docs.text:
Bonus depreciation allows you to immediately deduct a large percentage of the cost of qualified property in the year it is placed in service, rather than spreading the depreciation over its recovery period. In 2025, the bonus depreciation rate is 40% — meaning 40% of the cost of qualifying property can be deducted immediately, with the remaining 60% depreciated on the normal MACRS schedule.

This is significantly reduced from the 100% bonus depreciation that was available in 2022, but it remains a meaningful acceleration tool. The phase-down schedule under current law:

Year Bonus Depreciation Rate 2022 100% 2023 80% 2024 60% 2025 40% 2026 20% 2027+ 0% (unless Congress acts)

Qualifying property includes tangible property with a MACRS recovery period of 20 years or less (5-year, 7-year, and 15-year property), certain computer software, and qualified improvement property (QIP). Critically, bonus depreciation applies to both new and used property — you don't have to buy a brand-new asset to benefit.

Combine Cost Segregation with Bonus Depreciation

The real power move for real estate investors is stacking these two strategies. A cost segregation study reclassifies portions of a building from 27.5/39-year property to 5-year, 7-year, or 15-year property. Once reclassified, those components are eligible for 40% bonus depreciation in 2025. On a $2,000,000 commercial building with $400,000 reclassified as 5-year property, the 40% bonus depreciation generates a $160,000 immediate deduction — on top of the normal depreciation on the remaining $1,600,000. This can create a paper loss large enough to significantly reduce taxable income in the year of acquisition.

docs.title: Section 179 Deduction
docs.text:
The Section 179 deduction allows you to immediately expense the full cost of qualifying business property in the year you place it in service — instead of depreciating it over multiple years. In 2025, the Section 179 limit is $1,160,000. For every dollar of property placed in service above $2,890,000, the deduction phases out dollar-for-dollar.

Key differences between Section 179 and bonus depreciation:

  • Section 179 cannot create a loss. If your business has $50,000 of taxable income and $200,000 of Section 179 property, you can only deduct $50,000 under Section 179 (the rest carries over). Bonus depreciation has no such restriction — it can generate a loss.
  • Section 179 is elected property by property. You choose which assets to expense under Section 179. You can mix and match Section 179 and bonus depreciation on different assets in the same year.
  • Section 179 applies to a broader category including off-the-shelf computer software and certain qualified real property (improvements to nonresidential buildings).

A practical approach: use Section 179 first to reduce taxable income to near zero, then apply bonus depreciation to the remaining qualifying property if you want to push into a loss.

docs.title: Hire Family Members Strategy
docs.text:
Paying family members for legitimate work in your business is one of the simplest and most effective income-shifting strategies available to business owners. The tax logic: you deduct the wages as a business expense, reducing your taxable income at your marginal rate (potentially 22–37%). Your family member — who may be in a much lower bracket or have no income — pays taxes (if any) at their lower rate or owes nothing at all.

Children as employees in a sole proprietorship or partnership (not an S-Corp or C-Corp) have a particularly attractive tax benefit:

  • Children under 18 employed by their parent's sole proprietorship or partnership are exempt from FICA (Social Security and Medicare taxes) — saving both the employer's 7.65% and the employee's 7.65%
  • In 2025, a child can earn up to the standard deduction ($15,000) and owe zero federal income tax on that income
  • The wages must be for real work at fair market wages — designing your social media graphics at $25/hour is legitimate; "answering phones" at $50,000/year is not

Example: You own a sole proprietorship netting $180,000. You hire your 16-year-old daughter to photograph products, manage your Instagram account, and perform data entry — 15 hours per week during summer, 5 hours per week during the school year. You pay her $14,000 for the year at market rates. You deduct $14,000, reducing your taxable income and self-employment tax base. She earns $14,000, which is under the standard deduction — she owes zero income tax and zero FICA. Total family tax savings: $14,000 × (your marginal rate + SE tax rate). At a combined 37% + 14.13% effective rate, that's over $7,000 in tax savings on legitimate wages.

Spouses can also be employed in your business, but the FICA exemption does not apply to spouses — you must withhold and remit payroll taxes on their wages. However, employing your spouse allows them to participate in your business's retirement plan, potentially adding thousands more to tax-deferred retirement savings.

Documentation is Not Optional

The IRS scrutinizes family employment arrangements. For every family member you employ:

  • Maintain time records showing hours worked and tasks performed
  • Pay market-rate wages (document comparable pay with job listings)
  • Pay via check or ACH — not cash
  • Issue a W-2 at year-end
  • Do not pay the child and then take the money back (circular transactions destroy the deduction)

An undocumented or obviously inflated family wage is an audit risk. A well-documented, legitimate arrangement is bulletproof.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
Every strategy in this chapter is legal, used by millions of taxpayers, and well within the IRS's own rules — but none of them are automatic. They require deliberate action, correct execution, and in many cases professional help. Here is what to remember:

  • Backdoor Roth IRA: A two-step process — non-deductible traditional IRA contribution followed by Roth conversion — available to high earners who exceed direct Roth contribution income limits. Pro-rata rule applies if you have other pre-tax IRA funds. Form 8606 is mandatory every year.
  • Mega Backdoor Roth: After-tax 401(k) contributions up to the $70,000 Section 415 limit, converted to Roth inside the plan or via in-service withdrawal. Requires your plan to allow both after-tax contributions and conversions.
  • Augusta Rule: Rent your home to your business for up to 14 days per year. Income is tax-free to you; deductible by the S-Corp or partnership. Fair market rent and business purpose documentation are required.
  • Qualified Opportunity Zone: Invest capital gains in a QOZ fund within 180 days of a sale. Defers the original gain until 2026; appreciation in the QOZ investment is tax-free if held 10+ years.
  • Donor Advised Fund: Contribute appreciated assets for an immediate deduction at full FMV, avoid capital gains tax, and recommend grants to charities over time. The DAF pays no tax when it sells the assets.
  • Charitable Remainder Trust: Transfer appreciated assets into an irrevocable trust, avoid immediate capital gains, receive an income stream, and get a partial charitable deduction. Complex — requires an attorney.
  • S-Corp Election: Reduces self-employment tax by splitting net profit into W-2 salary (subject to SE tax) and S-Corp distributions (not subject to SE tax). Makes economic sense at roughly \(40,000–\)60,000+ in net profit. File Form 2553.
  • S-Corp Reasonable Salary: The IRS requires your W-2 salary to reflect fair market compensation for your services. Document your salary determination with BLS data or comparable job listings. Typical rule of thumb: 40–60% of net profit.
  • S-Corp vs. Sole Proprietor: Administrative costs of S-Corp (payroll service, state fees, accountant) typically run \(1,500–\)4,000/year. Break-even is around \(40,000–\)60,000 in net profit.
  • Health Reimbursement Account: ICHRA allows S-Corp owners to be reimbursed tax-free for individual health insurance premiums. QSEHRA covers small employers under 50 employees.
  • Qualified Small Business Stock / Section 1202: Up to 100% exclusion of capital gains (capped at $10 million or 10x basis) on sale of C-Corp stock held 5+ years, acquired at original issuance, with gross assets under $50M at issuance. Excludes service businesses in several fields.
  • Net Operating Loss: When business deductions exceed income, the deficit is an NOL. Under post-TCJA rules, NOLs carry forward indefinitely but are limited to 80% of taxable income in any carryforward year. No carryback (except farming).
  • Cost Segregation Study: An engineering analysis that reclassifies building components from 27.5/39-year life to 5, 7, or 15 years, dramatically accelerating depreciation. Worth the \(5,000–\)15,000 study cost on properties over $500,000.
  • Bonus Depreciation 2025: 40% immediate deduction on qualifying property (20-year or shorter recovery period, new or used) placed in service in 2025. Phasing to 20% in 2026 and 0% in 2027 under current law.
  • Section 179: Expense up to $1,160,000 of qualifying business property in 2025. Cannot create a loss (unlike bonus depreciation). Phase-out starts at $2,890,000 of property placed in service.
  • Hire Family Members: Pay children and spouses legitimate wages for real work. Children under 18 in a sole proprietorship or partnership are exempt from FICA. Children earning under the $15,000 standard deduction owe zero income tax. Document everything.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Check your Roth IRA eligibility. Look up your 2025 MAGI. If you're above $146,000 (single) or $230,000 (MFJ), you cannot contribute directly to a Roth IRA. Set up the backdoor Roth by opening a traditional IRA, making a non-deductible contribution, and converting immediately. Confirm you have no pre-tax IRA balances first.

  2. Ask HR if your 401(k) plan allows after-tax contributions and in-plan Roth conversions. Request the Summary Plan Description. If both are allowed, calculate the gap to the $70,000 total limit and start contributing after-tax to execute the Mega Backdoor Roth.

  3. If you own a business and hold meetings at home, calendar 10–14 business events this year. Document each with a written agenda, meeting minutes, and attendance log. Research comparable event space rental rates in your market. Have your S-Corp or partnership pay you fair market rent and issue a rental agreement.

  4. If you sold an asset with a large capital gain in 2025, check the 180-day window for QOZ investment. Count from the date of sale. If you're within the window, research QOZ funds and evaluate whether the deferral — and potential 10-year exclusion — makes sense for your situation.

  5. If you give to charity regularly, open a Donor Advised Fund. Fidelity Charitable has a $50 minimum. In a high-income year, contribute appreciated stock (not cash) to the DAF in a lump sum, claim the deduction now, and recommend grants to your chosen charities over the next several years.

  6. If you are self-employed with net profit above $60,000 and you are not an S-Corp, run the numbers. Estimate your SE tax savings using the formula: (Net Profit - Reasonable Salary) × 15.3%. Compare to estimated S-Corp costs in your state. If the savings exceed the costs, consult with a CPA about filing Form 2553. The election deadline for S-Corp status generally requires filing by March 15 of the year the election is to take effect.

  7. If you own a commercial or rental property worth more than $500,000, get a cost segregation quote. Ask a cost segregation firm for a free preliminary analysis showing the estimated reclassification and projected tax benefit. With 40% bonus depreciation available in 2025, this year may be the most advantageous time to commission a study before the bonus rate drops further.

  8. If you have children who do legitimate work in your business, set up payroll. Establish a simple payroll system (even Gusto or QuickBooks Payroll for one employee), pay market-rate wages via check or ACH, and issue a W-2 at year-end. Keep time logs. Do not pay more than $15,000 unless the work justifies it — stay under the standard deduction to eliminate the child's income tax liability.

docs.title: 10. State Taxes
docs.text: title: Chapter 10: State Taxes Overview description: How state income taxes work — no-tax states, multi-state filing, domicile vs. residency, reciprocity agreements, remote worker rules, and state conformity to federal law. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-23 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers how state income taxes work alongside federal taxes and the key differences between states. You'll learn which 9 states have no income tax (and what to watch out for), how to file in multiple states if you live or work across state lines, the difference between domicile and residency for tax purposes, reciprocity agreements between neighboring states, and special rules for remote workers. Also covered: state-specific deductions, conformity to federal tax law, and how to make state estimated tax payments.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 12 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. State Income Tax Overview
  2. No-Income-Tax States List
  3. Multi-State Filing Rules
  4. State Domicile vs Residency
  5. State Tax Reciprocity
  6. State-Specific Deductions
  7. State Standard Deduction
  8. Conformity to Federal Tax Law
  9. State Estimated Tax Payments
  10. Remote Worker State Tax Rules
  11. Moving to a No-Tax State
  12. State Tax Refund Taxability

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes

docs.title: State Income Tax Overview
docs.text:
Your federal tax return is just the beginning. Every year, most Americans file a second (or third, or fourth) return — to their state. State income taxes are collected separately from the IRS by each state's own revenue department, and the rules, rates, and forms are entirely their own. Federal and state taxes are independent systems that happen to share some of the same underlying numbers.

Here's how the two systems connect: your state return typically starts with your federal Adjusted Gross Income (AGI), then makes its own additions and subtractions to arrive at your state taxable income. From there, the state applies its own tax rates, credits, and deductions. The result — what you owe or what gets refunded — is completely separate from your federal tax bill.

Think of it this way: the federal government sets the foundation, and your state builds its own house on top of it. Some states build a modest ranch; California builds a mansion with a 13.3% top rate.

The SALT Cap — Where Federal and State Taxes Collide

The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017 capped the federal deduction for State and Local Taxes (SALT) at $10,000 per return (the same cap applies whether you're single or married filing jointly). This means if you pay $8,000 in state income taxes and $5,000 in property taxes, you can only deduct $10,000 on your federal Schedule A — not $13,000. High-tax states like New York, New Jersey, and California lobbied hard against this cap because it effectively raised the federal tax burden for their residents.

docs.title: No-Income-Tax States List
docs.text:
Nine states impose no broad-based individual income tax. If you live in one of these states and your income comes from wages, you're in luck — no state income tax return to file.

State No Tax On Watch Out For Alaska All income No state income tax; no sales tax either Florida All income Property taxes are significant in many counties Nevada All income Sales tax up to 8.375% in some counties New Hampshire Wages & salaries Investment income (dividends/interest) still taxed South Dakota All income No state income tax Tennessee Wages & salaries Investment income still taxed (Hall Tax repealed 2021) Texas All income Property taxes are among the highest in the nation Washington All income 7% capital gains tax on gains over $262,000/year Wyoming All income No state income tax or capital gains tax

New Hampshire and Tennessee Are Not Fully Tax-Free

As of 2025, both NH and TN tax investment income — specifically dividends and interest — so if you have a large investment portfolio, don't assume you'll escape all state tax just by living there. New Hampshire's interest and dividends tax is being phased out and is scheduled for full repeal, but verify the current status before filing.

Washington State's Capital Gains Tax

Washington has no income tax, but passed a 7% capital gains tax on long-term gains exceeding $262,000 per year. This caught many tech workers and investors off guard when it took effect. The tax applies to gains from stocks, bonds, and other capital assets — but notably excludes real estate. If you sold company stock and your gains topped $262,000, you owe Washington state money even though there's "no income tax."

docs.title: Multi-State Filing Rules
docs.text:
Life doesn't stay inside one state's borders. If you earned income in more than one state during the year, you generally have to file a return in each state where you earned that income. The rules break down into three filing statuses that states use:

  • Resident return: Filed in your home state (your state of domicile or statutory residence) on all your worldwide income.
  • Nonresident return: Filed in a state where you earned income but don't live. You're only taxed on the income earned within that state.
  • Part-year resident return: Filed in a state where you lived for part of the year (for example, if you moved mid-year). You're taxed on all income earned while you were a resident, plus any income from in-state sources during the nonresident portion.

The practical math: you'll often pay tax to both states on the same income. To prevent true double-taxation, your home state typically offers a credit for taxes paid to other states. The credit is usually the lesser of (a) what you actually paid to the other state, or (b) what your home state would have charged on that same income. You don't get a windfall — you pay the higher of the two rates — but you don't pay both states in full.

\[ \text{Credit for taxes paid to another state} = \min\!\left(\text{Tax paid to other state},\ \frac{\text{Out-of-state income}}{\text{Total income}} \times \text{Home state tax}\right) \]

Multi-State Filing: Marcus Works in Two States

Marcus lives in New Jersey and commutes into New York City four days a week; he works remotely from home (NJ) one day a week. His total salary is \(120,000. New York will tax the 80% he earned there (\)96,000); New Jersey will tax his full $120,000 as a resident, then give him a credit for the tax he paid to New York on that $96,000. He files two returns: a NY nonresident return and an NJ resident return.

docs.title: State Domicile vs. Residency
docs.text:
These two words sound interchangeable, but to tax authorities they are very different — and confusing the two can cost you a lot of money.

Domicile is your permanent legal home — the place you intend to return to whenever you're away. You can only have one domicile at a time. It doesn't change just because you spend a lot of time somewhere else. If you grew up in Ohio, still have your driver's license there, and your parents' house is your "permanent address," Ohio may argue that's still your domicile even if you've been renting an apartment in California for three years.

Statutory residency is different. Most states define you as a resident — for tax purposes — if you spend 183 or more days in that state during the year, regardless of where your domicile is. This is sometimes called the "183-day rule," and it can trigger dual residency: two states each claiming you as a full resident and each taxing your worldwide income. The credit for taxes paid to other states reduces the sting, but it doesn't always eliminate it entirely, especially when the two states have different rates, different deductions, or different treatment of specific income types.

Factors courts and auditors look at when determining domicile:

  • Where is your driver's license and vehicle registered?
  • Where are you registered to vote?
  • Where do you keep your primary bank accounts and safe deposit box?
  • Where is your primary doctor, dentist, and attorney?
  • Where do your children attend school?
  • Where do you spend the most time, and where do you spend the holidays?
  • Where do you list as your home address on legal documents, passports, and tax returns?

The Domicile Test Is About Intent, Not Just Days

Spending 200 days in Florida doesn't automatically make you a Florida domiciliary if your "closest connections" are still in New York. Conversely, you can establish Florida domicile even if you spend some time in New York — as long as you've taken affirmative steps to change your domicile and your facts support it. Document everything: the date you got your Florida license, updated your voter registration, opened a Florida bank account, and transferred your medical records.

docs.title: State Tax Reciprocity
docs.text:
Here's a welcome simplification for millions of commuters: reciprocity agreements between neighboring states allow you to pay income tax only to your home state, even if you work across the border. Without reciprocity, you'd file two returns and claim a credit; with it, you file only one.

Common reciprocity agreements (2025):

States in Agreement How It Works Pennsylvania / New Jersey PA residents working in NJ (and vice versa) pay only to their home state Maryland / DC / Virginia Residents of any one who work in another pay only to their home state Indiana and IL, IA, KY, MI, WI Indiana residents working in those states (and vice versa) file only at home Virginia / DC / MD / PA / WV / KY / NC VA residents working in these states stay on their VA return Wisconsin / IL, IN, KY, MI Same cross-border simplification

The mechanics: if you live in New Jersey and work in Pennsylvania, give your employer a Pennsylvania Certificate of Nonresidence (Form REV-419). Your employer stops withholding Pennsylvania tax from your paycheck and withholds New Jersey tax instead. At tax time, you file only a New Jersey return. Clean and simple.

Reciprocity Only Covers Wages and Salaries

Reciprocity agreements generally apply only to employee compensation — wages, salaries, tips. If you have other income from the neighboring state (rental income, self-employment income from clients there, etc.), reciprocity doesn't help. You'll still need to file a nonresident return in that state for that other income.

docs.title: Remote Worker State Tax Rules
docs.text:
Remote work scrambled the traditional rule that you pay state taxes where your employer is located. The modern default rule is simpler: you owe state income tax to the state where you actually perform your work. If you live and work from home in Colorado, Colorado gets the tax — even if your employer's HQ is in New York.

But there's a major exception: the "convenience of the employer" doctrine, applied by a handful of states. Under this rule, if you work remotely for a reason that benefits you (convenience) rather than your employer (necessity), the state where your employer is based can tax your income as if you worked there in person.

States that apply the convenience doctrine (as of 2025):

  • New York
  • New Jersey
  • Pennsylvania
  • Connecticut
  • Delaware
  • Arkansas

New York's Convenience Rule Is Aggressively Enforced

If your employer is headquartered in New York, New York will tax your remote income unless you can demonstrate that your remote work location is a necessity of the employer — for example, the employer requires it because they have no suitable office for you, or your role requires proximity to clients outside New York. Simply preferring to work from home in Texas does not satisfy the necessity test. New York has audited thousands of remote workers who stopped commuting to NYC offices during and after the pandemic.

docs.title: Diagram: Remote Worker State Tax Explainer
docs.text:
Remote Worker State Tax Explainer — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Calculate

Learning Objective: Given a remote worker's home state, employer state, salary, and work-from-home percentage, the learner can determine which state(s) owe taxes, whether the convenience doctrine applies, and calculate the approximate tax owed to each state.

Layout: - Left panel (inputs): dropdowns for Home State and Employer State; slider for Salary (\(30,000–\)300,000 in $5,000 steps); slider for Work-from-Home % (0–100%); toggle for "Employer requires remote work (necessity)" yes/no - Right panel (output): a two-column results card showing State 1 (home state) and State 2 (employer state), each with: taxable income allocated, applicable tax rate, estimated tax owed; a summary line showing total state tax and whether a convenience doctrine warning applies - Below results: a plain-English explanation paragraph that updates dynamically ("Since your employer is in New York and you work remotely for your own convenience, New York taxes 100% of your income...")

Inputs: - Home State: dropdown of all 50 states (defaults to Colorado) - Employer State: dropdown of all 50 states (defaults to New York) - Salary: slider, \(30,000–\)300,000 - Work-from-Home %: slider, 0–100% (represents % of days worked remotely) - Necessity toggle: yes/no boolean

Outputs: - Home state taxable income = Salary × (WFH% / 100) when convenience doctrine does NOT apply; = 0 when convenience doctrine applies and employer state claims 100% - Employer state taxable income = Salary × (1 − WFH%/100) under normal rules; = Salary × 100% when convenience doctrine applies and necessity = No - Tax for each state = income allocated × that state's flat or effective marginal rate (use simplified flat rates for the sim: CA 9.3%, NY 6.85%, TX 0%, CO 4.4%, NJ 6.37%, PA 3.07%, etc.) - Total state tax = sum of both states' tax - Convenience doctrine warning: appears in red when Employer State ∈ {NY, NJ, PA, CT, DE, AR} AND WFH% > 0 AND necessity = No

Colors: - Background: white (#FFFFFF) - Home state card: light blue (#E8F4FD) - Employer state card: light green (#E8F8F0) - Warning box: light red background (#FDE8E8) with dark red text (#C0392B) - Sliders: blue accent (#2980B9) - Typography: system sans-serif, 14px base

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

docs.title: Moving to a No-Tax State
docs.text:
Moving to a state with no income tax sounds like a slam dunk. And it can be — but only if you actually change your domicile in the eyes of the law. States (especially California and New York) have revenue departments that actively audit former residents who claim to have moved but still maintain significant ties to the old state.

The steps to establish a new domicile are not complicated, but they must be deliberate and documented:

  1. Get a driver's license in the new state and surrender your old one.
  2. Register to vote in the new state.
  3. Update your address on bank accounts, brokerage accounts, credit cards, and your passport.
  4. Transfer your medical records to a doctor in the new state.
  5. Execute a new will or update your estate documents listing your new state as your domicile.
  6. Physically move — count your days carefully. Keep a diary or use a calendar app with location data if you travel a lot.
  7. Notify your former state of your change of address on your final part-year return.

California is particularly aggressive. If you earn income from California sources after moving (rental property in CA, CA clients for your consulting business, stock options that vested while you were a California employee), California may still have a claim on that income. They even have Form 3840 specifically for allocating deferred compensation income earned in California but paid out after you've moved away.

The 'Closest Connections' Test

Courts resolve domicile disputes using a totality-of-facts test sometimes called the "closest connections" test. No single factor is decisive. A person who spends 200 days in Florida but keeps their New York apartment, their New York doctor, their New York country club membership, and sends their kids to New York schools will likely lose a New York domicile audit. The goal is for your life to genuinely reflect your new home — not just your tax strategy.

docs.title: State-Specific Deductions
docs.text:
Once you've determined your home state and allocated income correctly, you need to figure out what deductions your state actually allows. This is where significant differences emerge. States diverge from the federal tax code in three common ways:

Things federal allows that states often don't:

  • The $10,000 SALT deduction (states don't deduplicate this — you're not deducting your own state's taxes on the state return)
  • The federal Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction (Section 199A) — many states don't allow it
  • Mortgage interest deduction phase-outs — some states have stricter limits
  • Student loan interest deduction — some states have their own version; others don't

Things states allow that federal doesn't:

  • Deductions for contributions to that state's 529 college savings plan (very common — usually \(5,000–\)10,000 per contributor)
  • Retirement income exclusions — many states fully or partially exempt Social Security, military pensions, and government pensions from state tax
  • Property tax "circuit breaker" credits for seniors and low-income homeowners
  • Renter's credits — some states offer a credit based on rent paid as a proxy for property tax

529 Deductions Are State-Specific

If you contribute to your state's own 529 plan, you usually get a state income tax deduction. But if you contribute to another state's 529 plan (even if it has better investment options), you typically lose the state deduction. A few states (including Pennsylvania, Arizona, Kansas, and Missouri) allow deductions for contributions to any 529 plan, regardless of state. Check your state's rules before choosing a 529.

docs.title: State Standard Deduction
docs.text:
Every state that has an income tax makes its own choice about standard deductions. Some are generous; some are minimal; a handful of states have no standard deduction at all and only allow itemized deductions.

A sample comparison for Tax Year 2025:

State Single Standard Deduction Married Filing Jointly Federal $14,600 $29,200 California $5,202 $10,404 New York $8,000 $16,050 Illinois None (itemized only) None (itemized only) Pennsylvania None (flat rate on gross income) None Colorado Conforms to federal ($14,600) Conforms to federal ($29,200) Texas No income tax No income tax New Jersey $1,000 $2,000 Oregon $2,745 $5,495 Georgia $5,400 $7,100

Notice that states like New Jersey and Oregon offer standard deductions that are a small fraction of the federal amount. This means that even if you take the federal standard deduction, you may end up itemizing on your state return (or having a much smaller deduction), which increases your state taxable income — and your state tax bill.

Pennsylvania operates differently from most: it uses a flat rate applied to gross income with very limited deductions at all, so the concept of a "standard deduction" doesn't really apply in the same way.

docs.title: Conformity to Federal Tax Law
docs.text:
When Congress changes the federal tax code — passes a new law, creates a new deduction, modifies a credit — states don't automatically update their own tax laws. How quickly (or whether) a state adopts those changes depends on its conformity approach:

Rolling conformity: The state automatically adopts whatever the federal tax code says as of the current date. If Congress passes a new law in October, rolling conformity states apply it immediately. Examples: New York, California (with some exceptions), and most states.

Static conformity: The state's tax code is frozen as of a specific date. Any federal changes after that date don't apply unless the state legislature acts separately to update the conformity date. Example: Virginia's conformity date is updated periodically but is not automatic.

Selective conformity: The state picks and chooses which federal provisions to adopt, creating a patchwork. Some federal deductions are adopted; others are explicitly rejected. Example: California selectively conforms — it adopted most federal changes but does not conform to the federal QBI deduction, the increased bonus depreciation rules, or certain TCJA provisions.

Why Conformity Matters for Your Return

If your state uses static conformity and its conformity date precedes a federal change that benefited you (say, a new above-the-line deduction), you can't take that deduction on your state return. This requires an addition to your state taxable income — a line where you add back the federal deduction that your state doesn't recognize. Tax software handles this automatically for most common differences, but if you're preparing your return manually or have unusual items, check your state's conformity status.

docs.title: State Estimated Tax Payments
docs.text:
If you have income that isn't subject to withholding — freelance income, investment income, rental income, business income — you're responsible for paying your state estimated taxes quarterly, just as you do federally. Most states mirror the federal system closely, with a few important differences.

The general rule: if you expect to owe more than a certain threshold (typically $500 to $1,000, depending on the state) after withholding, you should make quarterly estimated payments to avoid a penalty.

Typical state estimated tax due dates (most states follow federal):

  • Q1: April 15
  • Q2: June 15
  • Q3: September 15
  • Q4: January 15 of the following year

States Don't Always Match Federal Due Dates

Some states have moved their own estimated tax due dates to differ slightly from federal. California, for example, front-loads payments: 30% is due April 15, 40% is due June 15, 0% in September, and 30% is due January 15. This is the opposite of the equal-quarterly approach at the federal level and catches many California taxpayers off guard. Always verify your state's specific schedule.

To calculate what you owe each quarter, states generally use the same safe harbor rules as the IRS: pay either 100% of last year's state tax liability (110% if your income is high), or 90% of the current year's actual liability. Meet either threshold and you avoid underpayment penalties even if you end up owing more at filing.

\[ \text{Quarterly state estimated payment} = \frac{\text{Prior year state tax}}{4} \quad \text{(safe harbor method)} \]

Most states allow online payment through their revenue department's website, and many accept credit cards (though often with a convenience fee). Some states also allow you to set up automatic debits for your quarterly payments, similar to IRS Direct Pay.

docs.title: State Tax Refund Taxability
docs.text:
Here's a scenario many people overlook: you received a state income tax refund last year. Is that refund taxable income on your federal return this year? The answer is: it depends on whether you itemized.

The logic: if you deducted your state income taxes on last year's federal Schedule A (itemized deductions), you got a tax benefit from that payment. When the state sends some of it back, you have to report the refund as income — because you essentially over-deducted. You benefited from a deduction you didn't fully "earn."

On the other hand, if you took the standard deduction last year, you never deducted your state taxes at all. The refund is simply the state returning your own money — no federal tax benefit was taken, so no federal tax is owed on the refund.

The IRS formalized this with the tax benefit rule: you only include the state refund in income to the extent you actually received a tax benefit from the deduction. This is reported on Schedule 1, Line 1 of your federal Form 1040 as "Taxable refunds, credits, or offsets of state and local income taxes."

State Refund Taxability: Two Scenarios

Scenario A — Itemizer: Last year, Priya itemized her deductions and deducted $9,000 in California state taxes on her federal return. This year, California sent her a $1,200 refund (she overpaid). That $1,200 is taxable income on her current-year federal return because she got a full deduction for the $9,000 last year.

Scenario B — Standard Deduction: Last year, David took the standard deduction ($14,600) on his federal return, so he deducted nothing for his state taxes. This year, Ohio sent him a $600 refund. David does not report the $600 as income — he never deducted those state taxes, so there's no benefit to claw back.

One more wrinkle: the tax benefit rule limits taxability. If deducting your state taxes didn't actually reduce your federal tax (because, say, AMT applied or the SALT cap limited your deduction to $10,000 anyway), then the refund attributable to the non-deducted portion is not taxable. This gets complicated quickly, but tax software walks you through the worksheet automatically.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• State income taxes are a completely separate system from federal taxes, but they typically start with your federal AGI as a baseline and make their own adjustments.
  • Nine states have no broad-based income tax, but "no income tax" doesn't always mean zero state tax obligation — watch for capital gains taxes (Washington), investment income taxes (New Hampshire), and high property taxes (Texas).
  • If you earn income in more than one state, you generally file a resident return in your home state and nonresident returns in each other state. Your home state typically gives you a credit for taxes paid to other states.
  • Domicile (your permanent legal home) and statutory residency (183+ days in a state) are different concepts. Getting both wrong can result in two states taxing your full worldwide income.
  • Reciprocity agreements between neighboring states let you pay income tax only to your home state on wages — use them by filing a nonresidence certificate with your employer.
  • Remote workers owe income tax to the state where they actually work — except in states (NY, NJ, PA, CT, DE, AR) that apply the "convenience of the employer" doctrine, which can tax remote workers as if they worked in the employer's state.
  • Moving to a no-tax state requires real steps: new driver's license, voter registration, updated bank accounts, changed medical records, and a genuine shift in your center of life. Keep records.
  • States often differ sharply from federal law on deductions — some don't allow the QBI deduction, some offer 529 plan deductions, and state standard deductions are usually much lower than federal.
  • States use rolling, static, or selective conformity to adopt federal tax law changes; know your state's approach so you don't miss required additions or subtractions.
  • If you have self-employment, freelance, or investment income, you likely owe quarterly estimated taxes to your state — due dates and safe harbor percentages vary by state.
  • If you itemized last year and received a state tax refund, that refund may be taxable income on your current federal return under the tax benefit rule.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Look up your state's income tax rate at your state's department of revenue website. If you live in a flat-tax state (Pennsylvania at 3.07%, Illinois at 4.95%, Colorado at 4.4%), a simple calculation tells you roughly what you owe. If your state has brackets, find where your income falls.
  2. Check whether you need to file in more than one state. Review your W-2s for Box 15 (state) and Box 16 (state wages). If you see two different states listed — or two W-2s from the same employer with different state withholding — you likely need to file a nonresident return.
  3. Verify your domicile. If you've recently moved, or if you split time between states, audit your own facts: Where is your driver's license? Where are you registered to vote? Where is your primary doctor? If the answer isn't clearly your intended home state, take the steps to correct it now — before a state auditor does the analysis for you.
  4. Check for a reciprocity agreement if you commute across state lines for work. Search "[your state] reciprocity agreement [neighboring state]" — if one exists, submit a nonresidence certificate to your employer to stop unnecessary withholding in the non-home state.
  5. Remote workers: find out your employer's HQ state. If it's in New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, Connecticut, Delaware, or Arkansas, research whether the convenience of the employer doctrine applies to your situation. Talk to a tax professional if your remote work arrangement is complex.
  6. Find your state's standard deduction. Compare it to your itemized deductions on your state return — especially if you pay significant mortgage interest or make charitable contributions. You may itemize on your state return even when you take the federal standard deduction.
  7. Look up your state's 529 contribution deduction if you have children or are saving for education. If your state offers a deduction for its own 529 plan, contributing before December 31 could meaningfully reduce your state tax bill.
  8. Calculate whether you need to make state estimated tax payments. If you freelance, have rental income, or significant investment income not subject to withholding, log in to your state's revenue portal and set up quarterly payments. Underpayment penalties at the state level are real and avoidable.
  9. Retrieve last year's state tax refund amount from your records (it's on the state refund check or direct deposit, or on the prior-year state return). If you itemized last year, compare that refund to what you reported on last year's Schedule 1 — you may need to include it in income this year.
  10. If you moved this year, prepare to file part-year returns in both states. Gather documentation of your exact move date, your new address confirmation, and any income earned in each state during each residency period.

docs.title: 11. IRS Audits
docs.text: title: Chapter 11: IRS Audits & How to Avoid Them description: How IRS audits work, what triggers them, how to respond, documentation best practices, CP2000 notices, penalty abatement, and offers in compromise. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-23 version: 0.04

docs.title: Chapter 11: IRS Audits & How to Avoid Them
Summary
docs.text: This chapter takes the fear out of IRS audits by explaining exactly how they work, what triggers them, and what to do if you receive one. You'll learn the three types of audits (correspondence, office, field), the most common red flags that attract IRS attention, how to document your deductions so you can defend them, how long you need to keep tax records (and why), what a CP2000 notice means and how to respond, and your options if you owe more than you can pay — including penalty abatement and offers in compromise.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 13 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. IRS Audit Types Overview
  2. Correspondence Audit
  3. Office Audit Procedures
  4. Field Audit Procedures
  5. Audit Trigger Red Flags
  6. Home Office Audit Risk
  7. IRS Audit Response Steps
  8. Documentation Best Practices
  9. Tax Record Retention Rules
  10. Statute of Limitations IRS
  11. IRS Notice CP2000
  12. Penalty Abatement Request
  13. Offer in Compromise IRS

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Tax Situation
  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & Self-Employment Taxes

docs.title: The Reality of IRS Audits
docs.text:
The word "audit" triggers more anxiety than almost any other word in personal finance. People picture interrogation rooms, aggressive agents, and financial ruin. The reality is far more mundane — and far more manageable. In 2024, the IRS audited roughly 0.4% of all individual returns, or about 1 in every 250 filers. Most of those "audits" were letters asking about a single line item, not a visit from a federal agent.

This chapter gives you the same calm, factual briefing that a good CPA gives clients before tax season: what an audit actually is, what causes them, what to do if you get one, and how to build records now that make the whole process painless. By the end of this chapter, you'll understand audits well enough that the anxiety evaporates — replaced by a simple set of habits.

docs.title: IRS Audit Types Overview
docs.text: The IRS has three ways to examine a return, and they are not equivalent. Think of them as three very different conversations on a spectrum from "mild inconvenience" to "serious legal matter."

docs.title: Correspondence Audit
docs.text:
A correspondence audit is a letter — nothing more. The IRS mails you a notice asking you to verify or explain one or two specific items on your return. This is by far the most common form: correspondence audits account for roughly 75% of all IRS audits. You respond by mail (or sometimes through an online portal) with copies of your supporting documents. You never go to an IRS office; you never speak to an agent face-to-face.

Common triggers for a correspondence audit include a mismatch between a 1099 the IRS received and what you reported, a charitable contribution deduction that seems large for your income level, or a question about whether you qualify for a specific credit. The process is transactional: you send proof, the IRS reviews it, and the matter is either closed or escalated.

Correspondence Audit Playbook

When you receive an IRS letter, read it carefully before panicking. The notice will clearly state exactly what the IRS is questioning and the deadline to respond (usually 30–60 days). Respond only to what is asked. Enclose copies of relevant documents — never originals. Send your response via certified mail and keep the tracking receipt.

docs.title: Office Audit Procedures
docs.text:
An office audit (also called a desk audit) requires you to visit an IRS Taxpayer Assistance Center in person. The IRS schedules a specific appointment and sends you a list of the items under examination. These are more comprehensive than correspondence audits — a single deduction category such as your Schedule C business expenses or charitable contributions might be reviewed in detail.

Office audits are less common than correspondence audits but more common than field audits. They are typically triggered when the IRS wants to see documents that are difficult to evaluate by mail, or when there are several related issues on the return. You are entitled to bring a representative — a CPA, enrolled agent, or tax attorney — and for an office audit, having professional representation is worth considering seriously.

docs.title: Field Audit Procedures
docs.text:
A field audit is the audit most people picture — an IRS revenue agent visits your home, your business, or your representative's office. Field audits are rare and are reserved for the most complex situations: typically business owners with complicated books, very high-income filers, or cases where the IRS suspects something more than a math error. The agent will examine your records in detail over one or more visits.

If you receive a notice scheduling a field audit, hire a tax professional immediately. Do not meet with an IRS revenue agent alone. You have the legal right to representation, and exercising that right is not an admission of wrongdoing — it is the rational choice.

docs.title: Audit Type Comparison
docs.text: Feature Correspondence Office Field How common ~75% of audits Moderate Rare Where it happens By mail IRS office Your location Who initiates contact Letter/notice Letter with appointment Letter with appointment Scope 1–2 specific items Several related items Full books and records Do you need representation? Often handle yourself Strongly consider it Yes — get a pro Typical resolution time 3–6 months 6–12 months 12–24 months

docs.title: Audit Trigger Red Flags
docs.text:
The IRS uses statistical models (called the Discriminant Inventory Function, or DIF) to score every return for audit risk. Returns that score far outside the norm for their income level get a second look. Think of it as a statistical outlier detector — your return gets flagged not because the IRS thinks you're dishonest, but because your numbers look unusual compared to everyone else at your income level.

Here are the most common patterns that elevate audit risk:

  • Large charitable deductions relative to income. Donating 40% of your income is unusual. The IRS knows what people at your income level typically donate.
  • Consistent business losses year after year. Losing money for 3–5 consecutive years raises the question of whether this is a legitimate business or a hobby (the "hobby loss" rules under IRC §183).
  • Very high round-number deductions. Claiming exactly $10,000 in business meals or precisely $5,000 in vehicle expenses looks estimated, not documented.
  • Cash-intensive businesses. Restaurants, salons, contractors, and similar businesses that handle a lot of cash are scrutinized more heavily because income underreporting is common in these industries.
  • Unreported crypto transactions. The IRS receives 1099s from exchanges and has made cryptocurrency compliance a priority since 2019. Every sale, exchange, or use of crypto to purchase goods is a taxable event.
  • Missing 1099s. If a client pays you $3,200 and issues you a 1099-NEC, but that income doesn't appear on your return, the IRS's automated system will catch it.
  • EITC claims. The Earned Income Tax Credit has a high error rate, so returns claiming it are audited at a higher rate — especially at lower income levels.
  • Math errors. Not a "red flag" in the traditional sense, but mathematical errors generate automatic notices. Tax software largely eliminates this risk.

The Home Office Deduction Myth

You may have heard that claiming a home office deduction is an automatic audit trigger. This is outdated and overstated. The IRS does scrutinize home office deductions, but if you legitimately work from home and meet the exclusive use test (the space is used regularly and exclusively for business), you are entitled to the deduction. Using the simplified method ($5 per square foot, up to 300 sq ft = max $1,500) reduces scrutiny further. The real risk is claiming a home office you don't actually use exclusively for business.

docs.title: Home Office Audit Risk
docs.text:
The home office deduction deserves its own section because it's both widely misunderstood and legitimately valuable for self-employed filers. The exclusive use test is the key: the space must be used regularly and exclusively for business. A dedicated room that also has a guest bed does not qualify. A corner of the living room where the kids do homework does not qualify. A room you use only for business — even a small one — does qualify.

There are two calculation methods:

  • Simplified method: $5 per square foot of dedicated business space, up to 300 square feet (maximum deduction: $1,500/year).
  • Regular (actual expenses) method: You calculate the percentage of your home used for business — typically by dividing the office square footage by total home square footage — and apply that percentage to your actual home expenses (rent or mortgage interest, utilities, insurance, repairs).
\[ \text{Business Use Percentage} = \frac{\text{Office Square Footage}}{\text{Total Home Square Footage}} \times 100 \]

If your office is 200 square feet and your home is 2,000 square feet, your business use percentage is 10%. You can then deduct 10% of your eligible home expenses. The simplified method is easier to document and reduces IRS scrutiny, but the actual expenses method often yields a larger deduction if your home costs are high.

docs.title: IRS Audit Response Steps
docs.text:
Receiving an IRS audit notice does not mean you did anything wrong. It means the IRS has a question. Your job is to answer it — no more, no less. Here is the process in order:

  1. Read the notice carefully. Identify exactly what is being questioned and the response deadline. Do not miss the deadline; missing it limits your options significantly.
  2. Gather your documentation. Pull together receipts, bank statements, canceled checks, or whatever supports the item in question. You need to be able to prove what you claimed.
  3. Respond only to what is asked. This is critical. Do not volunteer information about other parts of your return. Do not send more documents than requested. Each additional document is an opportunity for the examiner to find new questions.
  4. Send copies, not originals. The IRS can lose documents. Keep your originals; send certified copies.
  5. Send via certified mail. Get a tracking number. This creates proof that you responded and when.
  6. Consider professional representation. For correspondence audits covering one simple item, you may handle it yourself. For office and field audits — or any situation involving multiple years or multiple issues — hire a CPA, enrolled agent, or tax attorney.
  7. Know your appeal rights. If you disagree with the IRS's findings, you have the right to appeal to the IRS Office of Appeals, and beyond that to Tax Court, U.S. District Court, or the Court of Federal Claims.

You Have Rights

The IRS Taxpayer Bill of Rights (Publication 1) guarantees you the right to be informed, the right to quality service, the right to pay no more than the correct amount of tax, the right to challenge the IRS's position, the right to appeal, and more. These are real legal rights — not just aspirational statements. If you feel an IRS examiner is acting improperly, you can contact the Taxpayer Advocate Service (TAS) for independent assistance.

docs.title: Documentation Best Practices
docs.text:
The best defense against an audit is great records. "Contemporaneous records" — records created at the time of the expense, not reconstructed later from memory — carry far more weight with the IRS than a spreadsheet you made the night before sending your audit response.

Here are the core practices:

  • Keep receipts for anything over $75. The IRS requires written receipts for business expense deductions above this threshold. For items under $75, a log entry is technically sufficient, but a receipt is better.
  • Maintain a mileage log. For vehicle deductions, you need the date, destination, business purpose, and miles for each trip. Apps like MileIQ or Everlance automate this. A contemporaneous log is required — recreating it at year-end from calendar entries is permissible but weaker.
  • Use a dedicated business account. Mixing personal and business expenses is the single biggest record-keeping mistake self-employed filers make. A separate business checking account and business credit card create a clean, auditable paper trail automatically.
  • Document the business purpose. For meals, write the names of attendees and the business topic discussed on the receipt (or in a note linked to the receipt). "Lunch" is not a business purpose. "Lunch with Sarah Chen — discussed Q3 marketing contract" is.
  • Keep bank statements and credit card statements. These provide corroborating evidence even when a receipt is lost.
  • Back up digital records. Store copies of receipts in a cloud service (Google Drive, Dropbox, etc.) and locally. A house fire that destroys paper records does not excuse you from proving your deductions.

docs.title: Diagram: Audit Risk Assessment Tool
docs.text:
Audit Risk Assessment Tool — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Apply Bloom Verb: Assess

Learning Objective: Given a set of filer characteristics and deduction patterns, the learner can estimate their relative audit risk and identify which specific factors are elevating that risk.

Layout

Two-panel layout (stacked on mobile, side-by-side on desktop):

  • Left panel: Input controls (sliders and checkboxes)
  • Right panel: Audit risk gauge (semi-circular speedometer), a color-coded risk level label, and a bulleted list of contributing risk factors

Inputs (left panel)

All inputs update the gauge in real time with no submit button.

  1. Gross income slider: $0 – $2,000,000 (step $5,000; default $75,000). Label: "Gross Income"
  2. Charitable deductions as % of income slider: 0% – 60% (step 1%; default 3%). Label: "Charitable Deductions (% of income)"
  3. Checkboxes (each adds to risk score):
  4. "Self-employed / Schedule C filer"
  5. "Claimed home office deduction"
  6. "Business showed a loss this year"
  7. "Business has shown losses 3+ consecutive years"
  8. "Cash-intensive business (restaurant, contractor, etc.)"
  9. "Reported cryptocurrency transactions"
  10. "Claimed Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)"
  11. "Had unreported or missing 1099 income"

Risk Score Calculation

Base risk score starts at 10 (representing the 0.4% baseline audit rate, scaled 0–100).

  • Income > $500,000: +20 points
  • Income > $1,000,000: +40 points (replaces the +20)
  • Income < $25,000: +10 points (EITC population)
  • Charitable % of income > 10%: +15 points
  • Charitable % of income > 25%: +30 points (replaces +15)
  • Each checkbox checked: +8 points
  • "Business losses 3+ years" checkbox: +15 points (replaces the +8 for that item)

Cap the displayed score at 95.

Output (right panel)

Semi-circular gauge from 0–100, needle points to current score.

Color zones: - 0–25: Green ("Low Risk") - 26–50: Yellow ("Moderate Risk") - 51–75: Orange ("Elevated Risk") - 76–100: Red ("High Risk")

Below the gauge, display a risk level label (e.g., "Elevated Risk — 63/100") in the matching zone color.

Below the label, display a dynamic bulleted list titled "Factors elevating your risk:" that lists only the factors currently contributing points above the baseline. If no additional factors apply (score = 10), display "No significant risk factors identified. You're in good shape."

Footer note (below both panels)

Small gray italic text: "This tool estimates relative audit risk for educational purposes only. Actual IRS audit selection uses proprietary statistical models. A high score does not mean you will be audited; a low score does not guarantee you won't be."

Colors and Typography

  • Background: white (#FFFFFF)
  • Panel borders: light gray (#E0E0E0), 1px, rounded corners
  • Gauge background arc: light gray (#F0F0F0)
  • Gauge fill arc: transitions through green → yellow → orange → red based on score
  • Needle: dark charcoal (#333333)
  • Labels: system sans-serif, 14px body, 16px panel headers
  • Risk level label: bold, 20px, color matches zone

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript
