docs.title: Tax Record Retention Rules
docs.text:
How long do you need to keep tax documents? The answer depends on which document and what risk you're protecting against. The short version: keep your actual tax returns forever and keep your supporting documents for 3–7 years.

Here is the full breakdown:

Document Type How Long to Keep Why Filed tax returns (1040, etc.) Forever Proof of filing; needed for amended returns, Social Security, mortgage applications W-2s and 1099s 7 years Supports the return; matches IRS document-matching window Business expense receipts 3–7 years 3 years standard; 6 years if income underreported 25%+ Charitable contribution records 3 years from filing Supports Schedule A deduction Investment purchase records (cost basis) Until you sell + 3 years You need cost basis to calculate capital gains Real property purchase records Until you sell + 3 years Needed to calculate gain on sale and home sale exclusion Home improvement records Until you sell + 3 years Improvements increase your basis, reducing your gain Employment tax records 4 years If you had employees Records related to a loss carryforward Until loss is used + 3 years Carryforward losses link back to original loss year

docs.title: Statute of Limitations IRS
docs.text:
The statute of limitations is the IRS's deadline for auditing you. Once it expires, the IRS generally cannot assess additional tax for that year. Understanding these windows is important for deciding how long to keep records and how much risk exposure you actually have.

The three key windows are:

  • 3 years — The standard statute of limitations. The IRS has 3 years from the later of the return filing date or the return due date (April 15) to audit your return and assess additional tax. This covers the vast majority of situations.
  • 6 years — If you underreported your gross income by more than 25%, the IRS gets 6 years. This is not about lying — it could be a legitimate misunderstanding about whether income was taxable. The threshold is gross income, not taxable income.
  • No limit — If you filed a fraudulent return, or if you never filed a return at all, the statute of limitations never runs. There is no expiration date on fraud.
\[ \text{Standard Limitation Period} = \text{Filing Date (or Due Date, whichever is later)} + 3 \text{ years} \]

The Clock Starts When You File

If you file your 2024 return on February 10, 2025, the 3-year clock starts on April 15, 2025 (the due date) — not February 10. If you file late (say, on October 10, 2025, after an extension), the clock starts on October 10, 2025. Filing early does not shorten your exposure window; it starts on the due date at the earliest.

docs.title: IRS Notice CP2000
docs.text:
The CP2000 is one of the most misunderstood notices the IRS sends. It is not an audit. It is an automated notice generated by the IRS's document-matching system, which compares the income reported on your return to the 1099s, W-2s, and other information returns filed by payers. When there is a discrepancy, the IRS sends a CP2000 proposing a change to your return.

Common reasons you might receive a CP2000:

  • You forgot to report a 1099-NEC from a client
  • You received a 1099-B from selling stock but did not report it (or reported it differently)
  • You received a 1099-INT or 1099-DIV that you missed
  • A 1099 was issued in error or contains the wrong amount

The notice will show you the income the IRS believes you underreported, the additional tax it proposes, interest charges, and possibly a penalty. You have 60 days to respond.

Your three options:

  1. Agree — If the notice is correct, sign the response form and pay the additional amount (or set up a payment plan).
  2. Disagree — If you already reported this income correctly, or the 1099 is wrong, write a response explaining your position and attach supporting documents (your return pages, original 1099, corrected 1099, etc.).
  3. Partially agree — If part of the notice is correct and part is not, you can agree to the portion that is right and dispute the rest.

CP2000 in Practice

Marcus did some freelance design work in 2024 and received a $4,200 payment from a client. He reported the income on his Schedule C. However, the client's bookkeeper accidentally issued a 1099-NEC for $42,000 — an extra zero. The IRS's CP2000 proposes that Marcus owes tax on an additional $37,800 of income. Marcus responds with a copy of his bank statement showing the actual $4,200 deposit, a letter explaining the discrepancy, and a request that the client issue a corrected 1099. The IRS reviews his response and closes the case with no additional tax owed.

Do not ignore a CP2000. Ignoring it converts the proposed change into an actual assessment, and then you owe the money — plus interest that has been accruing the entire time.

docs.title: Penalty Abatement Request
docs.text:
If you end up owing a penalty — for filing late, paying late, or failing to make estimated tax payments — you may not have to pay the full amount. The IRS offers a program called First-Time Penalty Abatement (FTA) that can eliminate certain penalties entirely.

Who qualifies: - You have filed all required returns (or have a valid extension) - You have paid, or arranged to pay, all taxes owed - You have no penalties in the prior three tax years (clean compliance history)

Which penalties FTA covers: - Failure-to-file penalty (5% of unpaid tax per month, up to 25%) - Failure-to-pay penalty (0.5% of unpaid tax per month, up to 25%) - Failure to deposit (for business payroll taxes)

Which penalties FTA does NOT cover: - Estimated tax penalties (use Form 2210 or the reasonable cause exception instead) - Accuracy-related penalties - Fraud penalties

How to request FTA:

The easiest method is to call the IRS at 1-800-829-1040 and ask for penalty abatement under the First-Time Abatement policy. Have your tax year, the penalty amount, and your compliance history ready. The IRS representative can approve it on the phone for smaller amounts. For larger penalties, you may need to submit a written request. You can also request abatement after you receive an IRS bill for the penalty — you do not have to request it preemptively.

If you don't qualify for FTA because you had a prior penalty, you may still request abatement based on reasonable cause — circumstances beyond your control such as serious illness, a natural disaster, or the death of a family member. This requires a written explanation and is evaluated case by case.

docs.title: Offer in Compromise IRS
docs.text:
If you owe the IRS more than you can reasonably pay — not just "more than is comfortable," but genuinely more than you can pay given your income and assets — an Offer in Compromise (OIC) allows you to settle your tax debt for less than the full amount owed.

The IRS accepts an OIC when one of three conditions applies:

  • Doubt as to collectibility — You don't have enough income or assets to pay the debt in full before the collection statute expires (typically 10 years from assessment). This is the most common basis.
  • Doubt as to liability — There is genuine dispute about whether you actually owe the amount assessed.
  • Effective tax administration — You could technically pay the full amount, but doing so would create severe economic hardship or be fundamentally unfair given exceptional circumstances.

The OIC process:

  1. Check eligibility using the IRS's free OIC Pre-Qualifier tool at irs.gov before applying.
  2. Complete Form 656 (Offer in Compromise) and Form 433-A (OIC) (Collection Information Statement).
  3. Submit an application fee of $205 (waived if your income is at or below the federal poverty guidelines).
  4. Choose a payment structure: lump sum (20% of offer amount upfront, balance within 5 months of acceptance) or periodic payment (pay in installments while the IRS considers your offer).
  5. Wait. The IRS typically takes 6–12 months to evaluate an OIC. During this time, collection activity is suspended.

The IRS's acceptance rate for OICs is roughly 40%. The offers that are accepted tend to be well-documented, realistic (not lowball offers with no financial basis), and submitted by people who genuinely cannot pay the full debt.

OIC Red Flags to Avoid

Be skeptical of companies advertising "settle your IRS debt for pennies on the dollar." While OICs are legitimate, many tax relief companies charge high upfront fees for applications that have little chance of approval. Before paying anyone, use the IRS's free Pre-Qualifier tool and consider a free consultation with a nonprofit credit counselor or a fee-only tax professional. You can file an OIC yourself — the IRS process is open to anyone.

The IRS calculates its Reasonable Collection Potential (RCP) to determine the minimum offer it will accept:

\[ \text{RCP} = \text{Net Realizable Value of Assets} + \text{Future Income} \times \text{Multiplier} \]

Where the multiplier is 12 (for lump sum offers) or 24 (for periodic payment offers), and "future income" means your monthly disposable income after allowable living expenses. Your offer needs to at least equal the RCP.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The IRS audits about 0.4% of individual returns — roughly 1 in 250. Most audits are correspondence audits resolved by mail.
  • The three audit types — correspondence, office, and field — vary enormously in seriousness. Treat each appropriately.
  • Common audit triggers include large charitable deductions, business losses multiple years in a row, cash-intensive businesses, unreported crypto, and missing 1099s.
  • The home office deduction is legitimate if you meet the exclusive use test. The simplified method reduces scrutiny.
  • If audited, respond to exactly what is asked — no more. Send copies, use certified mail, and consider professional representation for anything beyond a simple correspondence audit.
  • Keep contemporaneous records: receipts over $75, mileage logs, business purpose notes on meals, dedicated business accounts.
  • Keep your actual tax returns forever; supporting documents for 3–7 years depending on the situation.
  • The standard statute of limitations for an IRS audit is 3 years from the filing date (or due date, whichever is later). It extends to 6 years if you underreported income by 25%+, and there is no limit for fraud.
  • A CP2000 notice is not an audit — it is a proposed change from the IRS's automated matching system. Respond within 60 days: agree, disagree, or partially agree.
  • If you have a clean 3-year compliance history, First-Time Penalty Abatement (FTA) can eliminate failure-to-file and failure-to-pay penalties with a phone call.
  • An Offer in Compromise lets you settle tax debt for less than owed if you genuinely can't pay. The acceptance rate is about 40%. Use the IRS's free Pre-Qualifier tool before applying.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Locate your last three years of filed tax returns. If you don't have copies, you can request a transcript at irs.gov/transcripts for free. File them somewhere permanent.
  2. Create a record-retention folder (physical or digital) for the current tax year. Label it with the year and start dropping receipts, bank statements, and 1099s into it as they arrive.
  3. Set up a mileage tracking app if you use your vehicle for business. The deduction is valuable; the log is required. Pick one app and use it consistently from day one of the tax year.
  4. Open a separate business checking account and credit card if you are self-employed and don't already have one. This single step eliminates most record-keeping headaches.
  5. Review last year's return for the common triggers listed in this chapter. Do your charitable deductions look unusual relative to your income? Do you have multiple years of Schedule C losses? If so, make sure your documentation is solid.
  6. If you received a CP2000 or other IRS notice and have not responded, respond immediately. Call the IRS (1-800-829-1040) or work with a tax professional. Every day you wait, interest accrues.
  7. Check if you qualify for First-Time Penalty Abatement if you have a penalty on an existing tax balance. Call 1-800-829-1040, ask for the Penalty Abatement department, and reference the FTA policy.
  8. If you have a tax debt you cannot pay, visit the IRS OIC Pre-Qualifier at irs.gov/oic before paying any private company to evaluate your situation.

docs.title: 12. Free Filing Resources
docs.text: title: Chapter 12: Free Filing Resources & Tools description: Free tax filing options including IRS Free File, Direct File, VITA, AARP Tax-Aide, and free software — plus when to hire a CPA, tax professional types, IRS Power of Attorney, and IRS Online Account access. generated_by: claude skill chapter-content-generator date: 2026-02-23 version: 0.04

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This final chapter is your practical guide to actually getting your taxes filed without spending money on software or professionals — if your situation qualifies. You'll learn the real difference between IRS Free File, IRS Direct File, VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance), and AARP Tax Aide, how to compare free tax software options, and — most importantly — how to know when your situation is complex enough that hiring a CPA or enrolled agent is worth the cost. Also covered: IRS Power of Attorney and how to give someone else permission to speak with the IRS on your behalf, and how to access your IRS online account to see your tax records, transcripts, and payment history.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 10 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. IRS Free File Program
  2. IRS Direct File Tool
  3. VITA Program Overview
  4. AARP Tax Aide Program
  5. Free Tax Software Options
  6. TurboTax Free Edition
  7. When to Hire a CPA
  8. Tax Professional Types
  9. Power of Attorney IRS
  10. IRS Online Account Access

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return Step by Step
  • Chapter 9: Advanced Strategies & Loopholes
  • Chapter 11: IRS Audits & How to Avoid Them

docs.title: Introduction: You Don't Have to Pay to File
docs.text:
Here's a fact that surprises a lot of people: the IRS has negotiated deals, built its own tools, and funded volunteer networks — all so that most Americans can file their federal return for free. The tax software industry has worked hard to obscure this, but the options are real, well-supported, and used by millions of people every year.

This chapter is your map. We'll walk through every major free filing option, give you an honest comparison, and then shift gears to a topic that's just as important: knowing when "free" isn't the right answer. Not every tax situation belongs in self-service software. Some situations — a new S-Corp, a divorce, an inheritance, rental properties — call for a licensed professional, and paying for that expertise is a smart investment, not a sign of failure.

By the end of this chapter, you'll know exactly where to go to file for free if your situation qualifies, what warning signs mean you need a professional, how to check your IRS records online any time, and how to give someone else official permission to deal with the IRS on your behalf. Let's finish strong.

docs.title: The IRS Free File Program
docs.text:
The IRS Free File Program is a formal partnership between the IRS and a coalition of private tax software companies. Under the agreement, these companies offer free federal tax preparation and e-filing to taxpayers whose Adjusted Gross Income (AGI) falls at or below $84,000 for the 2025 tax year. That income threshold covers roughly 70% of all US taxpayers — so there's a good chance you qualify.

The key detail that most people miss: you must access Free File through IRS.gov/freefile. If you go directly to a software company's website and navigate their menus, you will almost certainly land on a paid product. The only way to guarantee you're getting the free federally-negotiated version is to start at the IRS portal. From there, you'll see a list of participating partners — currently eight software companies — and each one has its own eligibility criteria beyond the AGI cap (some have age requirements, some restrict certain states, some require simple returns).

IRS Free File: Quick Facts

  • Income limit: AGI ≤ $84,000
  • Access: IRS.gov/freefile — do NOT go directly to a software company's site
  • Federal filing: Always free
  • State filing: May cost extra depending on the partner
  • Partners: 8 software companies as of 2025
  • Best for: W-2 income, standard or itemized deductions, common credits (Earned Income Credit, Child Tax Credit)

Free File guided software works like a standard interview — it asks questions, you answer, and it builds your return behind the scenes. If your return is straightforward, this is genuinely a great option. If you have self-employment income, rental income, or complex investments, you may find yourself hitting the limits of what the free version supports and being pushed toward a paid upgrade.

docs.title: IRS Direct File: The IRS's Own Tool
docs.text:
Direct File is different from Free File in an important way: it is built and operated directly by the IRS, not a private company. There are no upsells, no partner agreements, and no data being shared with third parties. The IRS launched Direct File as a pilot and has been expanding it; for 2025, it is available in approximately 25 states.

The tradeoff is scope. Direct File is designed for people with relatively simple tax situations. It handles W-2 income, Social Security benefits, unemployment compensation, HSA deductions, the standard deduction, and a selection of common tax credits. It does not support self-employment income, investment income beyond basic interest, or itemized deductions in their full complexity. If your return fits within those boundaries and you live in a participating state, Direct File is a clean, IRS-native experience with no commercial pressure attached.

Is Your State Participating in Direct File?

Direct File handles only your federal return. To check whether your state is supported and whether a state filing integration is available, visit IRS.gov/directfile. The participating state list expands each year — if your state wasn't included last year, check again.

docs.title: VITA: Free In-Person Help From Certified Volunteers
docs.text:
VITA stands for Volunteer Income Tax Assistance, and it is one of the IRS's most underutilized programs. VITA sites are staffed by IRS-certified volunteers — real people who have passed IRS training and passed competency exams — who prepare your return in person, for free.

VITA is available to three groups:

  • Taxpayers with income generally at or below $67,000
  • Persons with disabilities
  • Taxpayers with limited English proficiency

Finding a site is straightforward: go to IRS.gov/vita or call 211 (United Way's helpline). Sites are typically open January through April and are hosted in community centers, libraries, schools, and churches. You bring your documents — Social Security cards, W-2s, 1099s, prior year return, photo ID — and a volunteer prepares the return while you watch. You review it, sign it, and it gets e-filed on the spot.

What to Bring to a VITA Site

  • Photo ID (and spouse's if filing jointly)
  • Social Security cards or ITIN letters for all household members
  • All W-2s, 1099s, and other income documents
  • Bank account and routing number for direct deposit
  • Prior year's tax return (if available — helps verify AGI)
  • Records of deductible expenses (childcare costs, student loan interest, HSA contributions)

The quality at VITA sites is consistently good. Volunteers are not guessing — they follow IRS training and use IRS-approved software. For many working families, VITA is the single best free filing option available.

docs.title: AARP Tax-Aide: Free for Everyone
docs.text:
AARP Tax-Aide is similar to VITA in that it offers free, in-person tax preparation by trained volunteers — but it has no income limit and no age requirement, despite the name. AARP Foundation operates it, but you do not need to be an AARP member or be over 50 to use it. Anyone can walk in.

Tax-Aide operates more than 3,500 locations nationwide and is especially well-suited for retirees navigating pension income, Social Security taxation, and Required Minimum Distributions — areas where volunteers receive specific training. You can find a location at aarpfoundation.org/taxaide.

The practical difference between VITA and Tax-Aide comes down to location availability in your area. In some communities, you'll find one but not the other. Both are excellent; use whichever has a convenient location for you.

docs.title: Free Tax Software: A Side-by-Side Comparison
docs.text:
If you want to file yourself from your computer or phone, you have several genuinely free options beyond the IRS programs. Here's an honest comparison:

Software Federal State Best For IRS Free File (via partner) Free May cost extra AGI ≤ $84,000; interview-based guided filing IRS Direct File Free Free (in participating states) Simple W-2 / SS / unemployment returns; ~25 states Cash App Taxes Free Free Most return types; truly no upsell FreeTaxUSA Free $14.99 Broad coverage including Sch C, Sch D, rentals H&R Block Free Online Free Free (simple) W-2, standard deduction, basic credits TaxAct Free Free Varies Basic returns; limited credit support TurboTax Free Edition Free* Varies Very simple returns only — see warning below

Cash App Taxes (formerly Credit Karma Tax) deserves a special mention because it is genuinely free for both federal and state — no income limits, no credit card required, no upsells. It supports a surprisingly wide range of forms including Schedule C (self-employment), Schedule D (capital gains), and rental income. The interface is less polished than TurboTax, but the price is unbeatable.

FreeTaxUSA charges $14.99 for state filing but nothing for federal, and it handles more complex situations than most free options — including self-employment, investment income, and rental properties. For a taxpayer who needs Schedule C and wants to avoid a $100+ software bill, FreeTaxUSA is often the right call.

docs.title: The TurboTax Free Edition Warning
docs.text:
TurboTax deserves its own section because it is aggressively marketed and widely misunderstood. There are two different free TurboTax products, and the distinction matters enormously:

TurboTax Free Edition (what you find at turbotax.com) is a commercial product that only handles the simplest possible returns: W-2 income, standard deduction, and a narrow set of credits. If you have student loan interest, HSA contributions, 1099-NEC income of any kind, or almost any deduction beyond the standard, TurboTax will tell you mid-return that you need to upgrade — and that upgrade typically costs $89–$169.

TurboTax Free File is the version accessed via IRS.gov/freefile. It is part of the federal Free File agreement and covers a meaningfully broader range of situations for taxpayers under the $84,000 AGI threshold.

TurboTax Free Edition Is Not the Same as Free File

If you go directly to turbotax.com and select "Free Edition," you are NOT on the IRS Free File program. This is the commercial product that upsells most users. To access the actual free version under the IRS agreement, you must start at IRS.gov/freefile and follow the link from there. The IRS.gov/freefile portal will route you to the correct product.

The practical advice: if you are looking for a completely free TurboTax experience, start at IRS.gov/freefile. If you're willing to pay a modest fee for a polished interface, TurboTax is a capable product — you're just paying for the experience, not something you couldn't otherwise do yourself.

docs.title: Diagram: Free Filing Option Selector
docs.text:
Free Filing Option Selector — MicroSim Specification

Type: microsim

Bloom Level: Application Bloom Verb: Select

Learning Objective: Given a set of personal tax situation inputs (income, state, return complexity, preference for in-person vs. self-filing), the learner can identify which free filing option is the best match for their situation.

Layout: Two-panel layout. Left panel: input controls (sliders, dropdowns, checkboxes). Right panel: results panel showing ranked recommendations with color-coded match strength.

Inputs: - AGI slider: $0 – $150,000 (step $1,000); label shows current value - Filing complexity dropdown: "Simple (W-2 only)" / "Moderate (some 1099s, HSA, student loans)" / "Complex (self-employment, investments, rental)" - State dropdown: US state selector (used to check Direct File availability) - Preference radio buttons: "I prefer in-person help" / "I prefer online/self-service" - Disability or limited English checkbox

Outputs (right panel): A ranked list of up to 4 recommended options. Each option card shows: - Option name (e.g., "IRS Direct File", "VITA", "Cash App Taxes") - Match badge: "Best Match" (green), "Good Option" (blue), "May Not Qualify" (gray) - One-sentence explanation of why it was recommended or excluded - Link label (e.g., "IRS.gov/directfile") — not a live hyperlink, just displayed as text

Logic: - If AGI > $84,000: mark IRS Free File as "May Not Qualify"; remove TurboTax Free File; promote Cash App Taxes and FreeTaxUSA - If complexity = "Complex": demote Direct File ("Not supported — too complex"); promote Cash App Taxes and FreeTaxUSA - If state not in Direct File list: mark Direct File "Not available in your state" - If preference = in-person OR disability/limited English checked: promote VITA and AARP Tax-Aide to top - If AGI ≤ $67,000 OR disability/limited English: show VITA as "Best Match" - AARP Tax-Aide always shows as at least "Good Option" (no income or age requirement)

Colors: - Background: white (#FFFFFF) - Left panel header: IRS blue (#003087) - Best Match badge: #2E7D32 (dark green) - Good Option badge: #1565C0 (dark blue) - May Not Qualify badge: #616161 (gray) - Warning text: #B71C1C (dark red)

Typography: System sans-serif; inputs labeled clearly; results panel uses card-style layout with subtle drop shadows.

Implementation: p5.js or HTML/CSS/JS with vanilla JavaScript

docs.title: When to Hire a CPA or Enrolled Agent
docs.text:
Free filing is the right move for most simple situations. But "simple" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. There are specific circumstances where paying for professional help is not just convenient — it is genuinely worth hundreds or thousands of dollars in tax savings, penalty avoidance, or protection if things go sideways.

Consider hiring a licensed tax professional if any of the following apply to your situation:

  • You own a business — especially an S-Corp or multi-member LLC. Payroll, self-employment tax, basis tracking, and the Qualified Business Income deduction have enough complexity that DIY mistakes are common and expensive.
  • You have rental properties — depreciation schedules, passive activity loss rules, cost segregation, and eventual gain calculations at sale require precision.
  • A major life event occurred — divorce (dividing retirement accounts requires a QDRO; alimony rules changed in 2019), inheritance (basis step-up rules, estate tax thresholds), or exercising stock options (ISOs vs. NSOs have very different tax treatments).
  • You're significantly off on estimated taxes — if you're self-employed or have investment income and your quarterly payments were off by more than a few hundred dollars, a professional can help you avoid underpayment penalties and plan ahead.
  • You file in multiple states — apportionment rules, reciprocity agreements, and sourcing rules for income are genuinely complex and vary by state.
  • You received an IRS notice or are under audit — this is not a DIY situation. A licensed professional can represent you; software cannot.
  • You have complex investments — cryptocurrency, restricted stock units, options, wash sales, or foreign accounts (FBAR/FATCA requirements carry serious penalties for non-compliance).

The cost of a CPA or Enrolled Agent for a moderately complex return typically runs $300–$800. For an S-Corp with a personal return, expect $800–$2,000+. That sounds like a lot until you realize that a single missed deduction, an incorrect basis calculation, or a missed S-Corp election can cost far more.

docs.title: Tax Professional Types: Who Does What
docs.text:
Not everyone who prepares taxes has the same credentials or authority. Here's what the different titles mean — and why it matters:

Title Credential Who Issues It Can Represent Before IRS? Best For CPA Certified Public Accountant State board of accountancy Yes (with Form 2848) Broad tax and accounting expertise; business owners EA Enrolled Agent IRS Yes, unlimited Specialized in taxes; audits, back taxes, IRS disputes Tax Attorney JD + Tax LL.M. (often) State bar Yes Legal tax matters, tax court, criminal tax issues PTIN Holder Preparer Tax ID Number only IRS No Basic return preparation only; no formal credential "Ghost Preparer" None — No Avoid completely — they won't sign your return

The single most important thing to know: never use a preparer who refuses to sign your return. This is called a "ghost preparer," and it is a major red flag. The preparer is required by law to sign and include their PTIN on every return they prepare. If they won't sign, they know the return has problems — and you'll be the one who faces penalties if the IRS comes knocking.

CPAs are the most versatile option: they can handle everything from personal returns to complex business structures, audits, and financial planning. Enrolled Agents specialize exclusively in taxes and are often the best choice for IRS disputes, back tax resolution, and taxpayers with straightforward businesses. An EA who focuses on taxes full-time often knows the tax code better than a generalist CPA.

CPA vs. EA: Which Should You Hire?

Suppose you have a Schedule C sole proprietorship that generated $85,000 in net profit. You received an IRS CP2000 notice (proposed changes to your return) and you're not sure if the IRS is right.

Best fit: Enrolled Agent. The EA is IRS-licensed, specializes in exactly this kind of dispute, and can represent you in front of the IRS on both the CP2000 response and the underlying Schedule C issues. A CPA would also be appropriate — the key is to find someone with audit response experience, not just return preparation experience.

docs.title: IRS Power of Attorney: Letting Someone Speak for You
docs.text:
When you hire a CPA or EA to represent you before the IRS — whether for an audit, a payment plan negotiation, or just to respond to a notice — you need to give them formal legal authority to do so. That authority comes through Form 2848: Power of Attorney and Declaration of Representative.

Form 2848 is not complicated, but it is specific. You must:

  1. Identify yourself (name, address, SSN or EIN)
  2. Name your representative (name, address, credential/designation, PTIN or CAF number)
  3. Specify the tax matters covered — for example: "Form 1040, income tax, years 2023 and 2024"
  4. Specify what acts the representative is authorized to perform — receiving notices, making agreements, executing waivers, etc.
  5. Sign and date the form

The IRS then registers the representative in the Centralized Authorization File (CAF) system, and going forward, when the IRS contacts you about the specified matters, a copy will also go to your representative. The representative can call the IRS on your behalf, attend hearings, and enter into installment agreements without you being present.

Form 2848 Is Limited in Scope

A Power of Attorney under Form 2848 only covers the specific tax periods and tax types you list. If you authorize someone for "Form 1040, 2023 and 2024" and then an issue arises for 2022, they cannot act on that year without a new Form 2848. Be specific when filling it out, and update it if your situation changes.

You can submit Form 2848 by mail, fax, or — increasingly — through the IRS's online Tax Pro Account system. Once submitted and processed (usually a few business days for online submission), your representative can immediately begin working with the IRS on your behalf.

docs.title: Your IRS Online Account: A Tool You Should Be Using
docs.text:
One of the most underused tools in personal tax management is the IRS Online Account, available at IRS.gov/account. Once you create an account (identity verification is handled through ID.me), you have access to a comprehensive dashboard of your tax records.

Here is what you can see and do from your IRS Online Account:

  • View tax records and transcripts — account transcripts, return transcripts, wage and income transcripts, and record of account transcripts going back several years. These are the exact records the IRS has on file for you.
  • Check your prior year AGI — if you need last year's AGI to e-file this year's return and you can't find your copy, the IRS Online Account has it.
  • View payment history — see every estimated tax payment you've made, including dates and amounts.
  • Check your balance due — if you owe, you can see the current amount including penalties and interest.
  • Set up a payment plan — you can apply for an installment agreement online if you owe and can't pay in full right now.
  • View notices — some IRS notices now appear in your online account before the paper copy arrives.
  • Manage identity protection — you can request an IP PIN (Identity Protection PIN) to prevent someone from filing a fraudulent return in your name.

Pull Your Wage and Income Transcript Before You File

The Wage and Income Transcript shows every W-2, 1099, and information return that employers, banks, and brokers have reported to the IRS under your SSN. Pull this transcript before you file to make sure your return matches the IRS's records. If a 1099-B from your broker doesn't match what you report, the IRS's automated matching system will catch it and send you a notice. Catching discrepancies before you file is far easier than explaining them afterward.

The identity verification step through ID.me requires uploading a photo ID and taking a selfie. It takes about 10–15 minutes the first time. It is worth doing now, before you are in a stressful situation (audit, balance due, identity theft issue) and need to access your records urgently. Create your account today at IRS.gov/account.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The IRS Free File Program offers free federal filing to anyone with AGI ≤ $84,000 — but you must access it through IRS.gov/freefile, not directly through a software company's website.
  • IRS Direct File is the IRS's own free tool, available in ~25 states, with no commercial upsells — but it only handles simple returns (W-2, SS, unemployment, standard deduction).
  • VITA (IRS.gov/vita) provides free in-person filing by certified volunteers for taxpayers with income up to ~$67,000, people with disabilities, and limited English speakers.
  • AARP Tax-Aide (aarpfoundation.org/taxaide) offers free filing for anyone — no age requirement, no income cap — at 3,500+ locations nationwide.
  • Among self-service free software, Cash App Taxes is the most broadly free (federal + state, no income limits), and FreeTaxUSA is excellent for complex returns at free federal / $14.99 state.
  • TurboTax Free Edition at turbotax.com is not the same as IRS Free File — it upsells aggressively and covers only the simplest returns. Use IRS.gov/freefile to access the actual free version.
  • Hire a CPA or EA when you have business income, rental properties, major life events, multi-state filing, IRS notices, or complex investments. The cost is worth it.
  • Enrolled Agents specialize exclusively in taxes and are often the best choice for IRS disputes. CPAs offer broader expertise. Never use a preparer who won't sign your return.
  • Form 2848 (IRS Power of Attorney) gives a licensed professional formal authority to represent you before the IRS — for specific tax years and issues you list on the form.
  • IRS Online Account (IRS.gov/account) gives you access to your transcripts, prior year AGI, payment history, balance due, and payment plan options. Create your account now — before you need it urgently.

docs.title: What To Do Right Now
docs.text:
1. Determine your AGI from last year's return (Line 11 of Form 1040). If it is at or below $84,000, you qualify for IRS Free File.
  2. Check your state at IRS.gov/directfile to see if Direct File is available to you. If it is and your return is simple, this is your easiest path.
  3. Look up the nearest VITA or AARP Tax-Aide site if you prefer in-person help or feel uncertain about filing on your own. Go to IRS.gov/vita or aarpfoundation.org/taxaide.
  4. Create your IRS Online Account at IRS.gov/account right now. Pull your Wage and Income Transcript and verify it matches the documents you have on hand.
  5. Decide on your filing method: Free File partner, Direct File, VITA, AARP Tax-Aide, or free self-service software. Use the comparison table and MicroSim in this chapter to match your situation to the right option.
  6. Assess whether you need a professional. Review the "When to Hire a CPA" list in this chapter. If two or more items apply to your situation, get a consultation — most CPAs and EAs offer a free initial call.
  7. If you hire a professional, prepare Form 2848 in advance so they can represent you before the IRS if needed. Have your SSN, their PTIN/CAF number, and the specific tax years and forms ready.
  8. File by April 15 — or file Form 4868 for an automatic six-month extension. Remember, an extension to file is not an extension to pay. If you owe, pay your estimate by April 15 to avoid penalties and interest.

docs.title: Congratulations
docs.text:
You have completed the Tax Return Guide 2025.

You started this course as someone who wanted to understand their taxes without a CPA holding their hand — and you made it all the way through twelve chapters covering income, deductions, credits, self-employment, investments, retirement accounts, advanced strategies, and now the tools to actually get it done for free.

That is not a small thing. Most people spend decades handing their taxes to someone else because the system feels opaque and intimidating. You have now looked at that system directly. You understand how income is calculated and taxed, how deductions work, how credits reduce your bill dollar for dollar, what triggers an audit, and how to handle it if one comes. You know what the IRS has on file for you and how to look at it yourself.

Go file your return. Use the free options if you qualify. Hire a professional if your situation calls for it — and now you know the difference. Claim every deduction and credit you're entitled to, because leaving money on the table is not virtue — it is just leaving money on the table.

You've got this. File with confidence.

docs.title: 1. Target Audience (−1 pt)
docs.text: The audience is well-described but does not use a formal education-level classification. The skill framework expects a category such as "adult continuing education" or "general public / all levels." This is cosmetic but useful for learning graph calibration.

docs.title: 2. Learning Outcomes Header (−1 pt)
docs.text: The Bloom's Taxonomy section is titled "Learning Objectives by Bloom's Taxonomy" but is missing the standard lead-in statement: "After this course, students will be able to…" This phrase helps the learning graph generator anchor all concepts to learner outcomes.

docs.title: 3. Remember Level (−2 pts)
docs.text:
Only 3 outcomes present. Missing outcomes that would unlock additional concept nodes:

  • Recall IRS record retention requirements (3 years, 6 years, 7 years rules)
  • Recognize the difference between a W-2 and a 1099
  • List the types of income that are NOT taxable (gifts, inheritances, some benefits)

docs.title: 4. Understand Level (−2 pts)
docs.text:
Only 3 outcomes. Missing outcomes:

  • Explain the difference between a tax refund and actually owing less tax
  • Describe what happens when you file late or miss a payment
  • Explain what adjusted gross income (AGI) is and why it matters

docs.title: 5. Analyze Level (−2 pts)
docs.text:
One outcome uses the verb "Evaluate" (a higher Bloom's level) instead of an Analyze-level verb. This creates a taxonomy overlap. Also needs 4+ outcomes:

  • Needs fix: "Evaluate which tax-advantaged accounts best match your situation" → reword to "Compare tax-advantaged account options based on income and tax situation"
  • Missing: "Distinguish between legitimate business deductions and personal expenses"
  • Missing: "Break down the components of a tax bill (income tax vs SE tax vs FICA)"

docs.title: 6. Evaluate Level (−1 pt)
docs.text:
Strong but could add:

  • "Evaluate whether a given advanced tax strategy (Augusta Rule, backdoor Roth) applies to your financial situation"

docs.title: 7. Create Level (−2 pts)
docs.text:
3 outcomes are present but no capstone project is described. Suggested capstone:

  • Missing: "Produce a complete personal tax blueprint: a mock completed 1040, annotated W-2/1099, a quarterly estimated tax schedule, and a business deduction log"

docs.title: Priority 1 — Add capstone to Create level
docs.text:
A capstone project description significantly improves learning graph richness. Suggested addition:

Create a complete personal tax blueprint: a mock completed 1040, an annotated W-2/1099 pair, a quarterly estimated tax schedule, and a business deduction log for one year of side hustle income.

docs.title: Priority 2 — Fix the Analyze/Evaluate taxonomy slip
docs.text: Move "Evaluate which tax-advantaged accounts best match your situation" from Analyze to Evaluate, and replace it with a true analysis outcome using verbs like: distinguish, differentiate, compare, break down, categorize, examine.

docs.title: Priority 3 — Add 2 more outcomes to Remember and Understand
docs.text: These unlock more foundational concept nodes in the learning graph. Use verbs: recall, recognize, list, name, define (Remember) and explain, describe, summarize, classify (Understand).

docs.title: Priority 4 — Add formal audience level classification
docs.text: Add one line: **Educational level:** Adult continuing education / general public

docs.title: 1040 Tax Flow Simulator
docs.text:
Trace your money through every key line of Form 1040 — from gross income down to your final refund or balance due — in one interactive visual flow.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Form 1040 can feel like a black box: income goes in, a number comes out, and it's not always clear why. This simulator makes the path transparent. Enter your wages, filing status, withholding, and credits, and watch how each line of the return feeds into the next — income → adjustments → AGI → deductions → taxable income → tax → credits → payments → refund/owed.

The flow visualization is especially useful for understanding why your refund went up or down compared to last year, or for modeling the impact of a single change like adding an IRA contribution or claiming a new dependent.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter wages (W-2 Box 1) and filing status
  2. Enter federal tax withheld (W-2 Box 2) — this is the amount already prepaid
  3. Add other income — 1099s, side gig income, etc.
  4. Enter child tax credit eligible children if applicable
  5. Watch the 1040 flow update in real time — each node shows the dollar amount at that point in the return

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Gross Income → minus Adjustments = AGI
  • AGI → minus Standard or Itemized Deductions = Taxable Income
  • Taxable Income × bracket rates = Income Tax
  • Income Tax − Non-Refundable Credits = Net Tax
  • Net Tax − Withholding − Refundable Credits = Refund (negative) or Amount Owed

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 6: Tax Forms Deep Dive

docs.title: Audit Risk Assessment Tool
docs.text:
Estimate your relative IRS audit risk based on key characteristics of your tax return — and see which factors raise or lower your profile.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The IRS audits fewer than 1% of individual returns each year, but certain return characteristics are statistically associated with higher audit rates. High income, large Schedule C losses, unusually high deductions relative to income, unreported income, home office deductions, and round numbers are among the factors that can increase scrutiny.

This tool asks about your return's key characteristics and outputs a relative risk meter — not a prediction of whether you'll be audited, but a gauge of how your return compares to IRS audit selection patterns. It also explains what each risk factor means and what documentation you should keep on hand.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Answer each question about your return: income level, types of income and deductions, business losses, home office, vehicle deductions, foreign accounts
  2. View your risk meter — a visual gauge shows your relative risk level (Low / Moderate / Elevated / High)
  3. Review the factor breakdown — each answered question shows whether it adds or reduces risk and why
  4. Read the documentation tips — the tool suggests what records to retain for each flagged item

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• DIF score: IRS uses a Discriminant Information Function to select returns for review
  • High-income filers (over $200k) face higher audit rates
  • Schedule C with losses: more scrutiny, especially with large home office or vehicle deductions
  • Underreported income: IRS matches W-2s, 1099s, and K-1s — mismatches trigger automated notices
  • Substantiation: all deductions need contemporaneous records, not just end-of-year estimates

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 11: IRS Audits

docs.title: Capital Gains Rate Optimizer
docs.text:
Visualize how your long-term capital gains and qualified dividends are taxed across 2025 brackets — and find the income level where gains stay tax-free.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Long-term capital gains enjoy preferential tax rates — 0%, 15%, or 20% — that are completely separate from ordinary income brackets. The rate you pay depends on your total taxable income, not just the gain itself. This can create a planning opportunity: if your ordinary income is low enough, you may be able to realize gains at 0%.

This optimizer lets you enter your ordinary income and investment gains side-by-side and shows exactly which rate applies to each dollar of gain, where the thresholds fall, and how much additional gain you could realize before crossing into the next bracket.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your filing status and ordinary taxable income (wages, SE income, etc. after deductions)
  2. Enter your long-term capital gains and/or qualified dividend amount
  3. View the bracket visualization — a stacked bar or chart shows ordinary income vs. gains and which LTCG rate bucket each dollar falls into
  4. Adjust the gains slider to find the maximum gain you can take at 0% this year

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 LTCG 0% threshold: $48,350 (Single) / $96,700 (MFJ)
  • 2025 LTCG 15% threshold: up to $533,400 (Single) / $600,050 (MFJ)
  • 20% rate applies above those thresholds
  • LTCG "stacks on top of" ordinary income when determining the rate
  • Qualified dividends are taxed at the same preferential rates as LTCG

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 7: Investments & Capital Gains

docs.title: Free Filing Option Selector
docs.text:
Answer a few questions about your income, situation, and comfort level to find the best free federal tax filing option for you.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Most Americans can file their federal taxes for free — but navigating the options is confusing. IRS Free File covers filers under $84,000 AGI through brand-name software partners. IRS Direct File (launched 2024) lets eligible filers prepare and submit directly on the IRS website at no cost. VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance) offers free in-person help for those earning under $67,000. And some commercial software offers genuinely free tiers for simple returns.

This selector asks about your income, age, military status, state, and return complexity to match you with the best option — or combination of options — for your situation.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your approximate AGI for 2025
  2. Answer situational questions — military, age 60+, limited English, disabilities, self-employed, rental income, etc.
  3. Select your state — Direct File availability and state partnership vary
  4. View your matched options — ranked by best fit, with links and eligibility notes for each program

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• IRS Free File: available for AGI under $84,000; partner software may upsell state returns
  • IRS Direct File: free, no third-party software; available in ~25 states for simpler returns
  • VITA/TCE: free in-person preparation for income-eligible filers and seniors
  • AARP Tax-Aide: TCE variant for taxpayers 50+, no income limit
  • MilTax: free filing for active duty military and veterans regardless of income

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 12: Free Filing Resources

docs.title: Full Federal Tax Return Calculator
docs.text:
Walk through a complete multi-step 2025 federal tax return — from gross income through every deduction, credit, and payment — to estimate your refund or amount owed.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This is the most comprehensive simulator in the book — a guided walkthrough of the entire Form 1040 calculation, broken into logical steps. It mirrors the actual return sequence: filing status and personal information, income from all sources, above-the-line adjustments, deduction choice, taxable income, tax computation, credits, and finally the payments and refund/balance-due calculation.

Because it covers all the moving parts together, it's the best tool for understanding how different elements interact. Add a side gig and watch SE tax appear. Make an IRA contribution and see AGI drop. Add children and watch the Child Tax Credit cut your liability. This calculator is for learning how everything fits together, not for filing an actual return.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
The calculator is organized as a multi-step wizard — work through each step in order:

  1. Filing Status & Personal Info — status, age, dependency status
  2. Income — W-2 wages, self-employment, interest, dividends, capital gains, rental income, other
  3. Adjustments to Income — IRA, HSA, SE health insurance, SE tax deduction, student loan interest
  4. Deduction — standard vs. itemized comparison, with full itemized entry form
  5. Tax Computation — bracket-by-bracket calculation on ordinary income; separate LTCG rate computation
  6. Credits — Child Tax Credit, Child & Dependent Care, Education Credits, EITC (if eligible)
  7. Other Taxes — SE tax, AMT (simplified), net investment income tax
  8. Payments — withholding from W-2s, estimated tax payments, refundable credits
  9. Summary — total refund or amount owed, with full line-by-line breakdown

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
This calculator integrates all major tax concepts covered in the book:

  • Filing status determines brackets, standard deduction, and credit phase-outs
  • AGI is the gateway number — it determines eligibility for many deductions and credits
  • Self-employment income triggers SE tax (15.3%) and allows the SE deduction (50% of SE tax)
  • The standard deduction benefits most filers; itemizing requires Schedule A documentation
  • Credits reduce tax dollar-for-dollar; refundable credits can produce a refund beyond zero liability
  • LTCG and qualified dividends are taxed at preferential 0%/15%/20% rates

For Educational Purposes

This calculator is a learning tool. It simplifies some phase-outs and does not cover every tax situation. For actual filing, use IRS-approved software or consult a tax professional.

docs.title: Learning Graph Viewer
docs.text:
Explore the interactive concept map for the Tax Return Guide (2025) — 200 concepts across 13 categories, showing how each tax topic builds on others.

Open Learning Graph Viewer{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
• Search: Type in the search box to find specific concepts (e.g., "W-2", "deduction", "capital gains")
  • Filter by Category: Use the checkboxes in the sidebar to show/hide topic groups
  • Navigate: Drag to pan, scroll to zoom, click a node to highlight its connections
  • Statistics: The sidebar shows live counts of visible nodes and edges

docs.title: Graph Structure
docs.text:
Arrows point from a concept to its prerequisites — follow the arrows backward to see what you need to learn first.

  • Box-shaped nodes — foundational concepts (no prerequisites)
  • Rounded nodes — concepts that build on others

docs.title: 13 Tax Topic Categories
docs.text: Category Color Concepts Tax Foundations PaleTurquoise 12 Filing Status & Returns LavenderBlush 13 Deductions & Credits LightYellow 22 Retirement Accounts MintCream 16 Self-Employment Taxes Gainsboro 16 Year-Round Tax Planning Honeydew 14 Tax Forms Guide MistyRose 17 Investment Taxation Plum 20 Real Estate & Rental SteelBlue 17 Advanced Tax Strategies LightCoral 18 State Taxes Thistle 12 IRS Audits & Compliance Lavender 13 Filing Resources & Tools PeachPuff 10

docs.title: HSA vs. FSA Cost-Benefit Analyzer
docs.text:
Compare the tax advantages, flexibility, and trade-offs of a Health Savings Account versus a Flexible Spending Account for your 2025 healthcare spending.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Both HSAs and FSAs let you pay for qualified medical expenses with pre-tax dollars — but they work very differently. An HSA is triple tax-advantaged (contributions deductible, growth tax-free, qualified withdrawals tax-free) and funds roll over indefinitely. An FSA is use-it-or-lose-it, but is available with any employer health plan, not just high-deductible plans.

This analyzer lets you enter your expected annual medical spending, income and tax bracket, and health plan type to see the after-tax cost of your healthcare in each scenario — and which account type saves you more.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Select your health plan type — only HDHP-enrolled individuals qualify for an HSA
  2. Enter your expected annual medical expenses — premiums paid out-of-pocket, deductibles, copays, prescriptions
  3. Enter your marginal tax bracket — federal + state combined
  4. Set contribution amounts within the 2025 limits
  5. Compare the results — after-tax healthcare cost, tax savings, and year-end balance for each account type

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 HSA limits: $4,300 (self-only HDHP) / $8,550 (family HDHP)
  • 2025 FSA limit: $3,300 (employer plan), $640 rollover allowed
  • HSA triple tax advantage: deductible contribution + tax-free growth + tax-free withdrawal
  • FSA: use-it-or-lose-it (up to $640 rollover); available without HDHP requirement
  • At age 65, HSA funds can be withdrawn for any purpose (taxed as ordinary income, like a Traditional IRA)

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 3: Tax Reduction Strategies

docs.title: IRA Comparison Calculator
docs.text:
Compare Traditional and Roth IRA contributions side-by-side across your current and expected retirement tax brackets to find which account builds more after-tax wealth.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Traditional vs. Roth decision comes down to one question: will you pay a lower tax rate now or in retirement? A Traditional IRA gives you a deduction today but you pay ordinary income tax on withdrawals. A Roth IRA uses after-tax dollars now, but all qualified withdrawals — including decades of growth — come out tax-free.

This calculator projects the after-tax value of the same contribution amount in both account types over your chosen investment horizon, using your current and expected retirement tax brackets. It shows the crossover point: the bracket at which Roth starts to win.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your current tax bracket — the marginal rate you pay on the last dollar of income today
  2. Enter your expected retirement bracket — what you expect to pay on withdrawals in retirement
  3. Set contribution amount — up to the 2025 limit of \(7,000 (\)8,000 if age 50+)
  4. Set years to retirement and expected annual return
  5. Read the summary table — it shows pre-tax and after-tax values for both account types

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 IRA contribution limit: $7,000 / $8,000 (age 50+)
  • Traditional IRA: deductible contributions, taxable withdrawals
  • Roth IRA: after-tax contributions, tax-free qualified withdrawals
  • If current bracket < retirement bracket → Roth usually wins
  • Income limits apply for Roth eligibility and Traditional deductibility

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 3: Tax Reduction Strategies

docs.title: Marginal vs. Effective Tax Rate
docs.text:
See exactly how the 2025 federal tax brackets work — your income fills each bracket like water in stacked containers, not a flat rate applied to everything.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
One of the most persistent myths in personal finance is that crossing into a higher tax bracket means you pay that higher rate on all your income. You don't. The US uses a progressive system: only the income within each bracket is taxed at that bracket's rate. The marginal rate is what you pay on the next dollar; the effective rate is what you actually pay on average across all your income.

This visualizer makes it viscerally clear. Enter your taxable income and filing status, and watch a stacked bar fill up bracket by bracket — showing exactly how much tax is owed at each rate and what your blended effective rate is.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Select your filing status — the bracket thresholds differ for each
  2. Enter your taxable income — after all deductions
  3. View the bracket chart — each segment shows the income range, tax rate, and dollar amount of tax in that bracket
  4. Read the summary — marginal rate (top bracket you hit), effective rate (total tax ÷ taxable income), and total tax owed

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 brackets (Single): 10% / 12% / 22% / 24% / 32% / 35% / 37%
  • Marginal rate = the rate on your next dollar of income
  • Effective rate = total tax ÷ total taxable income (always lower than marginal rate)
  • The standard deduction reduces income before it enters the brackets
  • Long-term capital gains use separate (lower) brackets and "stack on top of" ordinary income

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 1: Understanding Your Taxes

docs.title: Mileage vs. Actual Vehicle Expense Calculator
docs.text:
Compare the IRS standard mileage rate method against tracking actual vehicle expenses for your self-employed or business vehicle deduction in 2025.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
If you use your vehicle for business, you can deduct the cost two ways: the standard mileage rate (70¢/mile for 2025) or actual expenses (gas, insurance, repairs, depreciation) prorated by business-use percentage. The standard mileage method is simpler; the actual expense method sometimes yields a larger deduction, especially for expensive or gas-heavy vehicles.

There are important restrictions: you must choose the standard mileage method in the first year the vehicle is placed in service if you want to use it in later years. And if you use actual expenses with depreciation, MACRS depreciation rules and luxury auto limits apply.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your total annual business miles and total miles driven
  2. Enter your standard mileage rate deduction — automatically calculated at 70¢/mile
  3. For actual expenses: enter gas, insurance, registration, repairs/maintenance, and lease payments or depreciation basis
  4. Compare the two columns — the calculator shows total deduction under each method and the business-use percentage applied to actual expenses
  5. Note the first-year election rule — if this is a new vehicle, your choice this year locks in your method going forward

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 standard mileage rate: 70 cents per mile (business)
  • Actual expense method: all vehicle costs × (business miles ÷ total miles)
  • Luxury auto depreciation caps apply to vehicles over ~$12,400 cost basis
  • Section 179 and bonus depreciation (40% in 2025) can accelerate depreciation under actual method
  • Mileage log is required for both methods — date, destination, business purpose, miles

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & SE Taxes

docs.title: Passive Loss Decision Tree
docs.text:
Work through the IRS passive activity loss rules step-by-step to determine how much of your rental property loss you can deduct against ordinary income this year.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Rental property losses are "passive" under IRS rules — meaning you generally can't deduct them against W-2 wages or other ordinary income. But there are two major exceptions: the $25,000 passive loss allowance for active participants (phases out between \(100k–\)150k MAGI), and the real estate professional exception for those who spend more than 750 hours per year in real estate activities.

This decision tree guides you through the key questions in order — do you actively participate? What is your MAGI? Do you qualify as a real estate professional? — and tells you exactly how much loss you can deduct now versus how much carries forward to future years.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your rental loss for the year (from Schedule E)
  2. Enter your MAGI — modified adjusted gross income (before the rental loss)
  3. Answer the participation questions — do you actively manage the property? How many hours did you spend on real estate activities?
  4. Read the decision output — it shows deductible loss this year, carried-forward loss, and the rule that applies

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Passive activity loss (PAL) rules: IRC §469
  • $25,000 rental loss allowance: phases out $1 for every $2 of MAGI over $100,000
  • Real estate professional: 750+ hours AND more than half of all work time in real estate
  • Suspended losses carry forward indefinitely and are released when the property is sold
  • Active participation (less stringent than RE professional) is needed for the $25k allowance

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 8: Real Estate & Rental Taxes

docs.title: Quarterly Estimated Tax Calculator
docs.text:
Calculate your 2025 quarterly estimated tax payments as a self-employed filer — including income tax and self-employment tax — and see the four due dates.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
When you work for yourself, no employer withholds taxes from your paycheck. The IRS instead requires you to prepay taxes in four installments throughout the year. Getting this wrong in either direction costs you: underpay and you owe a penalty at filing; overpay and you've given the government an interest-free loan.

This calculator takes your expected gross business income, deductible expenses, filing status, and prior-year tax (for the safe-harbor calculation) and produces the recommended payment for each quarter. It also factors in the self-employed health insurance deduction, which lowers both your income tax and SE tax base.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter annual gross business income — your expected total revenue for 2025
  2. Enter annual business expenses — deductible costs to arrive at net profit
  3. Select filing status and check if you have additional W-2 income
  4. Enter prior-year total tax — used for the 100%/110% safe-harbor option
  5. Enter self-employed health insurance premiums if applicable
  6. Review quarterly amounts — the calculator shows the recommended payment for each of the four due dates

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• SE tax: 15.3% (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare) on 92.35% of net SE income
  • 2025 due dates: April 15, June 16, September 15, January 15, 2026
  • Safe harbor: pay 100% of prior-year tax (110% if prior AGI > $150k) to avoid penalties
  • Self-employed health insurance premiums are deductible above the line

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 4: Side Hustle & SE Taxes

docs.title: Refund Impact Calculator
docs.text:
See in real time how changes to your income, withholding, deductions, and credits shift your expected 2025 federal refund or balance due.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Your tax refund (or bill) is not determined by your income alone — it is the difference between your total tax liability and the taxes you already prepaid through withholding and estimated payments. This calculator makes that relationship interactive: adjust any input and watch the refund meter update immediately.

It's particularly useful for year-end planning. With a few months left in the year, you can model the impact of making an IRA contribution, increasing withholding on your W-4, or claiming a credit you forgot — and see exactly how it affects your bottom line before the tax year closes.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your filing status and wages/income sources
  2. Enter federal tax withheld from your W-2(s) and any estimated tax payments made
  3. Enter refundable credits — EITC, Child Tax Credit (refundable portion), etc.
  4. Adjust above-the-line deductions — IRA, HSA, student loan interest
  5. Choose standard or itemized deduction and enter itemized totals if applicable
  6. Watch the refund/owed display update — green means refund, red means you owe

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Refund = taxes prepaid − taxes owed (when prepaid > owed)
  • Balance due = taxes owed − taxes prepaid (when owed > prepaid)
  • A large refund means you over-withheld — essentially an interest-free loan to the IRS
  • Adjust W-4 withholding to target a near-zero result at filing
  • Refundable credits (EITC, ACTC) can produce a refund even if you owe no tax

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return

docs.title: Remote Worker State Tax Explainer
docs.text:
Understand how multiple states may claim the right to tax your remote work income — and estimate your potential multi-state tax liability.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Remote work has made state taxes significantly more complicated. You might live in one state, work for a company headquartered in another, and occasionally travel to a third for meetings — and all three could potentially tax some of your income. The rules depend on each state's residency and source-income rules, "convenience of the employer" doctrines, and whether a reciprocity agreement exists between states.

This explainer walks through the key scenarios: resident vs. nonresident taxation, the "convenience rule" used by New York and a handful of other states, how reciprocity agreements work, and how to claim a credit for taxes paid to another state (to prevent true double taxation).

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your state of residence — where you live and are domiciled
  2. Enter your employer's state — where the company's office is located
  3. Enter your income and approximate days worked in each state
  4. See the state breakdown — which states have a claim on your income, approximate tax owed to each, and the credit for taxes paid to nonresident states

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Resident state taxes all income regardless of where earned
  • Nonresident state taxes income sourced to that state (work performed there)
  • Convenience rule: NY, CT, DE, NE, and PA may tax remote work days as if worked in-state
  • Reciprocity agreements: some neighboring states agree to only tax residents in their home state
  • Credit for taxes paid to other states prevents most (but not always all) double taxation

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 10: State Taxes

docs.title: Rental Property Annual Tax Summary
docs.text:
Calculate your rental property's net income, depreciation deduction, passive loss deductibility, and estimated tax impact for the 2025 tax year.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Owning rental property involves a tax calculation that's more complex than most realize. Gross rent is only the starting point — you subtract operating expenses, then depreciation (a non-cash deduction based on 1/27.5 of the building's cost basis), to arrive at net taxable income or a net loss. If you end up with a loss, the passive activity rules determine how much you can deduct now.

This calculator walks through the full Schedule E computation: gross rents, operating expenses (mortgage interest, property taxes, insurance, repairs, management fees), depreciation, and the resulting net income or loss. It then applies the passive loss allowance rules based on your MAGI and participation level.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter gross rental income for the year
  2. Enter all operating expenses — mortgage interest, taxes, insurance, repairs, utilities, management fees, other
  3. Enter the property's cost basis (purchase price minus land value) to compute depreciation
  4. Enter your MAGI and participation type to determine deductibility of any loss
  5. Review the Schedule E summary — it shows each line item, net income/loss, deductible vs. suspended loss

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Depreciation: residential rental property depreciated over 27.5 years (straight-line)
  • Net rental loss = passive loss (subject to PAL rules)
  • $25,000 allowance phases out at MAGI \(100k–\)150k for active participants
  • Mortgage interest on rental is fully deductible (no SALT cap applies to rental property)
  • Repairs are deductible; improvements must be capitalized and depreciated

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 8: Real Estate & Rental Taxes

docs.title: Retirement Plan Optimizer
docs.text:
See how much you can contribute to each tax-advantaged retirement account in 2025 and how those contributions reduce your current-year taxable income.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Maxing out tax-advantaged accounts is the single most powerful tax strategy available to most Americans. But the contribution limits, eligibility rules, and income phase-outs across 401(k), Traditional IRA, Roth IRA, HSA, and SEP-IRA are easy to confuse. This optimizer lays out exactly how much you can contribute to each account type given your income, age, and employment situation — and shows the total tax savings for each dollar contributed.

For self-employed filers it also calculates SEP-IRA and Solo 401(k) limits, which are based on a percentage of net self-employment income.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your income — W-2 wages and/or net self-employment income
  2. Select your age — catch-up contributions apply at 50+ (and special 60–63 limits for 401k)
  3. Indicate employer plan access — 401(k), 403(b), SIMPLE IRA, etc.
  4. Enter HSA eligibility — self-only or family HDHP coverage
  5. Review the contribution table — it shows the max for each account and the combined tax reduction at your bracket

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 5: Year-Round Tax Planning

docs.title: S-Corp vs. Sole Proprietor Tax Savings Calculator
docs.text:
Compare self-employment tax burden between operating as a sole proprietor and electing S-Corp status — and find your breakeven income level.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The biggest tax advantage of an S-Corp for self-employed individuals is the ability to split income into two buckets: a "reasonable salary" (subject to payroll taxes) and owner distributions (not subject to SE tax). A sole proprietor pays 15.3% SE tax on all net profit; an S-Corp owner only pays payroll taxes on the salary portion.

But S-Corps come with real costs: payroll administration, separate tax returns (Form 1120-S), potential state franchise fees, and additional accounting complexity. This calculator shows whether the SE tax savings at your income level outweigh the added costs — and at what income the switch becomes worthwhile.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your net self-employment income — annual profit from your business
  2. Enter an estimated reasonable salary — the IRS requires S-Corp owner-employees to pay themselves a market-rate wage
  3. Enter estimated S-Corp administrative costs — payroll service, tax preparation, state fees
  4. Compare the results — the calculator shows SE tax as a sole proprietor vs. payroll tax as an S-Corp, net savings, and the breakeven income level

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Sole proprietor SE tax: 15.3% on net profit × 0.9235 (up to Social Security wage base)
  • S-Corp: payroll taxes only on the salary; distributions avoid SE/payroll tax
  • 2025 Social Security wage base: $176,100
  • Reasonable salary requirement: IRS can reclassify distributions as wages if salary is too low
  • Typical S-Corp breakeven: \(50,000–\)80,000 net profit depending on state and admin costs

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 9: Advanced Strategies

docs.title: Standard vs. Itemized Deduction Calculator
docs.text:
Enter your deductible expenses to instantly see whether itemizing beats the 2025 standard deduction — and by how much.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Most Americans take the standard deduction because the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act roughly doubled it in 2018. But homeowners with large mortgages or high charitable giving may still benefit from itemizing. This calculator makes the comparison concrete: enter your mortgage interest, property taxes, state income taxes, charitable contributions, and qualifying medical expenses, and it shows your itemized total side-by-side with the standard deduction for your filing status.

The calculator also applies the SALT (state and local tax) cap of $10,000, the AGI floor for medical deductions (7.5% of AGI in 2025), and flags which method saves more.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Select your filing status to see the applicable standard deduction
  2. Enter your AGI — needed to calculate the medical expense floor
  3. Fill in your potential itemized deductions — mortgage interest, property tax, state income tax, charitable gifts, and out-of-pocket medical expenses
  4. Read the verdict — the calculator shows your total itemized amount vs. the standard deduction and recommends the larger one

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• 2025 standard deductions: $15,000 (Single), $30,000 (MFJ), $22,500 (HoH)
  • SALT cap: combined state/local income and property taxes capped at $10,000
  • Medical deductions: only the amount exceeding 7.5% of AGI is deductible
  • You can only itemize on Schedule A if you forgo the standard deduction

docs.title: Related Chapter
docs.text: • Chapter 2: Filing Your Return

docs.title: Tax Liability Calculator
docs.text:
Enter your income, deductions, and credits to see an instant 2025 federal tax estimate — including self-employment tax, SE deduction, and above-the-line adjustments.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This calculator covers the most common tax situations for individual filers in 2025. Enter W-2 wages, freelance/self-employment income, and other income sources, then apply above-the-line deductions (HSA, Traditional IRA, student loan interest) to arrive at your AGI. The calculator then compares your itemized deductions against the standard deduction and applies the correct tax brackets.

Self-employment income gets special treatment: the calculator automatically computes the 15.3% SE tax on 92.35% of net SE income, then deducts half of that SE tax above the line — exactly as IRS rules require.

Use this as a sanity-check before filing or when evaluating whether a deduction strategy is worth pursuing.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Select your filing status — Single, MFJ, MFS, or Head of Household
  2. Enter your income — W-2 wages, freelance net income, and any other taxable income
  3. Choose HSA coverage and enter HSA/IRA/student-loan contributions
  4. Itemized deductions — enter mortgage interest, property tax, state income tax, and charitable gifts to see if itemizing beats the standard deduction
  5. Review the right panel — it shows AGI, taxable income, bracket-by-bracket tax, SE tax, and estimated refund or amount owed

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Standard deduction (2025): $15,000 Single / $30,000 MFJ
  • SE tax rate: 15.3% on net income × 0.9235
  • Above-the-line deductions reduce AGI before choosing standard vs. itemized
  • SALT deduction cap: $10,000

docs.title: W-4 Withholding Adjustment Tool
docs.text:
Find the right W-4 entries to hit your target tax outcome — whether that's a specific refund amount, a near-zero balance, or maximum take-home pay.

View Fullscreen{ .md-button .md-button--primary }

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The redesigned W-4 (post-2020) replaced withholding allowances with a more direct system: you enter other income, deductions above the standard amount, and any extra amount to withhold per period. But many people still end up with a surprise bill or a larger refund than they wanted.

This tool works backward from your desired outcome. Tell it your filing status, expected total income from all jobs, any other income sources, and what you want at filing (refund target or near-zero). It calculates the exact dollar amounts to enter on Steps 3, 4a, 4b, and 4c of your W-4 — the instructions you hand to your employer.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text:
1. Enter your filing status and pay frequency (weekly, biweekly, monthly, etc.)
  2. Enter expected annual income from this job and any other jobs or income sources
  3. Enter dependents — the Child Tax Credit amount from Step 3
  4. Set your target outcome — desired refund amount or "as close to zero as possible"
  5. Read the W-4 entries — the tool outputs exactly what to write on Steps 4a, 4b, and 4c

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Step 2: check the box if you have multiple jobs or a working spouse
  • Step 3: enter total Child Tax Credit ($2,000 per qualifying child under 17)
  • Step 4a: other income not subject to withholding (interest, dividends, side income)
  • Step 4b: extra deductions above the standard deduction (if you itemize)
  • Step 4c: additional flat dollar amount to withhold per pay period
  • Update your W-4 whenever you have a major life change (marriage, new job, child, etc.)
